themes.theme: Strategy & Alpha Generation for an autonomous, local-first, stdlib-only crypto treasury agent (treasuryforge)
themes.consensus: Across all 10 agents a tightly-aligned picture emerges. (1) The strongest, most real 2026 edge for THIS agent is MECHANICAL/QUANT, not LLM-discretionary — every agent that touched the strategy question independently lands on rule-expressible signals (funding carry, time-series momentum, stat-arb/pairs, market-making spreads, GBM proposers) and explicitly relegates LLMs to an optional, noisy FEATURE feeding the PROPOSE layer, never a trigger or a safety layer. This is a clear vote on Phase-2 decision #2 (mechanical over LLM-discretionary). (2) The single most accessible, highest-leverage signal is the PERPETUAL FUNDING RATE — cited by 3 independent agents — because Hyperliquid's keyless public /info endpoint (type=fundingHistory) and Binance funding endpoints are no-auth JSON over HTTPS, fetchable with pure stdlib urllib, slow/well-behaved (hourly/8h), and map perfectly onto propose->policy->execute as a sentiment/carry signal. Delta-neutral cash-and-carry (spot-long/perp-short) is repeatedly named the best-fit core strategy because its ~zero net delta makes the existing drawdown circuit-breaker and solvency checks trivially easy to reason about. (3) The architecture is RIGHT and should stay untouched: signals/strategies live strictly in PROPOSE; the hard-rule POLICY engine remains the only enforcement authority; the meta-allocator (Hedge/multiplicative-weights) and regime detectors only SIZE proposals, never bypass policy. (4) The decisive deliverable is NOT a model but a VALIDATION DISCIPLINE: a causal (past-data-only), friction-and-liquidation-aware backtest used as a hard promotion gate (Sim -> Kraken paper -> tiny-cap onchain), reinforced by purged/combinatorial-purged cross-validation + Deflated Sharpe Ratio to defeat overfitting and selection bias. Multiple agents stress that nearly every published edge EVAPORATES once realistic fees, slippage, borrow and liquidations are applied. (5) Honesty about evidence is unanimous: most cited Sharpe/return/accuracy figures come from single studies, vendor marketing, or practitioner blogs (not replicated peer review) and MUST be re-validated on the builder's own out-of-sample crypto data before any real funds.
themes.contradictions: 1) STDLIB-ONLY vs the best tools: The strongest validation tools (hmmlearn, statsmodels MS-AR, ruptures, GBMs LightGBM/CatBoost, mlfinlab, copulas) all require numpy/scipy/pandas and break the stdlib-only constraint. Agents disagree on resolution: confine heavy deps to OFFLINE research/backtest only (regime/CPCV agents), reimplement the method in pure Python (BOCPD, Kalman ~30 lines, Engle-Granger ADF with tabulated constants, triple-barrier, Hedge ~30 lines), or relax the rule behind a strategy interface with a stdlib fallback proposer. The cleanest pure-Python online tools (BOCPD, Kalman hedge ratio, Hedge meta-allocator, raw funding pull) preserve the local-first ideal; the heavy ML belongs offline. 2) Order-book microstructure: one agent presents LOB imbalance as alpha, but the SAME agent then concedes the published edge lives at a 3-second horizon a REST-polling agent cannot capture — so it is demoted to an entry-timing/execution-quality filter, not standalone alpha. Treat as a contradiction the marketing implies (tradable) vs reality (timing filter only). 3) LLM frameworks: TradingAgents reports 23-27% backtest returns but the agent flags those are non-reproducible, on EQUITIES not crypto, and authors label it research-only — hype vs usefulness. 4) Time-series foundation models (TimesFM/Chronos) are marketed as forecasters but benchmarked NEGATIVE R2 and sub-chance directional accuracy on returns — a documented non-fit, useful (if at all) only for auxiliary volatility/funding forecasts. 5) Third-party normalized feeds (FundingPulse, Nansen, CryptoQuant granular) and aggregators contradict the zero-cloud ideal and are partly paywalled/black-box — keep direct-from-exchange stdlib path as the zero-dependency fallback. 6) Reference repos staleness: aoki-h-jp/funding-rate-arbitrage is MIT but last released 2023 and ccxt-heavy — port the logic, do not vendor.

top_techniques.name: Delta-neutral funding carry (spot-long/perp-short) driven by the keyless Hyperliquid /info fundingHistory feed
top_techniques.why: Named by 3 independent agents as the single best-fit Phase-2 strategy: fully mechanical/rule-expressible (no LLM discretion), its ~zero net delta makes the drawdown breaker and solvency checks trivial to reason about, and the data source is free, permissionless, no-auth JSON fetchable with pure stdlib urllib — perfectly local-first. Wire as PROPOSE-only paired-leg intents with a hard delta-band policy invariant.

top_techniques.name: Volatility-scaled time-series momentum (own-asset trend) gated by regime
top_techniques.why: The strongest, most durable academic crypto alpha in the 2026 literature (vol-scaled TSMOM Sharpe ~1.83 OOS vs ~0.84 buy-and-hold), pure arithmetic over a price series with no ML and no data-snooping surface. Time-series momentum is robust while cross-sectional and naive mean-reversion are weak; best run gated ON in trends, with a market-neutral BTC-residual reversion leg only in confirmed ranging regimes, flat when they disagree.

top_techniques.name: Engle-Granger cointegration pairs trading (z-score bands) -> Kalman dynamic hedge ratio -> OU optimal thresholds
top_techniques.why: The strongest natural fit for the propose/dispose design — mechanical, market-neutral, auditable. v1 is stdlib-only (OLS + ADF with tabulated critical constants + z-score); upgrade the spread estimator to an online Kalman filter (~30 lines pure Python, O(1) per tick, tiny persistent state) then add Ornstein-Uhlenbeck optimal entry/exit + half-life time-stop. Market-neutral long/short keeps net exposure low, pairing well with the drawdown breaker.

top_techniques.name: Causal, friction-and-liquidation-aware backtest + Purged/Combinatorial-Purged CV + Deflated Sharpe Ratio as a hard promotion gate
top_techniques.why: The decisive component across the entire corpus: multiple agents independently stress that nearly every reported edge collapses under realistic fees/slippage/borrow/liquidations, and that look-ahead (smoothed vs filtered probabilities, overlapping labels, untracked trial count) massively overstates skill. This is plain math (near-stdlib), directly serves the 'validated before any real funds' mandate, and gates Sim->Kraken-paper->tiny-cap onchain promotion. It is what makes quant strategies trustworthy in a way LLM-discretionary ones cannot be.

top_techniques.name: Hedge / multiplicative-weights meta-allocator over sub-strategies (+ rule-based regime gating with hysteresis)
top_techniques.why: ~30 lines of pure-Python arithmetic, provable O(sqrt(T log N)) no-regret guarantee that the ensemble tracks the best sub-strategy in hindsight, degrades gracefully (a bleeding strategy gets starved of weight), and slots in cleanly as a PROPOSAL-sizer that scales each expert's notional BEFORE the unchanged policy engine. Pair with CRP/Buy-and-Hold OLPS baselines as the must-beat backtest yardsticks. Caveat: crypto correlations collapse to 1 in risk-off, so the drawdown breaker stays the real backstop.

top_techniques.name: Avellaneda-Stoikov inventory-aware market-making (classical closed-form -> infinite-horizon recalibration)
top_techniques.why: Pure math over (mid, inventory, volatility, two constants), implementable in stdlib, intrinsically inventory-aware so it self-limits position growth in natural alignment with the policy engine's notional/solvency caps. Production-grade and auditable (Hummingbot reference impl); the infinite-horizon variant fits a continuously-running treasury agent. RL-tuned (Alpha-AS) and robust quote/no-quote extensions are later simulation-validated upgrades, not v1.

top_techniques.name: CPU-side local sentiment (CryptoBERT/FinBERT) as an OPTIONAL numeric feature, with mandatory adversarial-input sanitization as a DISPOSE hard rule
top_techniques.why: If sentiment is used at all, run it locally/offline (zero-cloud) as one bounded numeric feature feeding PROPOSE — never a trigger or safety layer. Critically, consuming external news opens a demonstrated attack surface (Unicode homoglyph + hidden-HTML headline manipulation costing double-digit returns), so Unicode NFKC normalization + HTML stripping + confidence gating must be a non-bypassable hard rule (stdlib unicodedata + html.parser), mirroring the kill-switch.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Ship funding-carry as the FIRST IntentGenerator: stdlib urllib POST to Hyperliquid /info (type=fundingHistory) -> compute carry vs fees -> emit paired (spot_buy, perp_short) intents with a hard |spot_qty - perp_qty| <= epsilon delta-band policy invariant.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (strategy: mechanical/quant over LLM) and Decision #1 (chain/exchange: favors EVM/DeFi via Hyperliquid's keyless onchain order book, with Kraken paper for the hedge leg)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Highest-leverage + most accessible signal in the corpus, named by 3 agents. Free, no-auth, pure-stdlib data; ~zero net delta makes existing safety rails easy to reason about; maps 1:1 onto propose->policy->execute. Add a fee-aware net-carry check before proposing (headline 10-30% APY is gross/pre-fee). Validate on real historical funding in SimExecutor first.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Build the causal, friction-and-liquidation-aware backtest + CostModel as a hard promotion gate, with purged/CPCV + Deflated Sharpe Ratio thresholds gating Sim -> Kraken paper -> tiny-cap onchain. Track the honest count of all configs tried so DSR's multiple-testing correction is real.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (makes quant strategies testable in a way LLM-discretionary ones are not; directly enables the 'mechanical' choice)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The corpus's most repeated, safety-critical finding: nearly every reported edge evaporates under realistic costs/liquidations, and naive backtests with smoothed states / overlapping labels / untracked trials massively overstate skill. This is the single piece of work that stops the agent acting on backtest mirages and fulfills the 'validated before real funds' mandate. Near-stdlib (plain math).

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement vol-scaled time-series momentum (sign of trailing return / rolling realized-vol, vol-targeted notional) as the second mechanical IntentGenerator, gated by a stdlib regime classifier (ADX/realized-vol/volume) that runs momentum in trends and a BTC-residual market-neutral mean-reversion leg only in confirmed ranges, flat when they conflict.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (mechanical/quant)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Strongest durable academic crypto alpha (TSMOM Sharpe ~1.83 OOS), pure arithmetic, no ML/data-snooping surface, emits a clean target-notional intent the policy engine clamps. Vol-targeting pre-shrinks size before drawdown, complementing the circuit-breaker. The BTC-residual reversion (OLS beta via covariance/variance, residual z-score) is closed-form and stdlib-feasible; degrade to long-residual-only on a spot-only onchain executor.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement the Engle-Granger cointegration pairs strategy (stdlib OLS + ADF tabulated constants + z-score bands) as a market-neutral IntentGenerator emitting paired long/short intents; later upgrade the spread estimator to a pure-Python online Kalman dynamic hedge ratio and add OU optimal thresholds + half-life time-stop. Enforce hard per-position stop-loss/time-stop and frequent re-testing.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (mechanical/quant)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The strongest natural fit for a hard-rule disposer — mechanical, market-neutral, fully auditable, stdlib-implementable. Kalman is ~30 lines, O(1) per tick, tiny persistent state — ideal for a local streaming agent. Dominant recurring risk is cointegration/structural break (indistinguishable from a tradeable divergence until the loss lands), so hard stops + time-stops + Sim->paper validation are mandatory. Defer copula confirmation (research-grade, threatens stdlib-only).

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add the Hedge / multiplicative-weights meta-allocator (~30 lines pure Python, weights persisted to local JSON) that scales each sub-strategy's proposed notional before the policy engine, with a per-strategy weight cap and a weight floor for recovery; use CRP/Buy-and-Hold OLPS as must-beat backtest baselines; layer a rule-based regime gate with hysteresis on top.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (mechanical/quant; combines the sub-strategies above)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Provable no-regret guarantee that the ensemble tracks the best sub-strategy, pure stdlib, degrades gracefully, and never bypasses hard rules (it only SIZES proposals). Honest caveat: crypto correlations collapse to 1 in risk-off so diversification is weakest exactly when needed, and regime detection lags 1-3 days — so keep it advisory-sizing only with the drawdown breaker as the real backstop. Medium (not high) because it is a force-multiplier that needs >=2 validated sub-strategies first.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add slow regime/bias modules feeding intent confidence/exposure: DeFiLlama free API (stablecoin dry-powder + exchange netflow, stdlib urllib, no key) for a coarse risk-on/off scalar, and GeckoTerminal keyless DEX API (pool liquidity/volume/OHLCV, includes Base) for onchain liquidity-shock de-risk signals — mapped to the symbol allowlist and throttled/cached for low rate limits.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #1 (chain/exchange: strengthens an EVM/DeFi-on-Base path with keyless onchain data)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Free, keyless, stdlib-fetchable slow bias signals that complement the fast funding/momentum signals to set regime/exposure rather than fire trades. Treat as signal, never as solvency truth (indexer lag/reorgs/wallet-labeling errors). Defer paid black-box feeds (Nansen smart-money, CryptoQuant granular, FundingPulse) behind a feature flag — they break zero-cloud and are crowded/laggable alpha; keep the direct-from-exchange stdlib path as the zero-dependency fallback.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: If/when the OnchainExecutor (Coinbase AgentKit on Base) goes live, route swaps through an MEV-protected RPC (e.g. Alchemy built-in protection on Base) with tight slippage caps, and verify protection empirically on Base Sepolia before mainnet tiny-caps. Treat as alpha-PRESERVATION, complementing the policy engine's per-tx notional cap.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #1 (chain/exchange: required if EVM/DeFi is chosen) and Decision #3 (custody/execution: shapes the OnchainExecutor)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Onchain signals are worthless if predatory MEV bots sandwich the agent's predictable, repeated fills. MEV protection is not absolute (documented private-path sandwich attacks) so verify on testnet rather than trusting 'built-in' claims; the notional cap already bounds extractable size. Medium because it only applies on the onchain leg, which is a later phase.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Treat LLM/news sentiment as an OPTIONAL local feature only: a CPU-side CryptoBERT/FinBERT scorer (zero-cloud, behind the strategy interface with cached deterministic replay for backtests) feeding PROPOSE — and make adversarial-input sanitization (Unicode NFKC + HTML stripping + confidence gating, stdlib unicodedata/html.parser) a NON-BYPASSABLE DISPOSE hard rule. Do NOT adopt time-series foundation models (TimesFM/Chronos) for the core return signal.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (explicitly votes AGAINST LLM-discretionary as the core strategy; LLM stays a noisy optional feature)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Sentiment is weakly/non-stationarily correlated with returns; published 96-97% accuracies are author-dataset-optimistic. But IF any news is consumed, sanitization is safety-critical for a treasury agent (demonstrated homoglyph/hidden-HTML headline attacks cost double-digit returns) and belongs alongside the kill-switch. TimesFM/Chronos are a documented non-fit (negative R2, sub-chance direction, beaten by CatBoost) — reserve at most for auxiliary volatility/funding forecasts. Low impact for alpha, but the sanitization hard rule is high-value defense if news ingestion ships.

themes.theme: LLM Agent Architecture for an autonomous crypto treasury agent (treasuryforge)
themes.consensus:
All 10 agents converge on one load-bearing design rule that independently validates treasuryforge's existing thesis: the LLM must be a PURE PROPOSER and never the disposer/enforcer. Every agent — citing the 2026 Agentic Trading survey (arXiv 2605.19337), the ElizaOS prompt-injection/memory-poisoning exploits, GUARD, and FinCon — states that account state, risk limits, and solvency truth must be deterministic and read-only to the model, and that no LLM output (intent, memory, reflection, judge verdict) may ever widen a hard limit. This maps 1:1 onto treasuryforge's hard-rule policy engine as the sole authority.

Second consensus: the LLM belongs only on the SLOW reflective/strategic loop (minutes-hours cadence), never on the fast/execution path. The survey's time-scale split (reactive=rules/no-LLM, reflective=CoT, strategic=planning) directly endorses keeping the policy engine and executor purely mechanical. Because a treasury agent rebalances on a slow cadence, Plan-and-Execute (one plan call per cycle) is favored over chatty ReAct loops for cost, auditability and fewer hallucination surfaces.

Third consensus (numeric/structural safety, production-grade and stdlib-friendly): never let the LLM be the calculator or the JSON formatter. Constrained/grammar-constrained decoding (llama.cpp GBNF) guarantees structurally-valid intent JSON; PAL/Program-of-Thought offloads ALL arithmetic (notional, fees, P&L, drawdown) to a deterministic Python interpreter using decimal/integer money, never float; a schema+custom-validator retry loop that fails CLOSED catches semantically-invalid intents before policy. These four layers stack cleanly onto PROPOSE->POLICY->EXECUTE and are largely pure-stdlib.

Fourth consensus (memory done safely): memory/reflection is real and useful but must be (a) strictly advisory to PROPOSE, (b) grounded in an EXTERNAL deterministic signal (realized PnL, alpha, drawdown, Sharpe slopes, policy verdicts) not pure self-judgment, and (c) embargo-aware/timestamp-gated to prevent look-ahead leakage so 'validated before real funds' is an honest claim. FinMem's layered memory (fast-decaying working layer + slow long-term, scored by recency+relevance+importance) is the best stdlib-implementable blueprint over sqlite3 with one local embedding model.

Fifth consensus (cost/ops, production-grade): prompt caching of the stable system+policy+tool-schema prefix (~0.1x reads) plus tiered/local-first routing (Ollama small-model triage -> frontier only on hard intents) make a 24/7 loop affordable, and are pure cost-latency levers because the policy engine validates every intent regardless of which model produced it.

Sixth consensus (honest about evidence): the field is empirically immature. The survey reports 0/19 studies fully reproducible and most LLM trading agents fail to beat buy-and-hold on recent data; the 'Alpha Illusion' paper shows multi-agent committees help in only ~20% of configs and add correlated (not independent) agents. Therefore keep real-fund notional caps tiny, treat all LLM-discretionary strategy as experimental, and prove any added complexity in SimExecutor backtests before promoting.
themes.contradictions:
1) Stdlib-only vs best-of-breed memory/orchestration: Several agents recommend production-grade off-the-shelf tools (Mem0, Graphiti/Zep, LangGraph, Coinbase AgentKit) that BREAK the pure-stdlib constraint, while others insist FinMem-style memory and a hand-rolled validate-retry loop are fully achievable in stdlib + sqlite3 + one local embedding model. Resolution offered consistently: isolate any non-stdlib piece behind an optional adapter/interface at the orchestration or memory seam so the policy+executor core stays pure stdlib and degrades gracefully.

2) Reflexion/self-critique usefulness vs the self-correction literature: The reflection-focused agents promote Reflexion/episodic memory loops as 'production-proven,' but the same and other agents cite DeepMind (2310.01798) and Feedback-Friction (2506.11930) showing LLMs CANNOT reliably self-correct from introspection alone and can degrade after self-critique. Reconciled (not truly contradictory) by the rule that reflection must be grounded in external deterministic signals and capped to one round, treated as soft priors only — but it tempers the optimistic 'learns from its mistakes' framing.

3) Multi-agent committee value: The TradingAgents/TrustTrade agents present role committees and debate as a strong pattern; the 'Alpha Illusion' agent directly contradicts the implied benefit, showing extra agents are correlated and debate wins in only ~20% of setups. Resolution: gate any committee behind mandatory P6 ablations (single-agent baseline, net-return-after-friction) in SimExecutor before adopting — committees are likely NOT worth the token/latency cost for a solo builder until proven.

4) Maturity labeling drift: The same FinMem framework is tagged 'research' by one agent and 'emerging' by another; Reflexion is called both 'research' and 'production.' The honest read is that the underlying papers/code exist (so the pattern is real) but trading-specific live validation does not — treat all of these as emerging-at-best for THIS workload regardless of the optimistic tag.

5) Hosted strict structured-outputs vs zero-cloud: One agent offers provider 'strict' Structured Outputs (OpenAI/Anthropic) as ~100% schema-adherent — but this breaks the local-first/zero-cloud principle by sending strategy+balances off-device. Correctly scoped as a future hosted-variant fallback only, not the default.

top_techniques.name: Deterministic propose->dispose->execute with read-only state (Architecture-Capability-Adaptation safety lens)
top_techniques.why: Independently endorsed by every agent and source-backed by the 2026 Agentic Trading survey, ElizaOS exploit post-mortems, GUARD and FinCon. The keystone that makes everything else safe: the policy engine is Layer-A read-only truth, the LLM only proposes. Already treasuryforge's design — adopt the survey's hardening checklist (explicit transaction-cost model, time-consistent splits, full trace logging).
top_techniques.maturity: emerging (survey-level lens, but the architectural rule is production-validated by real exploit evidence)

top_techniques.name: Grammar-constrained decoding (llama.cpp GBNF) + PAL arithmetic + fail-closed schema/validator retry loop
top_techniques.why: The production-grade, stdlib-friendly, zero-cloud stack for numeric/structural reliability. GBNF makes malformed/off-enum intents impossible; PAL/PoT offloads ALL money math to a deterministic interpreter using decimal/integer minor-units (fixes the documented CoT arithmetic collapse); the validate-retry loop catches semantically-invalid intents and fails CLOSED. Turns most hallucinations into structurally-impossible or caught-before-execution states.

top_techniques.name: Plan-and-Execute cadence + LLM only on the slow reflective/strategic loop
top_techniques.why: Matches the reasoning paradigm to the treasury's slow rebalance cadence: one cheap plan call per cycle yields a batch of candidate intents (a single auditable plan object) instead of ReAct's chatty per-step loop. Keeps the fast path purely mechanical, lowers cost and hallucination surface. Production pattern, endorsed by the survey's time-scale split.

top_techniques.name: FinMem-style layered advisory memory with embargo-aware, PnL-grounded reflection
top_techniques.why: The best concrete, stdlib-implementable blueprint for the PROPOSE side: fast-decaying working layer + slow long-term layer scored by recency+relevance+importance over sqlite3, with reflections written ONLY after policy disposes each intent and gated by an Outcome Embargo (timestamp filtering) to prevent look-ahead leakage. Grounded in external signals, advisory-only. Adopt the architecture, not any reported P&L.

top_techniques.name: Prompt caching of the stable prefix + tiered/local-first model routing (Ollama triage -> frontier on hard intents)
top_techniques.why: The biggest, lowest-risk cost/ops win for a 24/7 local-first loop. Cache the system+policy+tool-schema prefix for ~0.1x reads; a local small model triages every tick and only escalates genuinely hard proposals. Pure cost-latency levers because the policy engine validates every intent regardless of which model produced it. Fits zero-cloud via Ollama over stdlib urllib.

top_techniques.name: LangGraph orchestration (SQLite checkpointer + interrupt_before) wrapping Coinbase AgentKit as downstream OnchainExecutor only
top_techniques.why: Best architectural fit for making propose->policy->execute an explicit, auditable, resumable graph with a built-in human-approval/kill gate (interrupt_before on the execute node) — ideal for the testnet->mainnet promotion gate. AgentKit's CDP-managed MPC wallet is a turnkey answer to custody, used strictly as the wallet/signing backend behind the policy engine, never the brain. Opt-in at the seam so the core stays stdlib.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Harden the existing propose->dispose->execute split per the Agentic Trading survey checklist: explicit fee/slippage model in every executor, full structured trace log (intent + retrieved memories + policy verdict + execution result), and a time-consistent backtest harness gate before any executor promotion
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Source-backed validation of treasuryforge's core thesis and the cheapest path to making 'validated before real funds' honest. No architecture change — pure hardening + evidence checklist. The survey names the exact reproducibility gaps (0/19 fully reproducible); an explicit transaction-cost model is non-negotiable because backtests without it look profitable and lose money live.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Cross-cutting safety/validation across all three decisions; most directly gates decision #2 (strategy) by requiring proof in Sim before promotion

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add grammar-constrained decoding (llama.cpp GBNF pinning symbol to allowlist enum, side to buy/sell, numeric fields) + a hand-rolled schema/custom-validator retry loop that fails CLOSED on exhaustion
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Eliminates the entire class of malformed/off-enum/unparseable intents before they reach the policy engine, and catches semantically-invalid intents (over-cap notional, off-allowlist) with auto-retry, fixing ~95%+ on first retry. Zero-cloud, minimal deps (GBNF is in llama.cpp itself; the validator reuses the policy rules). Guarantees a well-formed intent — not a good trade — so it complements, never replaces, the policy engine.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (strategy): makes an LLM-discretionary proposer safe to wire in regardless of model

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Route ALL money arithmetic through a sandboxed PAL/Program-of-Thought step using decimal.Decimal or integer minor-units; add a separate non-LLM arithmetic verifier (recompute notional from qty*price, assert ranges/units) between PAL output and policy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Token-level arithmetic provably collapses on large/precise numbers (PAL 61% vs CoT 23% on GSM-hard) — exactly the regime of position sizing, P&L, drawdown %, and notional caps. Emitting whitelisted Python that a deterministic interpreter executes (AST allowlist, no imports/IO, fail-closed) means every notional/fee/slippage number is computed by code, never hallucinated. Pure stdlib, local-first.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (strategy): core numeric reliability for any sizing logic

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Structure the cycle as Plan-and-Execute: one planner LLM call per rebalance cycle -> list of candidate intents (the plan, a plain JSON object) -> deterministic executor maps each through the policy engine -> SimExecutor. Keep ReAct only for ad-hoc research. Re-plan every cycle against fresh deterministic state
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Matches the reasoning paradigm to the slow treasury cadence: fewer LLM calls (1-2 vs ~10), lower cost, fewer hallucination-propagation surfaces, and a single auditable plan object for the trace log. Re-planning each cycle mitigates Plan-and-Execute's stale-state brittleness.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (strategy): the orchestration shape for the LLM-discretionary path

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement FinMem-style layered memory in stdlib over sqlite3 (working/short/mid/long buckets, score = recency_decay + cosine_relevance + importance), writing a reflection record ONLY after the policy engine disposes each intent, gated by an Outcome Embargo (retrieve only episodes whose decision_time<=now and whose outcome was knowable). Ground reflections in realized PnL/alpha/Sharpe slopes/policy verdicts. Keep strictly advisory to PROPOSE; one embedding model is the only non-stdlib piece, behind an optional adapter
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Best concrete blueprint to let the agent learn from its own enforced outcomes without retraining, while the embargo prevents the single most dangerous failure (look-ahead/outcome leakage). Honest caveat: validated only on single-stock equities, not crypto; reflection can amplify a bad lesson and LLMs cannot reliably self-correct from introspection (DeepMind 2310.01798) — so cap to one round, version lessons, keep raw episodes, and never let a lesson touch a policy threshold.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (strategy): enriches the PROPOSE step

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add prompt caching (cache_control on the stable system+policy+tool-schema prefix; ttl '1h' for slow polling) and tiered routing with a local Ollama small model triaging every tick over stdlib urllib, escalating only hard proposals to a frontier model. Instrument cache_read_input_tokens to confirm hits
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Makes a 24/7 loop affordable (cache reads ~0.1x; documented 5-10x input-cost reduction on multi-turn agent loops) and keeps the always-on triage zero-API-cost and on-device, fitting the local-first/sensitive-balance preference. Pure cost-latency lever — the policy engine validates every intent regardless of tier, so a weaker model is never a safety surface. Validate triage accuracy against logged frontier decisions before trusting the cheap path.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Ops/cost lever (no Phase-2 decision directly); enables the decision #2 LLM path at solo-builder budget

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Wrap the propose->policy->execute pipeline as a LangGraph StateGraph with a SQLite checkpointer and interrupt_before on the execute node as the testnet->mainnet manual-approval/kill gate; keep the policy engine a pure deterministic non-LLM node. Isolate LangGraph to the orchestration seam only
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Turns the pipeline into an explicit, auditable, crash-recoverable graph with time-travel debugging of exactly why a trade was proposed, plus a free human-approval gate before any real funds move — directly serving 'validated before real funds.' Cost: a non-stdlib LangChain-ecosystem dependency that churns APIs; mitigate by confining it to the orchestration layer so the policy/executor core stays pure stdlib and swappable. Optional — the same gate can be hand-rolled in stdlib.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #3 (custody) indirectly via the approval gate before fund-moving execution; primarily an orchestration/ops choice

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement OnchainExecutor as a thin adapter over Coinbase AgentKit (coinbase-agentkit Python SDK) Trade/Send actions on Base Sepolia first, strictly downstream of the policy engine (per-tx cap, allowlist, solvency enforced BEFORE calling AgentKit). Use its CDP-managed MPC wallet as the managed-custody option to compare against a self-hosted smart-account
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Maps 1:1 onto the planned OnchainExecutor and gives a turnkey, testnet-first managed-MPC custody path — a concrete answer to decision #3. It is an action/wallet SDK (not a runtime), so it fits the pure-proposer design without dictating strategy. Honest caveats: vendor/custody lock-in to Coinbase CDP, and the TS SDK leads — verify Python package version/feature parity before depending on a specific action.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #3 (custody: managed MPC) and decision #1 (chain/exchange: EVM/Base)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Mine ElizaOS plugin-auto-trader for disposer rules (honeypot/RugCheck token validation, stop-loss/take-profit, per-strategy risk caps) and port them into the hard-rule policy engine — but do NOT run ElizaOS. Treat its memory-poisoning result as a hard requirement: the policy engine reads only trusted state, never LLM-authored memory. Pin/verify all Python deps against litellm-style supply-chain attacks
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Its safety-plugin list is a concrete, free checklist of disposer rules; its 2026 prompt-injection/memory-poisoning exploits (agents gaslit into losing millions) are the strongest real-world evidence FOR treasuryforge's architecture — proof the LLM must stay a pure proposer behind a deterministic non-LLM gate. Adopting the runtime would import a TS stack and a demonstrated attack surface, so mine concepts only. Some features are Solana/Jupiter-specific and won't transfer to EVM/Base.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #1 (chain/exchange) and #2 (strategy): supplies allowlist/token-safety rules; reinforces the safety boundary

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: If a multi-agent committee is ever considered (TradingAgents role committee + TrustTrade selective-consensus + DiscoUQ confidence-to-sizing), gate it behind the 'Alpha Illusion' P6 ablations in SimExecutor: single-agent baseline, role-similarity/correlated-error measurement, disagreement rate, net-return AFTER spreads/slippage/token cost, and coordination/latency cost. Only promote if it beats a single agent net of friction; prefer genuinely different model families over personas
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Honest hype check: committees sound smarter but the 2026 'Alpha Illusion' analysis shows added agents are correlated (not independent), debate wins in only ~20% of configs, and persona diversity is rhetorical. For a solo builder watching token spend, a committee is likely NOT worth the cost versus a single well-constrained agent — but the P6 protocol and the TrustTrade abstain-on-disagreement / confidence-sized-notional idea are worth keeping as a future gate, not a v1 build.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision #2 (strategy): a discipline for justifying — or rejecting — extra strategy complexity

themes.consensus:
There is strong, repeated agreement across all 10 agents on a single architectural principle: position sizing and risk math belong in the PROPOSE step as a *suggester* that outputs a CEILING, and the existing hard-rule POLICY engine must remain the sole DISPOSER via min(proposed, hard_cap). No dynamic method should ever be trusted to act alone. Specific consensus points:

1. Vol-targeting is the single highest-leverage drawdown reducer. Three independent agents cite it (one with concrete numbers: max DD -31%->-14%, Sharpe 0.99->1.54), all converging on stdlib-implementable inverse-vol sizing (size = target_vol/realized_vol, ~14-period ATR or EWMA RiskMetrics lambda=0.94) sitting between propose and the hard notional cap.

2. Fractional Kelly (1/4-1/2, pros favor 0.1-0.25) is the right edge-aware sizer, used strictly as a CAP not a target. Four agents independently land here, and all flag the SAME failure mode: mu/edge estimation error (standard error ~ sigma/sqrt(N)) can swing optimal size 2x; full Kelly produces 50-80% crypto drawdowns. Consensus: low fraction + treat as ceiling + bound by vol-target and per-tx cap.

3. The binary kill-switch should evolve into a graded, out-of-band, multi-signal circuit breaker (throttle -> read-only sandbox -> hard halt) with mark-to-market (realized+unrealized) drawdown and MANUAL re-arming. Multiple agents independently propose this exact 3-stage ladder and explicitly validate treasuryforge's propose/dispose/execute split as the correct way to make the breaker un-bypassable by the (possibly LLM) strategy.

4. Every dynamic risk method shares ONE failure mode and all agents say so: they lag/gap through fast crypto crashes and can lever UP into the calm-before-a-crash, and all correlation-based limits weaken precisely when correlations spike toward 1.0 in a crash. Therefore the hard per-tx cap + kill-switch are non-negotiable backstops, and everything must be SimExecutor/Kraken-paper validated before real funds.

5. Defense-in-depth via independent failure domains is universal: a dead-man switch (exchange-native Kraken cancelAllOrdersAfter and/or onchain spend-permission revocation) must live OUTSIDE the agent's own failure domain so guardrails survive the agent crashing/hanging. Local watchdog actions should stay non-financial (halt/alert) and delegate fund-moving to exchange/onchain timers so a false-positive can never move funds.
themes.contradictions:
1. STDLIB-ONLY vs. richer tooling. The hardest tension. The project is strictly stdlib-only, but several high-value recommendations break that: OPA/Rego needs a Go sidecar; JsonLogic/py-rules-engine are third-party; Hierarchical Risk Parity reference implementations pull NumPy/SciPy/pandas; Hypothesis (the single best-fit validator) and Foundry are non-stdlib. Resolution offered by the agents themselves: keep the policy CORE pure-stdlib; treat Hypothesis as a tests/-only dev dependency (legitimate, not a runtime violation); vendor a tiny auditable JsonLogic if rules-as-data is wanted; treat NumPy as a strategy-PROPOSE-only optional dep kept out of the policy core. So it is reconcilable, not a true deadlock, but it forces explicit scope decisions.

2. CEX-managed-policy vs. local-first/self-hosted ethos. The Coinbase CDP Server Wallet v2 signer-side Policy Engine is genuinely powerful (guardrails enforced BELOW a compromised agent) but is custody-coupled, vendor-locked to Coinbase, and contradicts both the self-hosted-smart-account custody option and the zero-cloud preference. One agent flags it as on-path (since AgentKit is the planned OnchainExecutor); another implicitly favors the self-hosted spend-permission revoke() route. This maps directly onto unresolved Phase-2 decision (3).

3. Corroboration-gating (require 2 anomalies before tripping) vs. fast single-cause response. One agent recommends requiring two anomalies in a window to cut false positives; this directly DELAYS response to a genuine single-cause fast crash — which every other agent says is the dominant crypto failure mode. Tension is real: corroboration is good for runaway-loop/velocity bugs but bad for crash response, so it should apply to behavioral (velocity/duplicate-fill) triggers, NOT to the P&L/drawdown trip.

4. CPPI floor "guarantee" vs. crypto gap risk. CPPI is presented as a bounded-worst-case floor, but multiple agents concede the 1/m bound assumes continuous trading/liquidity that crypto routinely violates (overnight/on-chain gaps pierce the floor) and that it can cash-lock and miss the rebound. So the "guarantee" is overstated; it is strong throttling, not a hard floor.

top_techniques.name: Volatility targeting (inverse-vol / ATR or EWMA RiskMetrics sizing) between PROPOSE and the per-tx cap
top_techniques.why: Cited by 4 of 10 agents as the single highest-leverage drawdown reducer (one study: max DD -31%->-14%, Sharpe 0.99->1.54). Pure-stdlib (~20 lines, EWMA lambda=0.94 or 14-period ATR), deterministic, unit-testable, SimExecutor-replayable, and composes cleanly as min(vol_target_size, notional_cap). Highest ratio of drawdown reduction to implementation cost.

top_techniques.name: Graded out-of-band circuit breaker (throttle -> read-only sandbox -> hard halt) with mark-to-market drawdown and MANUAL re-arming
top_techniques.why: Multiple agents independently converge on upgrading the existing binary kill-switch to a 3-stage state machine that fails soft, computes drawdown on realized+unrealized equity, and is un-bypassable by the strategy. Validates and directly extends treasuryforge's propose/dispose/execute split. Fully in the stdlib policy engine.

top_techniques.name: Three-layer dead-man / kill system: exchange-native (Kraken cancelAllOrdersAfter) + onchain spend-permission revoke() + local watchdog heartbeat
top_techniques.why: Flagged as the highest-value guardrail the agent does NOT yet have, because it survives the agent itself dying — closing the hung-but-not-crashed blind spot. Each layer lives in an independent failure domain; local action stays non-financial so a false-positive can't move funds. Maps onto both Kraken-paper and Base-Sepolia roadmaps.

top_techniques.name: Fractional Kelly (0.1-0.5) used strictly as a CEILING, ideally the binary form from the agent's own trade log
top_techniques.why: Four agents converge on it as the principled edge-aware sizer, all with the identical caveat (estimation error swings size 2x; full Kelly = 50-80% crypto DD). Self-calibrating from the agent's own SimExecutor/paper trade history fits a local-first logging agent. Stdlib arithmetic; always bounded by vol-target and the hard cap.

top_techniques.name: Historical-simulation CVaR/Expected-Shortfall + deterministic stress-scenario veto (BTC/ETH -70%, de-peg, correlation->1)
top_techniques.why: Turns the backward-looking circuit-breaker into a forward-looking tail veto on data the agent already produces, all in statistics/math/random. Crypto tails are fat (empirical 99% CVaR ~1.68x vs 1.16x normal) and Jan-2026 showed diversification failing under deleveraging, so deterministic config-driven stress scenarios are mandatory, not optional. Self-audited with Kupiec+Christoffersen.

top_techniques.name: Hypothesis stateful testing (RuleBasedStateMachine + @invariant) of the policy engine
top_techniques.why: The single best-fit guardrail VALIDATOR: emit random intent sequences through the real policy engine and assert kill-switch/drawdown/notional/allowlist/solvency invariants after every step, with auto-shrunk minimal counterexamples. Proves the disposer holds across arbitrary histories before real funds. A tests/-only dependency, so it does not violate the stdlib-only runtime.

top_techniques.name: Deterministic pre-trade slippage/liquidity guard (AMM getAmountOut / Quoter, or Kraken L2 book-walk) enforced atomically via minAmountOut / limit price
top_techniques.why: Turns liquidity risk into a hard, pre-trade computable number the policy engine disposes on, with the executor enforcing the chosen bound atomically (on-chain minAmountOut+deadline, or post-only/limit on CEX). Works on both Phase-2 venue paths and is testable with zero real funds. Pure-arithmetic for v2 AMMs.

top_techniques.name: Concentration gate: post-trade HHI ceiling paired with correlation-cluster exposure caps
top_techniques.why: Cheap stdlib sum-of-squared-weights ceiling that the policy engine thresholds like the notional cap, paired with cluster caps to fix HHI's correlation blindness (BTC/ETH ~0.7-0.8, BTC/Nasdaq ~0.9). Cluster map computed offline keeps the live disposer deterministic. Stops the agent stacking one macro factor across many tickers.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a stdlib vol-targeting sizing layer (EWMA RiskMetrics lambda=0.94, sqrt(365) annualization) between PROPOSE and the per-tx cap, final size = min(vol_target_size, notional_cap)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Strongest single drawdown reducer in the corpus (-31%->-14% max DD, Sharpe 0.99->1.54), ~20 lines of pure stdlib over data the executor already sees, fully unit-testable against canned return series and SimExecutor-replayable. Composes with the existing cap without touching the dispose contract. Lowest-risk, highest-payoff first build.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: makes a mechanical/quant sizing core viable independent of any LLM, reducing the case for LLM-discretionary sizing

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Upgrade the binary kill-switch to a graded 3-stage out-of-band circuit breaker (Yellow throttle/lower cap -> Orange force-route to SimExecutor/read-only -> Red halt+persist state) using mark-to-market (realized+unrealized) drawdown, with MANUAL-only re-arming and config the agent can read but not mutate
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Multiple agents independently converge here; it directly extends the existing drawdown breaker and validates treasuryforge's propose/dispose/execute split (strategy cannot reason past the breaker). Failing soft avoids capital-destroying whipsaw while preserving a hard Red floor. Entirely inside the stdlib policy engine. Caveat: stage cutoffs (e.g. -10%/-20%) and cooldown/re-entry are unsourced for crypto-treasury cadence and must be backtested.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: enforces that even an LLM-discretionary strategy is hard-bounded by an independent control plane

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add Hypothesis stateful testing (RuleBasedStateMachine + @invariant) in tests/ to fuzz random intent sequences through the real policy engine and assert all safety invariants after every step
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The 'validate before real funds' mandate, operationalized. Proves kill-switch/drawdown/notional/allowlist/solvency hold across arbitrary histories (not hand-picked cases) and auto-shrinks any failure to a minimal repro. Maps 1:1 onto the existing SimExecutor harness with zero new runtime infra; as a dev/test dependency it does not violate stdlib-only at runtime. Honest limit: it is bounded random exploration, finds bugs but cannot certify absence.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement an exchange-native dead-man switch on the PaperExecutor path (Kraken cancelAllOrdersAfter, 60s timeout pinged every 15-30s); wire the policy kill-switch flip to set timeout=0 AND stop the heartbeat so resting orders auto-flush
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The single highest-value guardrail the agent currently lacks: it survives the agent process hanging, crashing, or losing network — the policy engine only disposes intents while alive. Pure periodic HTTPS POSTs (stdlib), directly on the Kraken-paper roadmap, validatable with zero real funds. Honest limit: cancels resting ORDERS only, not filled positions — a separate flatten step is still required.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: concrete, production guardrail that materially strengthens the CEX/Kraken path

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a stdlib risk module: rolling historical-simulation CVaR/Expected-Shortfall on the agent's own returns plus a config-driven deterministic stress-scenario veto (BTC/ETH -70%, COVID/2022 replays, stablecoin de-peg, correlation->1); self-audit with Kupiec + Christoffersen periodically
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Turns the backward-looking breaker into a forward-looking tail veto using only statistics/math/random on data the agent already logs. Stress scenarios cover exactly what CVaR misses (out-of-sample tails, correlation breakdown) — mandatory given fat crypto tails and Jan-2026 deleveraging. Christoffersen clustering failure of static VaR models is a real, actionable warning for the drawdown budget. Honest limit: short windows understate tails (so stress is non-optional) and a small/young treasury may lack power for the backtests.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a deterministic pre-trade slippage/liquidity guard in the policy layer (AMM getAmountOut / v3-v4 Quoter on DeFi, or Kraken L2 book-walk on CEX) that disposes on predicted price-impact > cap, and have the executor enforce the bound atomically via on-chain minAmountOut+deadline or a CEX limit/post-only price
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Converts liquidity risk into a hard pre-trade number, mirroring the per-tx notional cap as a per-tx slippage cap, with the SAME SlippageGuard rule working on both Phase-2 venue paths. Production-grade and testable with zero real funds (Kraken paper / Base Sepolia). Honest limits: v2 closed-form is exact only for constant-product pools (verify 997/1000 fee constants per DEX); quotes go stale between read and broadcast; headline TVL hides depth-at-price.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: a single guard spec that works whether Phase-2 picks CEX or DeFi, de-risking that decision

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: For the OnchainExecutor path, structure custody as a self-hosted Base smart account where the agent is a constrained SPENDER under a Coinbase SpendPermission (allowance = notional/period cap, end-time = built-in dead-man expiry), with the human-held OWNER key holding the true revoke() kill switch; validate on Base Sepolia
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Custody-side analog of the per-tx cap, rate-limit, and kill-switch enforced by an audited on-chain contract the agent CANNOT bypass — true defense-in-depth below a compromised agent. The period allowance + expiry enforce rate-limit and a dead-man auto-disarm at the chain level for free, and revoke() is the human kill switch. Honest limits: adds key-management surface (owner key must be safeguarded offline), governs token TRANSFERS not DeFi-position unwinds, and contract addresses/audits (Spearbit Oct-Dec 2024) need re-verification before mainnet.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody: argues for self-hosted smart-account with the human as owner over managed MPC, while staying local-first

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add fractional Kelly (start 0.1-0.25) computed from the agent's OWN rolling trade log (binary form f*=(bp-q)/b, recomputed ~every 50 trades), used strictly as a CEILING fed into the same min() guard as vol-target and the per-tx cap, with a minimum-sample gate
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Four agents endorse it as the principled edge-aware sizer; self-calibration from the agent's own SimExecutor/paper history fits a local-first logging agent. But it is deliberately ranked below vol-targeting because all four agents flag the identical dominant risk: edge-estimation error swings size 2x and full Kelly = 50-80% crypto DD, so it must never be the sole gate. The conservative fraction is the load-bearing safety knob and should be a non-agent-controllable policy constant.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: lets a quant/discretionary strategy express conviction without authorizing oversized bets

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a post-trade HHI concentration ceiling (sum of squared weights, reject if > ~0.30-0.40) paired with correlation-cluster exposure caps, with the {symbol->cluster} map computed by an offline job that writes a small JSON the live disposer reads
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Cheap stdlib arithmetic gate in the same family as the notional cap; the cluster pairing fixes HHI's correlation blindness so the agent can't stack one macro factor (BTC/ETH/Nasdaq beta) across many tickers. Offline cluster computation keeps the live dispose path deterministic and fast. Marked emerging/medium because thresholds are heuristic and every correlation-based limit weakens exactly in crashes when correlations spike toward 1; keep conservative and only relevant once the agent is truly multi-symbol.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add CPPI floor/cushion throttling (ratcheting floor = max(prev, equity*(1-max_dd)), cap risky exposure at m*cushion) as a continuous complement to the graded breaker, reusing the peak-equity state already tracked
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Gives a closed-form, monotone, continuously-de-risking exposure ceiling that throttles gracefully instead of slamming off at a threshold — a stronger continuous companion to the breaker. Pure stdlib reusing existing drawdown state. Marked medium (not high) and explicitly NOT a 'guarantee': the 1/m floor bound assumes continuous liquidity crypto routinely violates (gap moves pierce it), and it can cash-lock and miss the rebound, so it is strong throttling layered behind the hard backstops, with m and re-entry needing backtests.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a local stdlib watchdog/heartbeat state machine (ARMED->WARNING->GRACE->TRIGGERED) whose ONLY actions are non-financial (set policy kill-switch + stop the exchange/onchain heartbeat); never let it move funds directly
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Closes the hung-but-not-crashed blind spot (a silently blocked loop emits no intents and no safety reaction), turning agent silence into a positive trigger. Pure stdlib daemon thread. Kept medium and deliberately non-financial so an over-aggressive timeout on a GC pause/network blip cannot itself liquidate — it delegates actual flushing to the exchange-native and onchain dead-man timers (compose with those).

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Externalize policy rules as serializable JSON via a tiny VENDORED JsonLogic evaluator (keep the stdlib in-process engine; defer OPA/Rego sidecar until the rule set genuinely outgrows it)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Makes caps/allowlist/thresholds version-controlled, diffable, hot-swappable data instead of code, fitting local-first far better than an OPA Go sidecar (which adds a non-Python runtime + network hop and must fail-closed). Marked low because it is an ergonomics/maintainability win, not a safety capability gain, and JsonLogic cannot express stateful checks (sliding-window rate-limit, live-balance solvency) — those still need Python helpers. Vendor one fork and pin it rather than depend on PyPI freshness.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Treat as lower-priority / verify-before-trust: Coinbase CDP Server Wallet v2 signer-side Policy Engine, Hierarchical Risk Parity (NumPy-dependent), Foundry/formal verification of on-chain guards, and corroboration-gating of the P&L trip
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Honest hype/scope check: CDP's strongest 'enclave-level, credential-proof' enforcement appears only in marketing, not the verified docs, and it contradicts the self-hosted/zero-cloud ethos with Coinbase lock-in. HRP's clean libs break stdlib-only and it optimizes diversification not alpha (don't over-claim). Foundry invariant fuzzing (production) and contract formal verification (research-grade) only pay off IF Phase-2 picks self-hosted-smart-account custody AND you deploy your own guard contract — overkill/off-stack for a CEX-only or audited-off-the-shelf-guard path. Corroboration-gating should apply only to behavioral/velocity triggers, never to the crash-response P&L trip.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody: scopes the heavy on-chain verification work strictly to the self-hosted-smart-account branch

themes.consensus:
Ten agents converge on one dominant lesson that directly vindicates treasuryforge's propose->dispose->execute architecture: NEVER trust the agent runtime or the LLM for authorization — enforce spend limits at a boundary the agent cannot reason or be injected past. The 2026 incident record (Grok-Bankr $150K Morse-code prompt-injection heist; the arXiv study where malicious LLM routers drained a live ETH wallet) is cited as proof that LLM guardrails are at the wrong layer. The wallet/policy boundary "does not care whether an instruction arrived in Morse, Mandarin, or marker pen — it cares whether the principal has the right to move those funds." This is exactly treasuryforge's hard-rule policy engine.

Second strong consensus: build a layered, defense-in-depth custody ladder, not a single tool. The same caps the local Python policy engine enforces should be MIRRORED a second time at the custody/signing layer (on-chain enforcer, MPC/TEE policy engine, or scoped exchange key) so a fully compromised host still cannot exceed budget. Every agent frames the vendor/on-chain layer as a REDUNDANT second ring, never a replacement for the local gate.

Third: the agent must hold only a narrowly-scoped, short-lived, revocable credential (session key / scoped API key / delegated sub-user), never a master/root key. Revocation/expiry = a true external kill-switch. Per-tx notional cap, symbol allowlist, and rate-limit each have a near 1:1 analogue at the custody layer (value caveat, contract/recipient allowlist, validity window).

Fourth: keys should never live in the Python process, env var, or plaintext config. The unanimous worst-pattern call-out is the private-key-as-env-var. Acceptable baselines range from OS-keystore + scrypt-encrypted keyfile (zero new infra) up through hardware/TEE/MPC isolation.

Fifth (cross-cutting honesty): NONE of the managed wallets reliably enforce YOUR notional caps by default — verified in the AgentKit README which documents no wallet-layer spend controls — AND none of these providers ships a real, first-class Python SDK except Coinbase's coinbase-agentkit/cdp-sdk (which itself breaks the stdlib-only constraint). For everything else (Turnkey, Privy, Cobo, Fireblocks) plan on raw REST over urllib plus a signing lib. So the local hard-rule policy engine MUST remain the authoritative dispose layer; the custody layer is supplementary.

Sixth: validate everything on Base Sepolia testnet (and/or a mainnet fork) with throwaway funds before any real capital, and verify all vendor "enclave/compromise-proof/pricing/tier" claims against live docs — many strong claims come from vendor or exchange marketing blogs.
themes.contradictions:
1) Managed-MPC vs self-hosted/on-chain — the core Phase-2 custody tension. Agents 1/4/5/6 lean toward Coinbase CDP managed MPC (TEE-enforced, production, lowest friction, real Python SDK, the project's own named roadmap) as the pragmatic answer. Agents 2/8/10 argue the opposite: managed MPC enforcement lives OFF-CHAIN at the vendor's infrastructure, which is a custodial trust + KYC + outage + vendor-lock boundary that CONTRADICTS the project's strict local-first / zero-cloud preference; they prefer ERC-7715/7579/4337 on-chain enforcers that are publicly verifiable and self-custodial. This is a genuine values-vs-friction tradeoff, not a factual disagreement.

2) On-chain enforcement maturity. Agent 2 labels ERC-7715/7579 "production"; agents 3/8/10 label the SAME ERC-7715 path "emerging" and explicitly flag it as still DRAFT as of April 2026, dev-build-gated (MetaMask requires a Flask dev build for full features), with stability tiers it "could not confirm" from the docs. Honest read: the underlying ERC-4337 smart accounts + audited spending-limit/session-key modules (ZeroDev, Rhinestone SmartSession, Safe) are production with ~30M+ 4337 accounts live; the specific ERC-7715 wallet_grantPermissions UX is still stabilizing. Don't treat 7715 as GA.

3) Solidity-cost framing. Agents 2/8/10 present writing/auditing custom on-chain hook/predicate/enforcer contracts as a serious risk and skill mismatch for a pure-Python builder; the mitigation (use AUDITED community modules — OpenZeppelin community-contracts, Rhinestone — not bespoke code) is offered but under-emphasized. The contradiction is whether on-chain enforcement is "achievable for a solo builder" — it is IF and ONLY IF you avoid bespoke Solidity.

4) ERC-7521 (validity predicates) is called the most ELEGANT fit for encoding the full policy engine on-chain (agent 8) but simultaneously the least mature (draft, sparse implementations) — so elegance and readiness point in opposite directions. Resolution: watch-list, not critical path.

No hard factual contradictions on the keystore baseline, the env-var anti-pattern, scoped CEX keys, or the defense-in-depth thesis — those are unanimous.

top_techniques.name: Authorization-at-the-execution-boundary (LLM-independent hard-rule policy gate)
top_techniques.why: The single most-validated technique across all 10 agents and the one treasuryforge ALREADY implements. The 2026 Grok-Bankr and malicious-router incidents prove this is THE correct mitigation layer. Actionable hardening: feed the policy engine ONLY the structured intent (symbol, side, notional, recipient) and NEVER the LLM free-text rationale; make policy params immutable to the agent process; add an append-only audit log of every (intent, verdict) pair; unit-test the gate adversarially with encoded inputs (base64/Morse/NATO). Production, zero new dependency, highest impact.

top_techniques.name: OS-keystore + scrypt-encrypted keyfile (kill the env-var anti-pattern)
top_techniques.why: Unanimous worst-pattern call-out is private-key-as-env-var. This is the zero-cloud, local-first baseline that fixes it NOW with essentially no new infra: scrypt/PBKDF2-encrypted eth-account JSON keystore, passphrase in Windows DPAPI/Credential Manager. Decrypted key lives only in-process at sign time, isolated inside the Executor module. Honest caveat: reaching DPAPI needs a small dep (keyring/pywin32) so stdlib-only needs a narrow exception or a manual passphrase prompt; and it gives no hardware isolation if the unlocked process is breached.

top_techniques.name: Scoped CEX API keys (trade-only, no-withdraw, IP-whitelisted)
top_techniques.why: Cheapest, most mature, best local-first fit and the perfect match for the planned PaperExecutor=Kraken-paper stage. Pure stdlib HTTP signing, no smart-contract/MPC infra. A withdraw-disabled key means the worst case of a rogue agent is bad trades, never fund exfiltration — a hard custody boundary enforced server-side by the exchange, complementing the local solvency/drawdown breakers. Caveat: does NOT enforce per-trade notional/rate inside the exchange (those stay only in the local engine), and IP whitelist breaks on dynamic residential IP changes.

top_techniques.name: ERC-4337 smart account + scoped session keys + audited spending-limit/allowlist modules (ZeroDev / Rhinestone SmartSession / Safe 4337)
top_techniques.why: The most production-ready SELF-CUSTODIAL path to make limits survive a full host compromise: the agent holds only a session key whose on-chain caps mirror the local policy (per-tx cap = value limit, symbol allowlist = contract/asset allowlist, rate-limit = validity window/count, kill-switch = revocation). ~30M+ 4337 accounts live by mid-2026 = mature tooling. Use AUDITED community modules, not bespoke Solidity. Caveats: SDKs are JS/TS-first (shell out to a small JS signer or hand-roll UserOp RPC), and security shifts to correct scope/module config — must test revocation on Base Sepolia first.

top_techniques.name: Coinbase CDP Server Wallets v2 + AgentKit (managed MPC, cb-mpc + AWS Nitro TEE policy engine)
top_techniques.why: The lowest-friction managed-MPC path and the project's own named OnchainExecutor roadmap, with explicit Base Sepolia support, an IDENTICAL code path testnet->mainnet, gasless on Base, keys never in the Python process, and a REAL Python SDK (pip install coinbase-agentkit / cdp-sdk). Policy engine enforces recipient/amount/contract allowlists + session caps + per-token allowances inside the enclave BEFORE signing. Caveats that keep it honest: it is managed/custodial (keyshare split with Coinbase, not pure self-custody), enforcement is OFF-CHAIN at Coinbase infra (not publicly verifiable), it breaks the stdlib-only + zero-cloud constraints, the enclave only enforces tx-SHAPE rules (drawdown/solvency/rate stay local), and the README documents no wallet-layer spend controls by default — you must configure them.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Harden the existing in-process policy gate: structured-intent-only input, agent-immutable params, append-only (intent,verdict) audit log, adversarial/encoding unit tests
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy: mechanical/quant vs LLM-discretionary) and cross-cutting safety
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Unanimously the highest-leverage, zero-cost move and the direct lesson of the 2026 Grok/router heists. It is the real authorization backstop regardless of which custody or chain you pick. Critically, it de-risks an LLM-discretionary strategy: if the policy engine never sees the LLM's free-text rationale and its params are immutable to the agent, prompt-injection cannot escalate spend. Implement before any real funds; it gates everything downstream.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Replace any plaintext/env-var key handling with an OS-keystore + scrypt-encrypted JSON keystore loaded only inside the Executor (Windows DPAPI for the passphrase)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody) — the self-hosted baseline rung
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Kills the single worst, unanimously-cited anti-pattern with near-zero new infra and stays fully local-first/zero-cloud. It is the first rung of the custody ladder and ships today behind the existing Execution interface without touching propose->dispose->execute. Accept a narrow non-stdlib exception (keyring/pywin32) or use a manual passphrase prompt.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: For the PaperExecutor/CEX path, issue trade-only, no-withdraw, IP-whitelisted, expiring API keys (Kraken/Coinbase) as a server-side reinforcement of the local policy engine
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange — the CEX option) and Decision 3 (custody = exchange)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Most mature, cheapest, best-fit hard custody boundary for the validate-before-funds CEX stage: worst case of a rogue agent is bad trades, never exfiltration. Pure stdlib HTTP signing, no smart-contract risk. It is the lowest-risk way to reach real-money-adjacent validation (Kraken paper) and makes the no-withdraw boundary and IP-lock an out-of-band mirror of the policy engine. Note the per-trade notional/rate caps still live only locally, so keep the policy engine authoritative.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: For the on-chain path, prototype an ERC-4337 smart account with a scoped, short-lived session key + AUDITED spending-limit/allowlist modules (ZeroDev or Rhinestone SmartSession or Safe 4337) on Base Sepolia; keep all EVM signing/RPC inside the Executor boundary
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody = self-hosted smart-account) and Decision 1 (chain = EVM/Base)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: This is the most production-ready way to get TRUE self-custodial, on-chain-enforced, publicly verifiable limits that survive a full host compromise, with a near 1:1 mapping to the existing policy primitives and instant revocation as a real kill-switch. It is the strongest fit for the project's local-first/zero-lock-in values. Make it achievable by using audited community modules (no bespoke Solidity) and shelling out to a thin JS signer so the Python core stays clean. Validate session-key revocation and expiry safe-fail on testnet before mainnet tiny-caps.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement the OnchainExecutor against Coinbase CDP Server Wallets v2 + AgentKit (managed MPC + Nitro TEE policy engine) as the pragmatic, lowest-friction Base path — configured as a REDUNDANT second gate, mirroring local caps as CDP policy rules
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody = managed MPC) and Decision 1 (chain = Base)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: It is the project's own named roadmap, the only option with a real Python SDK and an identical testnet->mainnet code path, keys never in-process, gasless on Base. Best choice IF lowest-friction tiny-cap rollout outweighs the zero-cloud ideal. Be honest in the build: it is managed/custodial (keyshare split with Coinbase), enforcement is off-chain/non-verifiable, it breaks stdlib-only + zero-cloud, the enclave enforces only tx-shape rules (drawdown/solvency/rate MUST stay local), and you must explicitly configure spend policies (the README ships none). Best implemented as a thin Executor calling CDP REST/CLI rather than importing the full SDK into the core.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a Secure-Element hardware signer (Ledger USB, or air-gapped Keystone via QR) as the un-bypassable final gate for the mainnet tiny-caps phase, behind the Execution interface
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody — self-hosted hardware rung)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Strongest practical key isolation a solo builder can self-host with zero cloud: host malware can only REQUEST signatures, never extract the key. Honest limit: per-tx physical confirmation breaks full autonomy, so it suits low-frequency/high-value treasury moves and human-confirmed mainnet first-funds, NOT a continuously-trading bot. Slot it in as a distinct Executor variant; serialize policy-approved intent to an unsigned tx, sign on-device, broadcast.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Use x402 + EIP-3009 transferWithAuthorization (via CDP facilitator on Base Sepolia) ONLY for the agent paying for its own data/tool dependencies — not for trading
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (execution/ops, EVM payment rail) — supplementary
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: A clean, production payment primitive where each outbound payment is a capped, time-boxed, single-use, fixed-recipient signed authorization mapping 1:1 onto the per-tx cap/rate-limit/allowlist, with the facilitator's verify/settle as an independent second gate and gasless settlement (agent holds no ETH). But honestly scoped: it pays for SERVICES (data feeds, paid MCP tools), it does NOT swap/trade assets, paid-endpoint demand is still thin (CoinDesk 2026), and the Python path is emerging/community. Treat as a nice-to-have ops rail, low priority, not custody-critical.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Keep ERC-7715 wallet_grantPermissions and ERC-7521 validity predicates on a WATCH-LIST, not the critical path
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody — future on-chain enforcement)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Both are the most elegant conceptual fits (7715 = scoped revocable grants mirroring the policy engine; 7521 = encode the full policy as on-chain predicates) but are still DRAFT/dev-build-gated/sparsely-deployed as of 2026 — agents disagree on whether 7715 is even GA. Adopting now means building against changing infra and auditing custom predicate contracts yourself. The lower-risk near-term substitute is the audited ERC-4337 module path above. Revisit when 7715 reaches GA outside dev builds.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Treat self-hosted threshold MPC (cb-mpc / Safeheron / Fireblocks MPC-CMP) and TEE/Nitro+attested-KMS as deliberate LATER upgrades, not initial scope
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody — advanced self-hosted)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: These give the strongest guarantees (agent-can't-sign-alone quorum; hardware-rooted attested isolation) and are the true zero-cloud self-custody endgame, but carry the highest ops burden: native C++/Rust build deps that strain a stdlib-only Python project, DKG/share-backup/refresh orchestration where a mistake means lost or stolen funds, and cb-mpc being an initial public release ('don't trust with mainnet value until independently reviewed'). Nitro+KMS additionally breaks zero-cloud. Overkill until real capital justifies the cost. Informs the custody decision direction but should not gate the validate-before-funds milestone.

themes.consensus:
Across all 10 findings a strong, repeated consensus emerges:

1. Execution quality for this agent is an ARCHITECTURE choice, not a math/latency arms race. Every agent independently concludes that colocation, sub-ms latency, FIX, and ML/deep-learning VWAP are wasted effort at treasury cadence. Crypto execution windows are wide (100-500ms) and a residential home PC suffices. The lever is moving timing/MEV risk OFF the agent, not racing it.

2. Be a MAKER, not a taker. The agent is structurally slow-cadence, so post-only (ALO) limit orders should be the default: they make 'never cross the spread / maker-fees-only' an exchange-enforced invariant (Kraken has 650+ rebate pairs), turning fees from guaranteed drag into zero-or-negative. The tradeoff (non-fill risk) is resolved by a bounded repeg-then-taker-fallback state machine whose caps live in the POLICY engine.

3. Cost must be modeled explicitly as a hard pre-trade POLICY gate, never trusted as gross spread. Multiple agents converge on net-of-cost = fees (Kraken Pro tier-0 ~20bps taker / 10bps maker) + order-book-depth-aware slippage, computed per-intent and shared by Sim/Paper/Live so backtests report honest net-of-fee implementation shortfall. The AgentTrading paper is cited repeatedly as a real validation (~29bps / $1.45 on a $500 BTC fill; its cost-cap caught a real phantom-250bps bug).

4. For the EVM/DeFi path, do NOT build a router. Intent-based execution (CoW Protocol batch auctions, 1inch Fusion/Swap API v6, UniswapX) maps 1:1 onto the agent's existing PROPOSE-intent design: sign an off-chain intent with a signed minimum-out (which becomes the POLICY-enforced limit), solvers bear the speed/MEV race, beating public-mempool routing ~5-30bps on extractable trades. The 'quote-as-policy-input' pattern (call keyless /quote during PROPOSE+POLICY, sign+submit only in EXECUTE) is the safest, stdlib-only insertion point and is independently named by several agents.

5. MEV protection is a transport-layer config knob, not a rewrite. Make the broadcast RPC a config-driven field (mev_mode) on the executor; the policy/flow is untouched.

6. CCXT (or python-kraken-sdk) is the de-facto multi-venue abstraction, but it belongs ONLY in the Executor adapter at the I/O boundary, keeping the agent+policy core stdlib-only and offline-testable via SimExecutor. Kraken's demo environment (demo-futures.kraken.com, sandbox=True) is the agreed validate-before-funds rung between Sim and live.

7. Vendor safety controls (CDP paymaster allowlist/caps, AgentKit spend controls, OKX read-only/demo, exchange trade-only keys) are valued as a SECOND independent circuit-breaker that mirrors the hard-rule policy engine, never as the source of truth.

8. Idempotency/reconciliation is non-negotiable before real funds: anchor every order to a stable client id (CEX cl_ord_id) or EVM nonce so the venue dedupes, wrap in a local SQLite transactional-outbox, model fills as cumulative-filled (not done/not-done), and on any retry RECONCILE before re-submitting — never blind-retry.
themes.contradictions:
1. CEX-first vs DEX-first sequencing. One cluster (CCXT/Kraken agents) argues start on a CEX and DEFER the on-chain/DEX path entirely until proven, because CEX has zero smart-contract/MEV/gas/slashing surface. Another cluster (1inch/CoW/intent agents) treats the EVM/DeFi path as primary and immediately actionable. Resolution: not truly contradictory given the project's roadmap (PaperExecutor=Kraken first, OnchainExecutor later) — CEX-first for the live-money rung, DEX intent-routing wired in parallel but gated behind it.

2. Strict stdlib-only vs the dependencies execution actually requires. Several techniques (CoW EIP-712 signing needs eth_account/web3.py; CCXT; python-kraken-sdk; AgentKit; Hummingbot; OKX kit is Node/TS) all break the stdlib-only ideal. The agents resolve this consistently: confine every dependency to the Executor ADAPTER (I/O boundary) and keep the agent+policy core pure-stdlib — but it is an explicit, unavoidable deviation, not zero-cost.

3. MEV-protection chain coverage. One agent recommends Flashbots Protect / MEV Blocker as the cheapest one-line upgrade; another agent flatly contradicts this for THIS project: Flashbots Protect and MEV Blocker are Ethereum-MAINNET-ONLY and do NOT cover Base, the actual target chain. bloXroute BackRunMe is the only researched option that explicitly supports Base. This is a real, load-bearing correction — the popular tools are the wrong ones here.

4. Exchange-native TWAP (Hyperliquid) vs client-side TWAP. One agent prefers delegating slicing to the venue (thin client); others prefer app-side slicing to stay exchange-agnostic and stdlib-only. Plus Kraken native TWAP availability is explicitly UNCONFIRMED — flagged as a verify-before-relying item.

5. Intent/private-mempool tools trade away raw speed for TRUST assumptions on solvers/builders and introduce a non-fill code path. Fine at treasury cadence, but agents disagree on how much to lean on async solver settlement ('submitted' != 'settled') given a small treasury can't easily monitor it.

top_techniques.name: Post-only (ALO) limit orders as the default order type
top_techniques.why: Highest-leverage, lowest-complexity win for a slow agent. Makes 'maker-fees-only, never cross the spread' an EXCHANGE-enforced invariant the LLM cannot violate; Kraken's 650+ rebate pairs can even pay the agent. Pure config on the intent (OrderType enum + oflags=post). Caveat: guarantees fee side not fill, so must pair with repeg/timeout fallback; rebate pair list changes (Kraken dropped 16 pairs 2026-03-06) so refresh from live endpoint.

top_techniques.name: Net-of-cost spread gate (fees + order-book-depth slippage) as a hard POLICY rule
top_techniques.why: Turns 'execution quality' into a testable veto the policy engine owns, not agent optimism. Gross spreads routinely collapse to ~zero after fees/slippage; the AgentTrading paper proved a real ~29bps fill and that the cost cap caught a phantom-250bps bug. Pure stdlib arithmetic; one cost surface shared by Sim/Paper/Live for honest net-of-fee backtests. Slippage coefficients are heuristic and need per-symbol calibration before mainnet.

top_techniques.name: Intent-based execution + quote-as-policy-input (CoW Protocol / 1inch Fusion)
top_techniques.why: For the EVM path, solvers bear the MEV/speed race (5-30bps edge on extractable trades) and the signed minimum-out IS the policy-enforced limit. The keyless /quote call inserts real microstructure data into PROPOSE+POLICY with no key and no broadcast — safe in sim/paper, stays stdlib-only. CoW additionally removes sandwich MEV and needs no gas/mempool logic. Caveats: EIP-712 signing pulls in one crypto dep; async settlement means submitted != settled; verify Base Sepolia coverage.

top_techniques.name: Transactional-outbox idempotency + reconcile-before-retry (cl_ord_id / EVM nonce)
top_techniques.why: The single biggest correctness hazard is a timeout AFTER the order hit the matching engine. A stable venue-side id (cl_ord_id) or nonce lets the venue dedupe; a local SQLite outbox with cumulative-filled accounting lets the agent recover post-crash.
