Non-negotiable rule: reconcile by querying the venue before re-submitting, never blind-retry. Neither cl_ord_id nor outbox alone gives exactly-once — that's why reconciliation is mandatory.

top_techniques.name: Configurable RPC / mev_mode knob, Base-aware (bloXroute BackRunMe)
top_techniques.why: MEV protection is a one-field transport change, not a rewrite, and keeps the agent vendor-agnostic. Critically corrects a common mistake: Flashbots Protect / MEV Blocker are Ethereum-mainnet-only; bloXroute BackRunMe is the only researched option that covers Base, the target chain. Add a policy rule blocking real-funds mainnet execution unless a chain-compatible protected mev_mode is set. L2 protection is inherently weaker (Base's sequencer already controls ordering).

top_techniques.name: Kraken demo (sandbox=True) + CCXT/python-kraken-sdk confined to the adapter
top_techniques.why: Gives a real-exchange shakedown (latency, partial fills, rate limits, order-status quirks) with zero funds at risk, matching the validate-before-real-funds mandate; going live is a base-URL/flag flip, not a rewrite. CCXT/SDK provide write-once multi-venue plumbing but must live only in the Executor adapter so the core stays stdlib-only and offline-testable. SDKs are unofficial/community — pin versions; confirm spot-paper parity (demo is futures-clearest).

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Make post-only (ALO) limit orders the default order type; add an OrderType enum (MARKET, LIMIT, LIMIT_POST_ONLY) to the proposed intent, with POLICY mandating post_only=True for normal-priority intents and relaxing only under an explicit 'urgent' gate.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Lowest-effort, highest-leverage execution win for a structurally-slow agent: converts fees from guaranteed drag to zero-or-negative and makes 'never cross the spread' exchange-enforced rather than LLM-trusted. Pure intent-field change; no heavy deps. Re-validate rebate-eligible pairs against the live Kraken fee endpoint (16 pairs dropped 2026-03-06), and pair with a fallback so non-fill risk is bounded.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: mechanical/quant — passive maker execution is a deterministic, auditable rule, reinforcing mechanical over LLM-discretionary execution

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a net-of-cost spread gate (taker/maker fee bps + order-book-depth-aware slippage, capped conservatively) as an independent POLICY hard-rule AND the shared fill-cost surface for SimExecutor/PaperExecutor/Live; reject or down-size any intent whose modeled net edge < threshold.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Makes execution quality a testable veto the policy engine owns, gives honest net-of-fee implementation shortfall in backtests, and directly strengthens the existing solvency/per-tx-notional checks. Proven by the AgentTrading reference (~29bps real fill; the cap caught a phantom-250bps bug). Pure stdlib. Calibrate slippage coefficients per-symbol before mainnet tiny-caps; use window-based 24h volume to avoid the phantom-slippage bug.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: a hard cost-model gate keeps risk logic mechanical and in the policy layer, not in the LLM path

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement idempotency as a layered transactional-outbox: write policy-approved intent + pending-execution atomically to a SQLite outbox keyed by intent_id, stamp a venue idempotency anchor (Kraken cl_ord_id for CEX, EVM nonce for on-chain), model fills as cumulative-filled ledger entries, and on any retry/restart RECONCILE by querying the venue before re-submitting.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Closes the most dangerous seam — a timeout/crash AFTER the order reached the venue — which on real funds means double exposure or corrupted solvency/drawdown accounting. Stdlib-only (sqlite3). Critical honesty: neither cl_ord_id nor the outbox gives exactly-once (Kraken enforces cl_ord_id uniqueness only across OPEN orders; outbox is at-least-once), so reconcile-before-retry is mandatory and effects must dedupe on intent_id.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody/ops: correct fill reconciliation is the prerequisite for any executor (managed-MPC or self-hosted) to be trusted with funds

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Build PaperExecutor against Kraken's demo environment (sandbox=True -> demo-futures.kraken.com) via python-kraken-sdk or CCXT, confined strictly to the Executor adapter; subscribe to the executions WebSocket to feed real fills into the drawdown/solvency checks. Going live = flip sandbox/base-URL.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Provides a real-exchange shakedown (latency, partial fills, rate limits) with zero funds at risk, matching the validate-before-real-funds mandate, and promotes to live as a config flip not a rewrite. Keeping CCXT/SDK only in the adapter preserves the stdlib-only, offline-testable core. SDKs are unofficial — pin versions; demo is futures-clearest, so confirm spot-paper parity for the actual symbols. Also set exchange API keys TRADE-ONLY with withdrawals DISABLED so 'funds cannot leave' is venue-enforced.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: validates the CEX (Kraken) path as the first live-money rung

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: For the on-chain leg, use the quote-as-policy-input + intent-based execution pattern: call the keyless CoW/1inch /quote during PROPOSE+POLICY (feeds notional/slippage/solvency, no key, no broadcast), then in EXECUTE sign an off-chain EIP-712 intent with the policy-approved minimum-out as the hard limit; treat 'submitted' != 'settled' with a non-fill reconciliation path.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Best fit for the EVM/DeFi path: solvers bear the MEV/speed race (5-30bps edge on extractable trades), CoW structurally removes sandwich MEV and needs no gas/mempool logic, and the signed min-out maps 1:1 onto the policy's slippage/notional caps. /quote is keyless read-only so it stays stdlib-only and safe to exercise in sim/paper. Caveats: EIP-712 signing pulls in one crypto dep (eth_account); verify Base Sepolia coverage before wiring; cow-py is grant-maintained (audit before depending).
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: makes the EVM/DeFi path viable and safe without building a router

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Make the broadcast RPC a config-driven mev_mode field on the on-chain executor, and add a POLICY hard-rule blocking real-funds mainnet execution unless a chain-COMPATIBLE protected mev_mode is set. For Base, use bloXroute BackRunMe (not Flashbots/MEV Blocker, which are Ethereum-mainnet-only). Reconcile asynchronous backrun rebates in the ledger.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: MEV protection is a one-field transport change that slots into the existing executor seam with no flow change. The load-bearing correction: the popular tools (Flashbots Protect, MEV Blocker) do NOT cover Base — the project's actual target chain — so bloXroute is the only researched Base option. L2 protection is inherently weaker (Base's sequencer already controls ordering), so validate non-visibility in the public Base mempool on testnet first and don't over-trust rebate magnitudes.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: only relevant if/when execution goes on-chain on Base

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Resolve post-only's non-fill risk with a bounded repeg-then-taker-fallback ExecutionStrategy layer: post passive post-only at the touch, wait a bounded timeout, repeg toward price up to a capped count, cross with a marketable order only as last resort — with max_repegs, max_chase_bps and allow_taker_fallback owned as hard POLICY rules.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Converts 'chase forever' into a deterministic, auditable state machine that keeps the maker-rebate default while bounding non-fill, and ensures chasing can never breach drawdown/slippage/solvency limits. Stdlib-implementable atop existing place+cancel. Honest caveat: repeg parameters aren't standardized in public docs and passive fills suffer adverse selection (a fill often means the move already went against you) — tune and validate in sim/paper before real funds.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: a deterministic execution state machine keeps execution mechanical and policy-governed

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: For the planned Coinbase OnchainExecutor, use ERC-4337 paymaster (CDP/AgentKit) gasless rail on Base so the agent never custodies ETH, mirror CDP sponsorship policies (contract/function allowlist + per-user + global cap) as a second infra-enforced circuit-breaker, make the cost estimator L2-aware (L1 data fee is 90-99% of cost; pad preVerificationGas ~25%), and pre-flight-simulate every UserOp before broadcast.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Base Sepolia gives unlimited free sponsorship = ideal validate-before-funds proving ground; the paymaster allowlist/caps form a second independent guardrail aligned with the hard-rule engine; L2-aware costing keeps the solvency/notional gate economically correct on Base; pre-flight simulation prevents failed sponsored ops from burning budget (the on-chain analogue of SimExecutor). Honest tradeoff: CDP/AgentKit gas+MPC custody live on Coinbase infra — this is the MANAGED-MPC option, not self-hosted, conflicting with the zero-cloud preference.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody: this is the managed-MPC path; informs the managed-MPC vs self-hosted smart-account choice

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Emit a per-parent-order ExecutionReport (arrival price, per-child fills, interval VWAP, realized implementation shortfall) into the existing log/SQLite store as ongoing TCA, and feed a rolling slippage stat back as a POLICY input (widen rate-limit / trip kill-switch on threshold breach) and as the SimExecutor backtest acceptance gate.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Closes the validate-before-real-funds loop the project demands: with stdlib csv/sqlite you can PROVE a strategy beats naive market orders on paper fills before mainnet, and surface execution drift like the drawdown breaker surfaces P&L drift. Cheap and pure-Python. Honest caveat: it's a measurement layer — it only helps if you act on the numbers; log raw fills so thin-book VWAP distortions can be recomputed.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: makes execution-quality an observable that gates promotion of any strategy

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: For larger rebalances, slice into evenly-spaced (jittered) post-only child orders client-side (app-side TWAP) so each child flows through the policy engine; defer exchange-native TWAP (Hyperliquid) and skip ML/deep-learning VWAP entirely. Graduate to a closed-form Almgren-Chriss implementation-shortfall schedule (single risk-aversion lambda knob) only AFTER plain TWAP is proven.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: App-side slicing stays stdlib-only and exchange-agnostic, naturally fits per-tx-notional cap + rate-limit (each child is small), and is abortable mid-schedule by the kill-switch. ML VWAP is hype at this scale (the cited paper ignores market impact and needs data/retraining infra). AC is a principled upgrade with one auditable knob but assumes linear impact and needs noisy regime-dependent calibration — a Tier-3 nicety, not a near-term need. Kraken native TWAP availability is UNCONFIRMED; verify before relying.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy: keeps execution mechanical/stdlib and explicitly rejects speculative ML execution

themes.consensus:
Across all 10 agents a clear, convergent picture emerges with very little disagreement:

1) ONE deterministic event-driven loop, reused everywhere. Nearly every agent independently lands on the same architectural keystone: drive agent->policy->executor from a single chronological event queue (one bar/tick at a time, e.g. collections.deque + a generator) so that (a) look-ahead bias becomes a STRUCTURAL impossibility rather than a discipline, and (b) SimExecutor and the future PaperExecutor/OnchainExecutor share identical decision logic ("backtest-to-live parity"). This is explicitly framed as a perfect match for treasuryforge's existing PROPOSE->dispose->EXECUTE shape. NautilusTrader is repeatedly cited as the reference implementation of this pattern, but the unanimous verdict is to BORROW THE PATTERN, not the library (its Rust core / pyo3 / LGPL violate the stdlib-only, local-first constraint).

2) Point-in-time data + signal-to-fill timing gap. Strong consensus that the single most common look-ahead bug is signal-on-close / fill-on-same-close. The fix is universal: gate the agent's inputs to knowable_at <= t, then fill at t+1 (next bar open / next tick) plus a small latency delay (~100-200ms). Several agents stress this is the difference between a backtest that is merely reproducible and one that is reproducibly CORRECT.

3) Realistic cost model replacing flat slippage. Overwhelming agreement that frictionless or flat-percent fills are the core failure mode (a 1.5-Sharpe frictionless strategy can collapse below 0.5 once impact is modeled). The recommended replacement is a decomposed, pure-arithmetic cost function: bid-ask spread + square-root market-impact (Impact = eta * sigma * sqrt(Q/V), pi/eta ~= 0.1 as a STARTING PRIOR, not truth) + explicit maker/taker fees + (for perps) funding carry + (for DeFi) deterministic x*y=k price impact + gas + an MEV haircut. All of this is stdlib-implementable. Crucially, every agent that mentions the impact coefficient flags it as venue/symbol-specific and demanding recalibration against real Kraken-paper / Base-Sepolia fills.

4) Overfitting-aware significance gates before any real funds. Multiple agents independently converge on the Lopez de Prado / Bailey toolkit as the promotion gate from Sim->Paper->Onchain: Deflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR>=0.95), Probability of Backtest Overfitting via CSCV (PBO<0.5), and Walk-Forward Analysis (anchored AND rolling). The shared insight: a solo builder sweeping many configs will inevitably overfit, so the raw best Sharpe is almost always a fluke; these convert "how many did I try (N)" into a hard go/no-go. They also agree the closed-forms are reimplementable in ~100-300 lines of stdlib (math.erf for the normal CDF, itertools.combinations for CSCV) and that the Hudson & Thames mlfinlab / pypbo dependencies should be avoided in-runtime (license/footprint) and kept to an offline dev venv if used at all.

5) Cheap stdlib stress tests calibrate the EXISTING policy engine. Strong consensus that Monte Carlo trade-resampling and block/stationary bootstrap (plus curated historical flash-crash replay) are the highest-value, lowest-cost robustness layer because the SimExecutor already emits the trade ledger they consume, they need only random/statistics, and their 95th/99th-pct drawdown output directly calibrates the drawdown circuit-breaker threshold and per-tx notional cap from a distribution of bad luck rather than one lucky path.

6) Determinism/reproducibility as a first-class artifact. Agreement on centralized seed control (random + numpy + any LLM sampling) plus a per-run JSON manifest that content-hashes the input data snapshot (hashlib.sha256), config, seed, and fee/slippage model -- essential audit evidence before tiny-cap mainnet and fully offline/local-first.

7) Free, real validation rungs before mainnet. Consensus on the ladder: Kraken demo-futures sandbox for the PaperExecutor (schema-identical to prod, sandbox=True flag) and Base Sepolia testnet (with faucets) for the OnchainExecutor, with the POLICY engine disposing intents IDENTICALLY across all rungs so only the base-URL/network changes.
themes.contradictions:
Genuine disagreements are few; most apparent tension is sequencing/scope nuance, not conflict:

1) Vectorized vs event-driven backtesting. One agent promotes VectorBT for fast parameter sweeps; others warn vectorized models can MASK look-ahead bias and path-dependent execution. Resolution is consensus, not conflict: use vectorized ONLY as a research-tier funnel ABOVE the execution interface to screen N parameterizations cheaply, then validate survivors in the event-driven, policy-gated engine. Vectorized results must never be trusted as execution-faithful and never touch the live path.

2) Maturity of PBO/CSCV. Agents split on labeling: some call DSR/PBO "production", one calls PBO "emerging". This reflects implementation risk (you must port and unit-test against the paper's worked example) rather than disagreement about validity -- all agree it is decades-validated methodology with research-grade (not turnkey) tooling.

3) When CPCV/purged-embargoed CV is needed. One agent treats CPCV as a core gate; another scopes it strictly to the LLM/ML-discretionary path (multi-bar outcome labels) and says walk-forward ALONE suffices for a purely mechanical single-bar strategy. This is a real scoping divergence that maps onto Phase-2 strategy decision #2: CPCV's cost is only justified if you pick a labeled-ML/LLM strategy.

4) How much to model market reactivity. Heavyweight interactive-order-book sims (ABIDES/PyMarketSim, NautilusTrader L2/L3 FillModel) are praised as the only way to capture the market reacting to the agent's own orders, but EVERY agent flags them as equities/NASDAQ-shaped, non-stdlib, hard to calibrate to crypto, and research/heavy. The honest read: these are optional, out-of-core, late-stage validation rigs -- valuable but easy to over-invest in for a solo builder; their absolute numbers are "directional, not ground truth."

5) Sortino/Calmar as ranking metrics. One agent surfaces evidence that Sortino/Calmar had weak in-sample-to-out-of-sample predictivity, so they must be descriptive color only, NOT the promotion gate -- a useful corrective against over-trusting headline risk-adjusted ratios.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Refactor SimExecutor + agent loop as a single chronological event-driven queue (deque + generator), reused unchanged by future Paper/Onchain executors
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Highest-leverage, near-unanimous recommendation. Makes look-ahead structurally impossible, makes backtests bit-reproducible, and guarantees backtest-to-live parity so the policy engine disposes intents identically across Sim/Paper/Onchain. Zero new dependencies; maps 1:1 onto the existing propose->dispose->execute chain. Borrow NautilusTrader's pattern, NOT its Rust/LGPL core.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Reinforces execution-interface design underpinning all three Phase-2 decisions (chain/exchange, strategy, custody) by keeping decision logic venue/custody-agnostic

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Enforce point-in-time inputs (knowable_at<=t) with fill-at-t+1 (next bar open / next tick) plus a configurable ~100-200ms latency delay in SimExecutor
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Kills the most common and most capital-destructive backtest bug (signal-on-close/fill-on-same-close). Small, unit-testable change that directly de-risks the Sim->Paper->Onchain transition by making the simulated decision/execution timeline match live reality. Caveat: free crypto OHLCV feeds may already be revised, so flag knowable_at as estimated.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (cross-cutting correctness prerequisite)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Replace flat slippage with a decomposed stdlib cost function: spread + sqrt impact (eta*sigma*sqrt(Q/V), eta~0.1 prior) + maker/taker fees + funding carry; add deterministic x*y=k impact + gas + MEV haircut on the DeFi path
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Frictionless/flat-percent fills are the core failure mode -- impact can halve a strategy's Sharpe. Pure arithmetic, no dependencies, sized by order-notional-vs-volume the policy engine already tracks; forces solvency/notional checks to face realistic cost. Funding is the single most-ignored crypto cost. Treat eta, MEV haircut and funding as priors to recalibrate against real Kraken-paper / Base-Sepolia fills.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Strategy (#2): exposes whether an edge survives real costs; Chain/exchange (#1): CEX maker/taker+funding vs DeFi x*y=k+gas+MEV are modeled distinctly, informing the venue choice

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a stdlib overfitting promotion gate: Deflated/Probabilistic Sharpe (DSR>=0.95) + PBO via CSCV (<0.5) + Walk-Forward (anchored AND rolling), with honest trial-count N logged in the run manifest
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The rigorous, citable Sim->Paper->Onchain gate that defeats the inevitable solo-builder selection bias from sweeping many configs. Directly answers Phase-2 strategy choice with evidence, not a raw Sharpe. Reimplementable in ~100-300 lines stdlib (math.erf normal CDF, itertools.combinations for CSCV); keep pypbo/mlfinlab to an offline dev venv only. Trap: understating N or short crypto history silently restores overfitting -- pass it AND require paper confirmation.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Strategy (#2): the hard quantitative gate that validates mechanical/quant vs LLM-discretionary before real funds

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Build a montecarlo.py + bootstrap.py stress module consuming the SimExecutor ledger (trade-resampling + block/stationary bootstrap) and a fixtures/ flash-crash replay library; feed 95th/99th-pct drawdown into the policy circuit-breaker and notional cap
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Cheapest, highest-value robustness layer for a solo builder -- pure stdlib (random/statistics), reuses the ledger that already exists, and calibrates the EXISTING policy thresholds from a distribution of bad luck rather than one lucky path. Block/stationary bootstrap preserves crypto autocorrelation that naive shuffling destroys (use block length ~sqrt(n), report across several lengths). Flash-crash fixtures verify the kill-switch actually fires. Treat all as a floor, never a ceiling.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (calibrates existing policy engine; cross-cutting safety)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Emit a per-run JSON manifest (hashlib.sha256 over the input data snapshot + config + seed + fee/slippage model) and centralize one seed across random/numpy/any LLM sampling
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Makes every backtest provably replayable and detects backtest-vs-live drift on mutable 24/7 crypto data -- the audit evidence needed before tiny-cap mainnet. Fully stdlib (hashlib/json), offline. Caveat: seeding gives statistical not always bitwise reproducibility (FP/hardware drift, possible LLM non-determinism at temperature 0), so define an explicit numeric tolerance. DVC is an optional heavier upgrade only if hashed manifests stop scaling.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Strategy (#2): required to make an LLM-discretionary strategy reproducible/auditable at all

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Wire PaperExecutor to Kraken demo-futures sandbox (sandbox=True / base-URL toggle) and OnchainExecutor to Base Sepolia testnet, with vendor-neutral faucets (Alchemy/QuickNode/Chainlink) kept as an option alongside AgentKit/CDP's built-in faucet
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Exercises the real exchange/chain API surface (orders, rejects, rate-limits, signing) with zero funds while the policy engine disposes intents identically. Honest caveats that bear directly on Phase-2: Kraken demo is FUTURES-only/self-serve while spot API test access is gated behind manual onboarding (affects CEX feasibility), and AgentKit/CDP's faucet+managed-wallet quietly pre-commits the custody question to managed-MPC -- use vendor-neutral faucets to keep self-hosted smart-account custody open.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Chain/exchange (#1) via Kraken-vs-Base validation surfaces; Custody (#3) because AgentKit/CDP faucet couples to managed-MPC whereas vendor-neutral faucets keep self-hosted custody open

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a tail-risk metrics panel (historical CVaR/Expected Shortfall, max drawdown, Ulcer/Martin Index, Calmar) that may only TIGHTEN the policy engine's hard limits; keep Sortino/Calmar as descriptive color, not the gate
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Crypto returns are fat-tailed, so volatility-based Sharpe understates crash risk. These distribution-agnostic downside metrics calibrate the circuit-breaker and notional cap from data. Critical discipline: a benign backtest may never LOOSEN kill-switch/drawdown limits (historical CVaR cannot see a tail larger than the worst sampled event); add a conservative safety margin. Evidence shows Sortino/Calmar predict OOS weakly -- use them for reporting, not promotion.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (calibrates existing policy engine)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Use a vectorized backtester (open-source VectorBT) strictly as an offline research-tier parameter-screening funnel ABOVE the execution interface; promote only survivors into the event-driven, policy-gated engine
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Useful for cheaply mass-screening mechanical/quant parameters, but vectorized models MASK look-ahead and path-dependent execution and have no backtest-to-live parity. Must never touch the policy/executor/live path and its results must not be trusted as execution-faithful. Non-stdlib (pandas/numpy) so it lives outside core; confirm the open-source edition suffices (full tier is commercial). Only worthwhile if the chosen strategy has a large parameter grid.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Strategy (#2): only relevant for mechanical/quant param sweeps, not LLM-discretionary

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Defer heavyweight interactive-order-book sims (ABIDES/PyMarketSim, NautilusTrader L2/L3 FillModel) to a late-stage, opt-in, out-of-core validation rig before mainnet tiny-caps
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: They are the only tools that let the market REACT to the agent's own orders (endogenous impact replay cannot capture), which matters before mainnet. But every agent flags them as equities/NASDAQ-shaped, non-stdlib (heavy Rust/numpy/pandas), hard to calibrate to crypto, and research/heavy -- easy for a solo builder to over-invest in. Their absolute numbers are directional, not ground truth. Use only to sanity-check the stdlib square-root model late, never in the core loop.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (late-stage execution-realism validation)

themes.consensus:
Across all 10 research agents a single architecture emerges with striking agreement: for a local-first, stdlib-only treasury agent, market data should be a PLUGGABLE ADAPTER behind the existing MarketTick/ticks() seam, and DATA-QUALITY FAILURES should be promoted to first-class HARD-RULE inputs to the policy engine — exactly mirroring the kill-switch/solvency/drawdown disposers already built. Five points recur independently:

1. Match the feed to the chosen execution venue. CEX branch -> Kraken public REST (keyless, stdlib-only, same venue as planned PaperExecutor); DeFi/Base branch -> Pyth Hermes pull-oracle (lives where the OnchainExecutor will run). This keeps the price the agent reasons over identical to the price it executes against, eliminating cross-venue basis risk. (Agents 1, 2, 3, 7, 10)

2. Treat every feed as UNTRUSTED INPUT and fail closed. Staleness/freshness guards, cross-source deviation checks, and confidence-interval gates should HALT trading rather than trade on phantom prices — the half-open frozen socket is named the single most dangerous failure for an unattended agent. (Agents 3, 4, 5, 8 — near-unanimous)

3. Start keyless and stdlib-pure, graduate deliberately. Kraken/Binance public REST + the Fear & Greed index need only urllib+json; CCXT, web3.py, The Graph, oracles, and aggregators all add third-party deps and/or keys and must stay OPTIONAL behind the adapter interface. None of the "real" options are truly stdlib-pure once you go beyond keyless REST. (Agents 1, 2, 6, 7, 9, 10)

4. One feature definition, point-in-time correct. The strongest "validated before funds" lever is not a product but a discipline: compute each feature ONCE as a pure function both the live loop and the backtest call (kills training-serving skew), enforce no-look-ahead, and pass a SINGLE immutable market+wallet snapshot into both PROPOSE and the policy DISPOSE checks so the agent and the safety rules see identical numbers. (Agent 6, reinforced by 5)

5. Backtest on real candles before any paper/live run, and respect survivorship bias. Free Binance public-data and Kraken OHLCVT ZIP archives (urllib+zipfile+csv+hashlib, zero deps) are the canonical offline corpus; keep the symbol allowlist small/conservative because >58% of listed tokens are "dead" and survivorship bias inflates backtest returns. (Agent 9)

Consensus mechanism to add to the policy engine: a "data_unhealthy" / "feed_stale" disposition built from (a) a staleness budget, (b) structural/sequence/checksum integrity checks, (c) a robust local outlier filter, and (d) cross-source median + confidence gating — any of which blocks PROPOSE/EXECUTE.
themes.contradictions:
Real tensions surfaced, not just emphasis differences:

1. stdlib-only vs capability. The hardest contradiction with the project's own constraint: every genuinely powerful option (CCXT, web3.py, The Graph client, Chainlink-web3, dune-client, Bytewax, FinBERT/torch, LunarCrush key) breaks stdlib-only or zero-key/zero-cloud. Agents resolve it the same way — keep them OPTIONAL behind the adapter, default to keyless stdlib REST — but it is a genuine architectural concession, not a free lunch.

2. WebSocket vs REST polling. Agents 4 and 8 partly disagree. Agent 4 pushes a full hybrid REST-snapshot + WS-delta with sequence/CRC32 integrity as the "2026 consensus answer." Agent 8 counters that for a LOW-FREQUENCY treasury agent the latency gain is uncertain and a strict staleness budget over plain stdlib REST polling is the pragmatic starting point, deferring WS and L2-depth machinery until the strategy actually needs sub-second quotes or order-book depth. Honest read: Agent 8 is right for v1 (rebalancing cadence), Agent 4 is right later. Notably stdlib has NO mature WebSocket client — this is the one place pure-stdlib is genuinely weak (you hand-roll RFC6455 or accept a dep).

3. Pyth confidence interval: safety primitive vs over-trusted hint. Agents 3 and 5 both love confidence/staleness gating, but Agent 5 flags it as "emerging" and cites real audits where protocols MIS-handled Pyth confidence — publisher-reported, not a guaranteed worst case. So confidence gating is high-value but must itself be sanity-checked, and Pyth Hermes requires bearer-token auth from 31 Jul 2026 (breaks zero-key purity on the DeFi path).

4. Cross-check independence is partly illusory on the CEX path. Agent 3/5's dual-oracle (Pyth vs Chainlink) cross-check assumes an EVM/RPC dependency that does not exist on the pure-CEX/off-chain branch, so the "second source" degrades to another off-chain quote that may share an upstream (correlated failure) — weaker than advertised until the OnchainExecutor phase. Agent 5 also notes you need 3+ sources to truly outvote one bad feed; with 2 you can only detect disagreement.

5. Destructive cleaning is discouraged. Agent 5's most production-grade source (Databento) argues AGAINST scrubbing data at the ingestion layer — flag, don't drop. This mildly contradicts naive "clean the feed" instincts: anomaly handling belongs in agent/policy logic as gates, not silent edits.

top_techniques.name: Staleness budget enforced as a hard policy rule
top_techniques.why: The single highest-leverage, lowest-effort change agreed across agents 3/4/5/8: stamp each quote with ingestion time, reject any intent built on data older than staleness_budget_seconds. Pure stdlib arithmetic, fully unit-testable, no WebSocket, slots straight into the existing dispose() alongside notional cap and drawdown. Converts the deadliest failure (frozen-but-open feed) into a clean fail-closed halt.
top_techniques.maturity: production (the rule); emerging only in threshold-tuning

top_techniques.name: Keyless stdlib REST feed matched to the execution venue (Kraken public REST for CEX; Binance fapi for funding/OI)
top_techniques.why: Real prices with zero credentials, zero SDK, zero new dependency — perfectly consistent with stdlib-only/local-first, and the SAME venue as the planned Kraken PaperExecutor so no cross-venue basis risk during validation. urllib.request + json only.

top_techniques.name: Pyth Hermes pull-oracle over plain REST/SSE (DeFi/Base path)
top_techniques.why: Lets a stdlib agent value its book with urllib (no web3, no node, no gas) and ships a confidence interval + publish_time that map directly onto the hard-rule policy engine. It is also the price the on-chain OnchainExecutor will use, keeping reasoning and execution coherent.
top_techniques.maturity: production (note: bearer-token auth mandatory from 31 Jul 2026)

top_techniques.name: Data-integrity gate: confidence + structural + robust-outlier + cross-source median, all FLAG-don't-drop, feeding a data_unhealthy disposer
top_techniques.why: The convergent safety idea from agents 3/4/5: value owned assets at the conservative confidence bound, reject wide-confidence/stale/crossed/sequence-broken ticks, run a stdlib rolling-median/MAD (Hampel) outlier filter, and require N-of-M cross-source agreement. Each becomes a deterministic veto in the existing 'policy disposes' stage. Databento's flag-don't-drop discipline preserves an auditable trail.

top_techniques.name: Single feature definition + point-in-time/no-look-ahead audit + one immutable snapshot into PROPOSE and DISPOSE
top_techniques.why: Makes the 'validated before real funds' promise actually hold. One pure compute_features() function both live loop and backtest call (kills training-serving skew by construction); a Freqtrade-style sliced-dataframe audit catches lookahead bias; passing ONE snapshot to both proposer and policy guarantees the agent and the kill-switch/solvency see identical numbers. Pure discipline, no new deps.

top_techniques.name: Free static archive backtest corpus (data.binance.vision + Kraken OHLCVT ZIP), survivorship-aware
top_techniques.why: Zero-key, zero-dep offline replay (urllib+zipfile+csv+hashlib with SHA256 verification) to validate the drawdown circuit-breaker and notional cap over years of real candles before any paper/live run. Kraken's archive matches the paper venue. Keep the allowlist small because survivorship bias is severe.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add staleness_budget_seconds as a hard-rule dispose() parameter; reject any intent whose source quote age exceeds it, wired to the same kill-switch/circuit-breaker disposition path
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none directly (cross-cutting safety; applies under any chain/exchange and strategy choice)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Highest leverage / lowest effort in the entire corpus. Pure stdlib arithmetic, fully unit-testable in SimExecutor, no WebSocket, no dependency. Neutralizes the deadliest unattended-agent failure (a half-open socket serving a frozen price). Christian's own home-IP/residential-connection history makes silent staleness a concrete risk; log observed quote ages first, then set the threshold. Ship this before anything else.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement RealMarketFeed as a keyless Kraken public REST adapter (urllib+json) emitting MarketTick on the same ticks() iterator as MarketSimulator
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange) — favors/validates the CEX branch
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Lets the agent trade on REAL prices with zero new dependency and zero credentials, preserving stdlib-only/local-first, and uses the SAME venue as the planned Kraken PaperExecutor so there is no price-basis gap between validation and paper trading. This is the natural first real feed and a low-risk way to de-risk the CEX option in Decision 1 before committing.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a data-integrity gate (structural/crossed-quote/sequence checks + stdlib rolling-median/MAD Hampel outlier filter + cross-source median with deviation band), FLAG-don't-drop, exposed as a data_unhealthy hard-rule disposer
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none directly (safety overlay for all branches)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Maps cleanly onto 'agent proposes, policy disposes': a single data_unhealthy signal joins kill-switch and solvency as a hard veto. The robust outlier filter (statistics.median + deque, peer-reviewed Hampel/Brownlees-Gallo) is pure stdlib; cross-source median needs the keyless CoinGecko/second-quote adapter. Databento's flag-don't-drop preserves an audit trail matching the verify-before-funds methodology. Caveat: true single-bad-feed tolerance needs 3+ sources; with 2 you only detect disagreement.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Adopt the single-feature-definition + point-in-time/no-look-ahead discipline; pass ONE immutable market+wallet snapshot into both PROPOSE and the policy DISPOSE checks; add a Freqtrade-style lookahead audit to the existing test suite
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy) — essential for the mechanical/quant branch; protects any strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Without this, the 'validated before funds' guarantee is hollow: leaked features make a strategy look profitable in SimExecutor then evaporate live. Zero new dependencies — it is a discipline + small refactor (one compute_features() pure function both live and backtest call, one snapshot shared by proposer and policy). Directly strengthens whichever strategy is chosen in Decision 2 and is a prerequisite before trusting any mechanical signal with paper funds.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Build a stdlib backtest corpus loader from data.binance.vision + Kraken OHLCVT ZIPs (urllib+zipfile+csv+hashlib, SHA256-verified), keep the symbol allowlist small/conservative (BTC/ETH + few majors), enforce no-look-ahead loading
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy) — provides the offline validation fuel before paper/live
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Zero-key, zero-dependency offline replay to validate the drawdown circuit-breaker and per-tx notional cap over years of real candles before any PaperExecutor run. Kraken's archive matches the planned paper venue. Must handle gaps as no-trade (not forward-fill) and the 2025-01-01 Binance microsecond timestamp change, or volatility/drawdown stats corrupt. Survivorship bias is severe (>58% dead tokens) — hence the conservative allowlist.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: On the DeFi/Base branch, use a PythPriceFeed (Hermes REST/SSE over urllib) for valuation, and gate on its confidence interval + publish_time as policy primitives (value owned assets at the lower bound; reject wide-confidence/stale quotes)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange) — only relevant if the EVM/DeFi/Base path is chosen
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Keeps the agent's mark identical to the on-chain price the OnchainExecutor uses, with no web3/node/gas for the off-chain read, and turns the oracle's native confidence/staleness fields into deterministic circuit-breakers. Conditional on Decision 1 picking DeFi. Honest caveats: Pyth confidence is publisher-reported (sanity-check it, audits show it mis-handled), and Hermes requires bearer-token auth from 31 Jul 2026 — breaks zero-key purity. Prototype the Hermes read before committing.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a feed-health watchdog (last-message timestamp + heartbeat/ping-pong miss detection) with exponential-backoff-with-jitter reconnect and resubscribe-then-reconcile, wired to the data_unhealthy disposer; defer full WebSocket + sequence/CRC32 L2 machinery until the strategy needs sub-second quotes/depth
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none directly (resilience overlay)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: For a low-frequency treasury agent, Agent 8's pragmatic read wins: a strict staleness budget over stdlib REST polling is the right v1, and the watchdog handles flaky residential connectivity without a hand-rolled RFC6455 WebSocket. The heavier hybrid (WS delta + REST snapshot + sequence/CRC32 integrity) is real and production-grade but only justified later — building L2-depth resync prematurely is wasted effort for quote-only rebalancing. Deterministically-seeded jitter keeps it replayable under SimExecutor.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add free venue-native funding-rate/open-interest feeds (Binance fapi premiumIndex/fundingRate/openInterestHist; Hyperliquid POST /info on the DeFi path) as a mechanical carry/funding-arb signal on the PROPOSE side only
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy) — enables a concrete mechanical/quant strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Zero-key, plain-JSON, stdlib-friendly signal that gives Decision 2's mechanical branch an actual edge (funding carry) rather than just price momentum, with data identical to a future execution venue. Stays advisory to the proposer; the hard-rule policy engine remains sole disposer. Schemas/rate-limits are per-venue and must be hand-normalized; no single endpoint gives a cross-venue aggregate for free.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add the keyless Crypto Fear & Greed Index (alternative.me, urllib+json) as a macro regime feature on the PROPOSE side, optionally doubling as a defensive input that tightens the notional cap in Extreme Greed; cache last-good and degrade safely
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy) — a cheap regime overlay, most useful for an LLM-discretionary variant
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The one sentiment feed that costs nothing and adds no dependency, giving a contrarian macro signal. Genuinely useful but LOW impact: coarse (daily), single-vendor, no SLA, opaque BTC-centric methodology — never a standalone trigger. Heavier sentiment options (LunarCrush key+cost, local FinBERT torch dependency) are hype-adjacent for a stdlib-only solo build and should be deferred; FinBERT is also trained on Reuters English, not crypto slang, so it mislabels crypto-Twitter.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Treat CCXT, web3.py/The Graph/Chainlink-web3, dune-client, CoinGlass/CoinAPI aggregators, Bytewax, and LunarCrush/FinBERT as OPTIONAL adapters behind the feed/executor interface — never the stdlib default
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange) — these become relevant only as the venue/scale is chosen
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Each is genuinely powerful (CCXT = unified multi-venue + execution; web3.py = on-chain spine; Dune = offline research/ops; aggregators = cross-venue funding/liquidation context) but each breaks stdlib-only and/or zero-key/zero-cloud. The agreed resolution across agents: gate them behind the existing adapter seam so SimExecutor + keyless stdlib REST stay the default, and only adopt the specific one the chosen Phase-2 venue/scale demands. Prevents premature dependency sprawl while keeping a clean upgrade path.

themes.theme: Monitoring, Observability & Ops for an autonomous, local-first, stdlib-only crypto treasury agent
themes.consensus:
Across all 10 agents a single architecture emerges with near-unanimity: reliability and observability must be built as STACKED, INDEPENDENT layers, each catching what the layer below cannot, and every layer must feed the EXISTING propose->policy->execute design rather than bypass it. The repeated, load-bearing agreements are:

1. THREE INDEPENDENT LIVENESS LAYERS (cited by 4 of 10 agents almost verbatim): (a) an OS service supervisor (NSSM on Windows / systemd on Linux) for auto-start-on-boot + crash-restart with backoff/throttling so a crash loop can't hammer the exchange or burn rate-limit; (b) an in-process liveness watchdog (systemd sd_notify WATCHDOG=1, or a stdlib daemon-thread last-tick check on Windows) to catch the SILENT FREEZE where the process is alive but no longer enforcing policy; (c) an EXTERNAL, off-box dead-man's-switch heartbeat (Healthchecks.io ping model, self-hostable on the user's existing Netcup/Oracle VPS) to catch total host loss. Healthchecks.io specifically was independently recommended by 4 separate agents.

2. AUDIT LOG = APPEND-ONLY HASH-CHAINED JSONL, STDLIB-ONLY (cited by 3 agents): write each PROPOSE->DISPOSE->EXECUTE record as one canonical-JSON line sealed with an HMAC-SHA256 hash chain (hashlib+hmac, zero deps) so tampering is forgery-resistant, not merely detectable. Add provenance fields (session->decision->executor->authorized-limit) so a losing fill can be walked straight back to the strategy decision and the policy authority that allowed it.

3. STATISTICAL ANOMALY DETECTION MUST BE ADVISORY, NOT AUTO-EXECUTING, AND STDLIB-CHEAP (cited by 3 agents): EWMA / running z-score (Welford) control charts on the agent's own per-tick streams (equity, drawdown, fill notional, fee, denial rate) give a behavioral baseline in a few lines with no ML deps. For strategy-degradation specifically, a pure-Python Page-Hinkley/CUSUM on the live return stream is the early, auto-halting gate.

4. CRITICAL UNIVERSAL RULE (every agent that touched restart/recovery): NO auto-restart, fallback, or replay may bypass the PolicyEngine or silently un-trip a latched breaker. Restart MUST be paired with idempotent startup reconciliation that re-runs solvency/drawdown/kill-switch BEFORE resuming intents, and the latched _tripped/_starting_equity state must be journaled so it survives a kill.

5. SEVERITY-TIERED, ZERO-CLOUD ALERTING: a self-hosted ntfy push channel (single stdlib urllib POST), driven by severity tiers so kill-switch/drawdown trips page you while routine PnL stays quiet, plus a written blameless runbook whose 'block' step IS the existing kill-switch.

6. CALIBRATE ON SIM/PAPER FIRST: every threshold (z, CUSUM lambda, watchdog timeout, staleness max-age, PSR cutoff) must be tuned on the user's own paper-trading history because crypto's fat tails make naive fixed thresholds whipsaw. Validate entirely on SimExecutor/PaperExecutor before any real funds.
themes.contradictions:
1. STDLIB-PURITY vs RICHER TOOLING. The sharpest tension. Several agents push stdlib-only solutions (urllib heartbeat, hashlib hash-chain, Welford z-score, hand-rolled Page-Hinkley, OTel attribute NAMES used as a JSON schema without the SDK). Others recommend real dependencies/services: River drift detectors (ADWIN/KSWIN), MLflow Tracing (SQLite server), self-hosted Langfuse (ClickHouse+Docker), Arize Phoenix, Dokku/Kamal PaaS. The honest reconciliation the agents themselves converge on: keep the trading hot path and audit core 100% stdlib; treat ANY server/dependency as an OPTIONAL, swappable ops sidecar outside the hot path, never a core requirement.

2. ZERO-CLOUD vs ON-CHAIN/SAAS MONITORING. The dead-man's-switch agents say 'self-host off-box to stay zero-cloud,' while the on-chain-safety agent recommends Tenderly/Forta wallet webhooks which are explicitly hosted SaaS and reactive (fire AFTER the tx). Resolution: Tenderly/Forta are a CUSTODY-PHASE add-on relevant only once funds are actually on-chain (OnchainExecutor), accepted as a deliberate zero-cloud exception for the out-of-band 'agent is fully compromised' case; they are not part of the Sim/Paper stack.

3. AUTO-HALT vs ADVISORY-ONLY for statistical detectors. One agent wires EWMA/z-score to optionally FLIP the kill-switch; three others insist these detectors stay strictly ADVISORY (alert-only) because they signal THAT something shifted, never WHY, and mis-tuning would gate good trades. The cleaner consensus: only the deterministic Page-Hinkley/CUSUM on the RETURN stream earns an auto-halt-to-human-review (with K-of-M hysteresis); feature/EWMA/z-score anomaly alerts stay advisory.

4. MMR / off-host signing / DSSE maturity. Merkle Mountain Range logarithmic-proof logs and DSSE-signed off-host anchoring are flagged 'emerging' with Rust/early-GitHub reference implementations and self-described limits; one agent is bullish, another cautions they are more code-to-get-wrong than a flat chain. Consensus: ship the flat HMAC chain first (production), treat MMR + off-host anchoring as additive later layers.

No agent contradicted the core three-layer liveness model or the 'never bypass the policy engine' rule — those are unanimous.

top_techniques.name: Three-layer liveness: OS supervisor (NSSM/systemd) + in-process watchdog + external dead-man's-switch heartbeat
top_techniques.why: Independently recommended by 4 of 10 agents with near-identical reasoning. Each layer catches a distinct failure the others can't: crash (supervisor), silent freeze (watchdog), total host loss (off-box heartbeat). Maps directly onto runner.py's 'wrap in a service' docstring and the user's existing NSSM/cloudflared/VPS toolchain. Near-zero new deps (urllib GET, sd_notify socket write, or a stdlib daemon thread).

top_techniques.name: Append-only HMAC-SHA256 hash-CHAINED JSONL audit ledger with provenance fields
top_techniques.why: Cited by 3 agents as THE strongest stdlib-only primitive. hashlib+hmac+json, zero deps, fully self-hosted. Makes every PROPOSE->DISPOSE->EXECUTE event tamper-EVIDENT (forgery needs the key, not just file-write access). Provenance (session->decision->executor->authorized-limit) turns post-mortem forensics into a trivial SQLite/JSONL walk from a losing fill back to the policy authority that allowed it. Production-ready today; MMR/off-host anchoring are additive emerging layers.

top_techniques.name: Crash-only recovery: durable write-ahead ledger + idempotency keys + journaled breaker state
top_techniques.why: Closes a concrete, identified bug in the current build: RunReport.ledger and PolicyEngine._tripped/_starting_equity/_recent_ts are in-memory only, so a crash silently UN-TRIPS a latched drawdown breaker on restart — a dangerous treasury outcome. WAL + client_order_id idempotency makes the agent killable at any instant with no double-fill, and snapshot()/restore() makes the latched breaker survive. Stdlib-only (json + os.replace atomic writes). The non-negotiable pairing every agent agrees on.

top_techniques.name: Pure-stdlib Page-Hinkley/CUSUM on the live return stream as the auto-halting degradation gate
top_techniques.why: ~30 lines of stdlib, O(1) state, no River/scikit dependency. The lowest-cost, most direct 'my strategy stopped working' alarm — detects a downward mean shift in realized return EARLY, before drawdown deepens enough to trip the financial breaker. The one detector that earns an auto-halt (to human review, with K-of-M hysteresis), distinct from advisory EWMA/z-score feature anomaly alerts.

top_techniques.name: Stale-data freshness gate as a first-class PolicyEngine rule
top_techniques.why: Acting on a frozen feed is a direct loss vector and the cleanest example of folding ops-safety into the EXISTING disposer: PolicyEngine.evaluate() already receives the tick, so a STALE_DATA deny (tick.ts vs wall-clock max-age) surfaces automatically in RunReport.denials and the ledger — auditable, same path as drawdown/cap denials. 2026 production crypto bots ship this alongside the kill-switch. Combined with the heartbeat it distinguishes 'loop alive but feed dead.'

top_techniques.name: Severity-tiered self-hosted ntfy alerting + blameless runbook whose 'block' step is the kill-switch
top_techniques.why: Zero-cloud on-call for a solo builder: single urllib POST to a self-hosted topic, Android DND-override for CRITICAL (kill-switch/drawdown trip) while routine PnL stays INFO, preventing alert fatigue. The runbook (acknowledge->block-at-control-plane->pull trace->fix policy->regression-test->re-enable->post-mortem) turns a 3am breaker trip into a deterministic checklist, and its action items land as NEW hard policy rules + replay regression tests so the same loss is provably impossible to repeat.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Crash-only WAL ledger + journaled PolicyEngine state (snapshot/restore) + idempotency keys on the Executor protocol
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Fixes a real, named correctness bug: in-memory RunReport.ledger and latched PolicyEngine._tripped/_starting_equity/_recent_ts reset on restart, so a crash can silently UN-TRIP a drawdown circuit-breaker and a retried order can double-fill on a real venue. WAL (json + os.replace atomic checkpoint) + startup reconciliation that re-runs solvency/drawdown/kill-switch before resuming intents makes the agent crash-only and safe to supervise. Adding a client_order_id to the Executor Protocol (mapped to Kraken userref / AgentKit idempotency key) prevents replay double-spend on PaperExecutor/OnchainExecutor.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange): the idempotency key is the load-bearing primitive that lets the same WAL/reconcile code safely back either a CEX (Kraken userref) or an on-chain (AgentKit idempotency key) executor, so it should be designed before that choice is locked.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Three-layer liveness: NSSM/systemd supervisor (throttled restart) + in-process last-tick watchdog + external Healthchecks.io dead-man's-switch off-box
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Recommended by 4 independent agents. The agent is a 24/7 unattended loop holding money; the scariest failure is the silent freeze/host-death where NO alarm fires and the treasury stops enforcing policy. Supervisor catches crash, in-process watchdog (stdlib daemon-thread last-tick check on Windows; sd_notify WATCHDOG=1 on the VPS) catches the wedged-but-alive loop, off-box Healthchecks.io ping (stdlib urllib GET per tick, self-hostable on the existing Netcup/Oracle VPS) catches total host loss. The user already runs NSSM/cloudflared/VPS infra so this maps directly. HARD constraint: watchdog restart must re-enter the market only behind the existing kill-switch/solvency gates and be rate-limited (StartLimitIntervalSec) to prevent crash-loops re-opening risk. Heed the user's own NSSM gotcha: AppEnvironmentExtra changes need `nssm restart`, verify with a live probe.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (cross-cutting ops floor for all three decisions)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Append-only HMAC-SHA256 hash-CHAINED JSONL audit ledger with provenance fields (session->decision->executor->authorized-limit)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Cited by 3 agents as the strongest stdlib-only primitive. Before real funds you need a court-of-record, tamper-evident trail of every intent, policy verdict + which rule fired, and executor fill. HMAC (not bare SHA-256) raises forgery from 'recompute the file' to 'also steal the key', and chaining closes the independent-hash weakness in the cited 2026 OTel pipeline. Provenance fields make trading-loss forensics a trivial walk from a losing fill back to the strategy decision and the policy authority that permitted it. Pure stdlib (hashlib+hmac+json), self-hosted, offline. Use canonical JSON (sort_keys, separators) or verify() throws false alarms. Treat MMR logarithmic proofs and off-host root anchoring as additive EMERGING layers added only when volume/external-auditor needs arise.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody): the keyed, externally-verifiable audit chain is what lets an outside reviewer trust the agent's decision history before it touches a self-hosted smart-account's funds, and pairs with the untrusted-agent reconciliation below.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Stale-data freshness gate as a new STALE_DATA rule in PolicyEngine.evaluate()
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Acting on a frozen feed is a direct, common loss vector and the cleanest fold of ops-safety into the existing disposer. evaluate() already receives the tick, so comparing tick.ts to wall-clock/last-seen against a max-age and returning Verdict(allowed=False, reason='STALE_DATA') surfaces automatically in RunReport.denials and the LedgerEvent stream — auditable proof it fired, same path as drawdown/cap. Becomes the feed-freshness watchdog when MarketSimulator is swapped for a live Kraken/Coinbase feed. Caveat: use NTP/monotonic handling for clock skew and tune max-age to avoid spurious pauses on normal jitter.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange): on-chain RPC feeds and CEX websockets have very different freshness/jitter profiles, so the max-age threshold is venue-specific and must be recalibrated when the executor changes.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Pure-stdlib Page-Hinkley/CUSUM on the live return stream as the auto-halting degradation gate (alert-and-halt-to-human, K-of-M hysteresis)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The lowest-cost, most direct 'has my strategy stopped working' alarm: ~30 lines of stdlib, O(1) state, watches realized per-trade/per-bar return and fires on a downward mean shift BEFORE drawdown trips the financial breaker. Wire as an Observer over the executed-fill events; on drift, raise a degradation event the PolicyEngine treats like the kill-switch (stop proposing, alert). It detects THAT not WHY, so it must gate to halt+human-review, not auto-restart, and use K-of-M confirmation because crypto noise whipsaws single breaches. Calibrate lambda/delta on the user's own paper history.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy): this is the safety net that makes a mechanical/quant strategy honest about alpha decay and is the auto-halt backstop if Decision 2 goes LLM-discretionary (a degrading/hallucinating model gets caught by its returns).

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Streaming EWMA / robust-z-score (Welford + MAD) advisory anomaly detector on per-tick equity, drawdown, fill notional, fee, denial-rate
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Catches UNKNOWN-shaped anomalies inside the static caps (abnormal slippage run, fee spike, runaway fill rate, off-baseline equity move) with zero new deps, fed from runner.py's existing per-tick _log/equity path. Must stay strictly ADVISORY (emit a warning push, never gate/trigger a trade) because a false positive should cost only a notification. Use robust stats (MAD not SD) and a warm-up window because naive fixed z=3 over-fires in volatile crypto regimes and under-fires in calm ones. Complements, never replaces, the deterministic policy engine.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (advisory observability layer for all strategies)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Deterministic balance reconciliation treating the agent as untrusted (every balance delta must map to an allowed fill)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: 2026 security guidance (and real 2026 thefts: Lobstar $250K mis-transfer, $204K prompt-injection drain, $45M protocol breach) explicitly says wrap AI agents in external controls and treat them as untrusted. Cheap, deterministic, stdlib-only: per tick assert equity_change ~= sum(executed fill quote/fee deltas) and that every balance delta has a matching allowed-Verdict FILL ledger entry; any mismatch -> anomaly event + kill-switch. SimWallet.apply_fill() already enforces no-negative defensively, so the two reconciliation sides exist. For the on-chain phase, reconcile internal accounting against the queried on-chain balance each tick. Tune float epsilon (code already clamps 1e-9) and tolerances for fees/slippage/gas to avoid false alarms.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody): reconciliation against the authoritative balance is the integrity check that decides how much you can trust a managed-MPC vs self-hosted smart-account custody path; a divergence is the earliest signal of unauthorized outflow.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Severity-tiered self-hosted ntfy push alerts (single urllib POST) wired into every blocking policy disposition
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Zero-cloud, zero-dependency on-call for a solo builder. Every disposition that BLOCKS an intent (kill-switch, drawdown, notional cap, rate-limit, allowlist, solvency) emits a push; CRITICAL (kill-switch/solvency/drawdown trip) overrides Android DND while routine PnL stays INFO, preventing alert fatigue. ntfy self-hosts on the same box (single point of failure) so it MUST be paired with the off-box dead-man's-switch above to catch the case where the box and its own alerter die together. Apprise is an optional no-lock-in fan-out to email/Telegram later.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (cross-cutting alerting)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Blameless post-mortem template whose SMART action items MUST land as new hard policy rules + a replay regression test (incident-replayed-as-failing-then-passing-test = merge gate)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Closes the learning loop: for an agent where the policy engine is the safety source of truth, the highest-leverage post-mortem output is a NEW hard rule (tightened cap, denylist entry, fresh breaker threshold) plus a test that replays the incident intents and asserts the new rule blocks them — so the same loss is provably unrepeatable. Severity ladder maps to existing controls (Sev-1=kill-switch, Sev-2=drawdown breaker, Sev-3=cap/rate-limit reject). Honest caveat: discipline depends on the solo operator; make 'incident replayed as failing-then-passing test' the literal merge gate or post-mortems rot after small losses. Echoes the user's own bit-exact-gate methodology.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (process layer for all decisions)

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: OpenTelemetry gen_ai.* attribute NAMES as the JSON-log schema now, with a deferred optional self-hosted MLflow/Langfuse/Phoenix sidecar
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Use OTel GenAI semantic-convention attribute names (gen_ai.request.model, token usage, finish_reasons) and a contextvar correlation/cycle_id as the SCHEMA for the stdlib JSON audit log, so the audit trail doubles as future trace data with no lock-in. Defer the actual server (self-hosted MLflow Tracing on local SQLite gives trace REPLAY — re-run a bad decision sequence and confirm the policy engine still blocks it; Langfuse/Phoenix via OTLP add UI) as an OPTIONAL ops sidecar OUTSIDE the trading hot path, never a core dep. The GenAI conventions are still experimental — pin the version and never log secrets/raw prompts.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy): token/cost-per-cycle and finish_reason telemetry only matter if Decision 2 is LLM-discretionary; the schema is free now and the sidecar is justified only then.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Graceful-degradation fallback chain (LLM -> mechanical -> HOLD) + per-dependency circuit breaker (CLOSED/OPEN/HALF-OPEN) distinct from the financial breaker
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: De-risks Decision 2 directly: run LLM-discretionary as primary but fall through to a deterministic mechanical strategy on timeout/low-confidence, and ultimately to None=HOLD (the existing safe sink), so a slow/hallucinating/rate-limited model can never escalate into a bad trade. A separate per-dependency breaker (failure-count FSM) around the executor/feed stops the agent hammering a rate-limiting Kraken API or burning gas on a flapping Base RPC, isolated as a bulkhead from PolicyEngine's drawdown breaker. HARD rule: a fallback strategy must still pass the same PolicyEngine; pair silent degradation with the heartbeat/alert so 'quietly doing nothing' still pages you.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy): the fallback chain is the mechanism that lets you adopt LLM-discretionary without betting the treasury on the model, by guaranteeing a deterministic safe floor.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: SOPS+age encrypted secrets-in-git decrypted to memory at launch (sops exec-env) + _FILE secret-reading convention + hardened systemd unit (systemd-analyze security gate)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Phase 2 introduces real money-moving keys (Kraken paper key, AgentKit/Coinbase key, eventual signer/private key). SOPS+age lets the solo builder version-control the full deploy config without a cloud secrets vendor and inject keys only at runtime so plaintext never hits disk; the agent just reads os.environ (stdlib). A small _read_secret() helper (read ${NAME}_FILE else env, fail loudly if neither) makes the same code work for env, SOPS, and container-mounted secrets. On the VPS, a hardened systemd unit (ProtectSystem=strict, NoNewPrivileges, ReadWritePaths limited to the state dir, audited to a low systemd-analyze security score as a CI gate) confines a compromised process from rewriting the kill-switch/policy files it doesn't own. The single hardest dependency is OFFLINE backup of the age private key — the root of trust. Git-push PaaS (Dokku/Kamal) is over-engineering unless container isolation/one-command rollback is genuinely needed.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 3 (custody): secret handling and process sandboxing are exactly the controls that distinguish a safely self-hosted smart-account signer from a managed-MPC service where the key never lives on your box.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: On-chain wallet balance-threshold / token-transfer webhooks (Tenderly / Forta) as an out-of-band custody-phase safety net
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Once funds are actually on-chain (OnchainExecutor on Base), an INDEPENDENT off-process watcher on the real wallet address fires if balance drops below a floor or moves beyond the per-tx notional cap the policy engine enforces — catching the 'agent code/host fully compromised' case the in-process layers can't. HONEST hype caveat: these are hosted SaaS (breaks zero-cloud), REACTIVE not preventive (alert fires AFTER the tx confirms), and irrelevant for SimExecutor/PaperExecutor. Low impact because it only matters in the final on-chain phase, is a deliberate zero-cloud exception, and partly duplicates the deterministic balance reconciliation done locally. Adopt only as a custody-phase add-on, not core.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 1 (chain/exchange): only applicable if Decision 1 chooses the EVM/DeFi on-chain path; a CEX path replaces this with the exchange's own withdrawal-whitelist/2FA controls.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: River drift detectors (ADWIN/KSWIN) on input features and rolling Probabilistic/Deflated Sharpe Ratio as a significance gate
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Genuinely useful but lower priority and partly outside the stdlib constraint. River (one vetted pip dep) adds ADWIN/KSWIN feature-drift detection that can warn on regime change BEFORE PnL moves — but drift on inputs does not always mean PnL harm, so it must be advisory and conflicts with stdlib-only (reimplement from the published algorithm if purity matters). PSR/DSR (computable in stdlib with a hand-shipped normal-CDF) turns 'is the alpha real' into a calibrated probability that corrects for fat tails and the multiple-testing/overfit bias of backtesting many variants — but it is noisy on short crypto windows and the DSR N-trials term is judgmental, so use hysteresis. Both are refinements to add AFTER the Page-Hinkley gate proves itself, not first.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: Decision 2 (strategy): PSR/DSR is the rigorous 'is this strategy statistically still working' health metric for a mechanical/quant strategy, and the DSR N-trials deflation directly fights trusting an overfit backtested config in live.

themes.consensus:
Across all 10 agents a single dominant pattern emerges: the safest, most-automatable on-chain path for a local-first Python treasury agent in 2026 is MECHANICAL stablecoin yield + defense-in-depth, not speculative or LLM-discretionary trading. Five points of strong agreement:

1. ERC-4626 curator-managed stablecoin vaults (Morpho/Aave/Spark, ~3.5-8% APY) are the load-bearing primitive. Three independent agents converge here. The uniform deposit/redeem ABI keeps the OnchainExecutor protocol-agnostic via one adapter, and independent risk curators (Gauntlet, Steakhouse, Chaos Labs) absorb the risk-modeling a solo builder cannot replicate. Mechanical rate-rotation between vaults only when (APY_B - APY_A)*principal*horizon > gas (the Giza ARMA pattern) is repeatedly endorsed as a clean fit for PROPOSE->POLICY->EXECUTE.

2. Project the existing Python POLICY engine DOWN into the chain. Four separate agents (session-keys, ERC-7715/4337, AgentKit smart-wallet, Hyperliquid approveAgent) all reach the same conclusion: encode the same caps (per-tx notional, symbol/contract allowlist, rate-limit as expiry, kill-switch as revoke) into a smart-account / scoped session key so the chain reverts anything the Python layer misses. This turns one trusted layer into a defense-in-depth pair. Consensus: keep the Python policy engine as the fast pre-flight check, the on-chain scope as the backstop.

3. Make Health Factor and peg-health first-class deterministic POLICY invariants. Both lending and depeg agents agree these are read-only, zero-fund, pure-Python scalars (HF via getUserAccountData; peg via multi-signal deviation/imbalance/slippage + Chainlink oracle anchor) that slot directly into the disposer as pre-trade gates and circuit-breaker inputs. Persistence-gating (15-30 min) to avoid thrashing is repeatedly emphasized.

4. Base + Coinbase AgentKit (Python, base-sepolia) is the consensus OnchainExecutor target. Sub-cent fees make frequent treasury actions economical; the free CDP faucet enables zero-fund end-to-end validation. Multiple agents independently note AgentKit ships NO native staking/lending action, so a thin custom YieldExecutor wrapping Morpho/Aave calldata is required — which fits the existing Execution interface.

5. A clear validation ladder before real funds: SimExecutor -> mainnet-fork harness (the repeatedly-cited 'missing rung') -> Base Sepolia / Hyperliquid testnet -> mainnet tiny-caps. Every agent treats 'validated before real funds' as non-negotiable and stresses that published thresholds (0.995, 50 bps, 60/40, 10%/2% drawdown) are illustrative defaults requiring per-symbol backtesting.

Universal caveat: NONE of the executor SDKs are stdlib-only. eth-account/EIP-712 signing, web3, websockets, cdp-sdk are unavoidable at the on-chain boundary. Consensus is to quarantine those deps strictly behind the Execution interface and keep the policy core stdlib-pure.
themes.contradictions:
1. CUSTODY (Phase-2 decision 3) — the strongest contradiction. Two agents push toward a SELF-HOSTED ERC-4337/ERC-7702 smart account with owner-held master key + scoped session key. But the AgentKit agent notes Coinbase 'Agentic Wallets' are MANAGED-MPC, which nudges the SAME decision toward managed custody. Both use 'smart account' language but land on opposite custody models. Resolution: the smart-account standard is custody-neutral; the choice is who holds the master key. For a high-autonomy local-first builder, self-hosted smart account + scoped session key is the better fit, but managed-MPC via CDP is the lower-friction on-ramp.

2. STRATEGY VENUE — DeFi yield vaults vs perps DEXs. Most agents favor mechanical stablecoin yield (vaults/lending). One agent argues Hyperliquid perps give a stronger PROTOCOL-ENFORCED custody boundary (approveAgent wallets provably cannot withdraw). These are different risk profiles, not directly comparable: vaults are hold-by-default low-risk; perps are directional/leveraged. For a conservative treasury, yield wins for v1; the Hyperliquid custody insight is worth borrowing even if perps are not the strategy.

3. STDLIB-ONLY PURITY. Agents disagree on how much to compromise. The depeg/HF-monitoring agents show much of the value (scoring, gas math, HF computation) is pure-Python and only needs urllib for feeds. The executor agents concede heavy deps are unavoidable. No real conflict once you separate the stdlib-pure policy/strategy core from the dep-heavy executor boundary.

4. RESTAKING / LRTs (EigenLayer) — one agent surfaces it for extra yield but immediately self-relegates it to the smallest, hardest-capped tier (up-to-100% slashing). This is the closest thing to hype in the set and is honestly flagged as such by its own author. Treat as research-only, not v1.

5. Higher-LLTV venues (Morpho ~86-94% vs Aave ~80%) — one agent frames the +50-150 bps as an optimization; another implicitly warns naive rate-chasing raises liquidation odds. Resolved by coupling higher-LLTV venues to higher HF floors in policy.

top_techniques.name: ERC-4626 curator-managed stablecoin lending vaults (Morpho/Aave/Spark on Base) as the core yield primitive
top_techniques.why: Single most-endorsed technique (3+ independent agents). Uniform deposit/redeem ABI means one executor adapter covers all vaults; curators absorb risk-modeling; isolated markets + timelocks give native circuit-breaker semantics that mirror the existing POLICY engine. Agent only decides how-much/which-vault. ~3.5-8% APY, fully permissionless on Base = works for a self-hosted account.

top_techniques.name: Mirror the Python POLICY engine on-chain via ERC-7715/ERC-4337 scoped session keys (delegation manager)
top_techniques.why: Endorsed in four different forms (7715/4337, EIP-7702 session keys, AgentKit smart-wallet, Hyperliquid approveAgent). Converts one trusted policy layer into defense-in-depth: notional cap->max spend, allowlist->allowed contracts, rate-limit->expiry, kill-switch->revoke. A leaked key or compromised process is physically capped at the granted scope. Directly answers the custody decision.

top_techniques.name: Health-Factor + peg-health as first-class deterministic POLICY invariants (read-only RPC)
top_techniques.why: Both lending and depeg agents converge. HF (getUserAccountData) and peg-health (Chainlink 0.995 oracle anchor + multi-signal deviation/imbalance/slippage with 15-30min persistence gating) are zero-fund, manipulation-resistant, pure-Python scalars that slot straight into the disposer as pre-trade gates and breaker inputs. Soft-liquidation 'deleverage-to-target-HF' (DeFi Saver pattern, proven at scale Feb 2026) is the matching PROPOSE->DISPOSE->EXECUTE defense.

top_techniques.name: Base + Coinbase AgentKit (Python, base-sepolia) as the OnchainExecutor with a thin custom YieldExecutor
top_techniques.why: Consensus execution target. Sub-cent fees make frequent actions economical; free CDP faucet enables zero-fund end-to-end validation; CdpSmartWalletProvider can enforce caps on-chain. AgentKit ships NO native staking/lending, so wrapping Morpho/Aave deposit/withdraw calldata behind the existing Execution interface is the required (and clean) integration.

top_techniques.name: Mainnet-fork simulation harness (web3.py + forked Aave/Morpho/Uniswap) as the validation rung between SimExecutor and testnet
top_techniques.why: Directly serves the 'validate before real funds' mandate with real ABIs, real liquidation thresholds, real revert behavior at zero cost. Lets you replay liquidation/depeg scenarios by warping oracle state and assert the policy engine + auto-defense behave correctly — far higher fidelity than SimExecutor's synthetic model, still fully local.

top_techniques.name: Real gas/fee accounting folded into the policy boundary (eth_feeHistory percentile oracle + OP-Stack L1 data-fee model + stuck-tx bump daemon)
top_techniques.why: On Base the volatile dominant cost is the L1 data/blob fee, which a naive EIP-1559 estimate undercounts and which can eat tiny-cap trades. Feeding true per-tx cost into the solvency/drawdown checks (and a rule rejecting intents whose fee exceeds a fraction of notional) plus a replace-by-fee daemon keeps the agent's internal book consistent with chain state during spikes.

top_techniques.name: Intent-based bridging (ERC-7683 / Across) treated as a top-tier threat surface IF any cross-chain hop is needed
top_techniques.why: Atomic complete-or-revert + solver-absorbed fill risk eliminates the worst failure mode (stuck/half-bridged funds): a failed hop is a safe no-op the policy engine detects and retries. Bridges remain ~40% of Web3 losses, so every hop gets a lower notional cap, contract allowlist, settlement-poll breaker. Keep v1 single-chain (Base only) and avoid bridging entirely until needed.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Build v1 strategy as mechanical stablecoin yield on curator-managed ERC-4626 vaults (Morpho/Spark on Base), with threshold-gated rate-rotation (Giza ARMA pattern). Agent PROPOSES deposit/redeem/rotate intents; POLICY disposes via vault-address allowlist, per-vault exposure cap, solvency reserve buffer, and a rotation rate-limit (gas-vs-delta gate).
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: EVM/DeFi on Base, and (2) strategy: mechanical/quant over LLM-discretionary
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Most-endorsed path across the whole corpus. Mechanical, deterministic, fully backtestable in SimExecutor before any funds; uniform ABI keeps the executor as one small adapter; curators absorb the modeling a solo builder cannot do. Directly settles strategy decision in favor of mechanical, and chain decision in favor of EVM/Base. Hard-allowlist curator-vetted vault addresses; never let the agent pick arbitrary pools.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Provision the OnchainExecutor signer as a scoped, time-bounded, revocable session key on a SELF-HOSTED ERC-4337 smart account (ERC-7715 / EIP-7702), with a delegation contract enforcing the SAME caps as policy.py (max cumulative spend = notional cap, allowed contracts = vault allowlist, expiry = rate-limit/kill-switch). Owner retains the master key offline.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody: self-hosted smart account + scoped session key over managed-MPC
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Endorsed in four independent forms. Converts the single trusted Python layer into defense-in-depth: even a fully compromised agent process is physically capped at the granted on-chain scope. Best fit for a high-autonomy local-first builder who wants to hold his own keys. Keep the Python policy as the fast pre-flight check and set on-chain limits as a strict (slightly looser) superset so Python trips first. Test revocation/expiry explicitly on Base Sepolia before trusting it as the kill-switch — 7702 tooling is still maturing.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a peg-health POLICY gate: Chainlink USDC/USD feed (0.995 trigger + updatedAt staleness check) as the authoritative anchor, combined with a local pure-Python multi-signal detector (deviation bps + pool imbalance >60/40 + exit-slippage >15bps) under a 15-30 min persistence window, fused into one per-symbol flag wired to the existing kill-switch / circuit-breaker.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Stablecoin yield is only as safe as the peg. Deterministic, manipulation-resistant, stdlib-friendly (only the feed needs urllib/eth_call). Lets the agent de-risk BEFORE a depeg unwinds rather than after. Persistence-gating prevents breaker thrash. Published thresholds are illustrative — backtest per-symbol on Sim/Paper before trusting them to move funds.
