recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Implement the OnchainExecutor on Base via Coinbase AgentKit (Python, network_id='base-sepolia') as a thin adapter behind the existing Execution interface, dispatching to action providers only AFTER policy approval; write a custom YieldExecutor tool wrapping Morpho/Aave deposit+withdraw calldata (AgentKit has no native lending action). Quarantine all pip deps (cdp-sdk, web3) at this boundary; keep the policy/strategy core stdlib-pure.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: Base/EVM execution rail
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Consensus lowest-friction real OnchainExecutor. Sub-cent fees + free CDP faucet give zero-fund end-to-end validation of the full kill-switch/drawdown/notional loop against a live testnet venue. Slots cleanly into the Sim -> Sepolia -> mainnet-tiny ladder by flipping the network constant. Verify provider/method signatures against the installed version — the SDK is fast-moving.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Insert a mainnet-fork harness (Anvil/Hardhat fork + web3.py) as a ForkExecutor variant — the missing rung between SimExecutor and Base Sepolia. Replay liquidation/depeg scenarios by warping fork oracle state to drive HF/peg below trigger and assert the policy engine + soft-liquidation auto-defense fire correctly.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Directly serves the 'validated before any real funds' mandate with real Pool ABIs, real liquidation thresholds, real revert behavior at zero cost and fully local. Highest-fidelity test short of spending money. Treat the fork node as an external process the test harness launches so the core stays stdlib-friendly; lives in test/integration, not core.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Make Health Factor a first-class POLICY invariant via a HealthFactorMonitor (read-only getUserAccountData) with tiered floors (warn 1.2 / critical 1.05), and implement a 'DeleverageToTarget' soft-liquidation strategy (trigger-HF -> target-HF repay/deposit) with a HARD cap on auto-defense capital per window. Only relevant if the agent ever BORROWS; skip for a pure deposit-only yield v1.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Real, production-proven (DeFi Saver fired 1,227 protective txs in a day during the Feb-2026 ~$5B unwind). Deterministic and testable on the fork harness. Rated medium because a conservative v1 should be deposit-only (no borrow leg), in which case HF monitoring is unnecessary — but it is the right design the moment any leverage/borrow is introduced.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Fold real gas/fee accounting into the policy boundary: a web3.py eth_feeHistory percentile oracle for EIP-1559, PLUS the OP-Stack L1 data/blob-fee component for Base, surfaced into solvency/drawdown checks, with a policy rule rejecting intents whose estimated fee exceeds a configurable fraction of trade notional, and a stuck-tx replace-by-fee bump daemon under a max-fee ceiling.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Tiny-cap mainnet trades can be eaten by fees, and on Base the L1 data fee (not L2 gas) is the volatile dominant cost a naive estimate undercounts. Folding true cost into the existing per-tx-cap/solvency checks keeps gas inside the policy boundary; the bump daemon keeps the internal book consistent with chain state during spikes. Validate scalars against real Base Sepolia receipts — constants change with upgrades.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Keep v1 SINGLE-CHAIN (Base only) and defer all cross-chain bridging. If/when a hop becomes necessary, use intent-based bridging (ERC-7683 / Across) gated as a top-tier threat surface: separate lower notional cap, bridge-contract allowlist, independent rate-limit, and a settlement-poll circuit-breaker that trips if fill is not confirmed within fillDeadline.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange: scope the chain decision to a single L2 for v1
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Bridges are ~40% of all Web3 losses and the worst failure class for an autonomous agent (stuck/depegged funds). Intent bridging's atomic complete-or-revert + solver-absorbed risk makes a failed hop a safe no-op, but the cleanest risk reduction is to not bridge at all in v1. Verify exact testnet Across addresses and the open()/orderDataType ABI against live docs before wiring — those specifics were single-sourced.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Harden the treasury portfolio side with issuer-diversification caps (per-issuer concentration limit across USDC/USDT/USDS/PYUSD) plus attestation-freshness and on-chain totalSupply-contraction guards feeding the same peg-health flag.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Turns counterparty/custody policy into enforceable code: no single-issuer concentration, 'attestation older than 30 days = elevated risk', 'supply contracting fast = smart money exiting' early warning. Cheap (eth_call totalSupply + HTTPS attestation poll). Rated medium because it adds operational surface (more tokens, more approvals) and dilutes yield; valuable but secondary to the core vault + peg-gate stack.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Borrow Hyperliquid's approveAgent custody insight (protocol-enforced agent wallet that can trade but provably cannot withdraw) as a design reference even though perps are NOT the v1 strategy. If a directional/perps sleeve is ever added, Hyperliquid's official Python SDK with one-constant TESTNET_API_URL is the validate-before-funds rung.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody / (2) strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The 'agent key can trade but cannot drain' boundary is a strong custody pattern worth mirroring conceptually in the smart-account session-key design. But perps are directional/leveraged and out of scope for a conservative hold-by-default treasury v1, and the SDK is async/non-stdlib. Keep as a documented fallback venue, not an initial target.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Treat restaking / LRTs (EigenLayer, ether.fi) and Uniswap concentrated-LP / v4 hooks as research-only, NOT v1. If concentrated LP is ever pursued, use an audited managed-ALM vault (Gamma/Arrakis) as a single deposit/withdraw primitive and calibrate caps with an offline CLMM backtester first.
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Honest hype-flagging: restaking carries up-to-100% slashing that compounds across AVSs — its own author relegates it to the smallest hardest-capped tier. Concentrated LP permanently realizes impermanent loss on every rebalance and assumes a live arbitrageur — wrong fit for a conservative treasury. Neither belongs in v1; both are strictly higher-risk than the curator-vault base layer that already delivers comparable yield with less tail risk.

themes.theme: Safety, Adversarial & Governance for an autonomous crypto treasury agent
themes.consensus: All 10 research streams converge on one load-bearing claim: for a money-handling agent in 2026, the LLM must be treated as structurally untrusted, and the real safety guarantee MUST live in a deterministic, non-LLM layer that bounds worst-case loss. This is the exact architecture treasuryforge already has (agent PROPOSES a typed intent -> hard-rule POLICY disposes -> wallet EXECUTES within enforced limits). Multiple independent sources name this propose/dispose/execute split as the de-facto production pattern and as the most attack-robust control protocol ('defer on critical actions', SEC Rule 15c3-5 / CFTC Reg AT control stack, AI-Control blue-team protocols). The recurring corollaries: (1) prompt injection / data poisoning CANNOT be solved at the model level (OpenAI/Anthropic/DeepMind consensus; ~50% attack success even on strong models in AgentDojo), so defense-in-depth that minimizes blast radius is the only sound posture; (2) the dominant attack surface is the DATA feed (poisoned price oracles, spoofed order-book depth, gray-box market perturbation that wrecks an agent with NO model access), so robustness is bought cheapest at the data-ingestion and policy boundaries, not via heavy ML; (3) the agent must never self-report or be able to write the metric it is judged on (P&L, solvency, drawdown must be independently computed and read-only to the agent); (4) approval channels that are not authenticated/cryptographically signed and out-of-band are 'security theater'; (5) caps should be enforced TWICE — policy-constrained off-chain AND signature/contract-constrained at the custody layer — with hard pre-signature enforcement preferred over webhook monitoring; (6) the existing policy engine maps near one-to-one to institutional control norms (kill-switch=Reg AT kill switch, notional cap+solvency=15c3-5 pre-trade controls, rate-limit=order throttle), so it should be adopted as engineering hygiene plus a git-backed code-change audit trail and an append-only execution log. Strong cross-cutting strategy guidance: favor the mechanical/quant action path over LLM-discretionary, keeping any LLM strictly advisory behind the policy engine, because a deterministic disposer and deterministic rationale are inherently more verifiable and the LLM out of the action loop eliminates whole injection classes.
themes.contradictions: The only material tension is the Phase-2 CUSTODY decision (managed MPC/TEE vs self-hosted smart-account). Several streams recommend managed agent wallets (Coinbase Agentic Wallets, Cobo, Human.tech WaaP, MetaMask Agent Wallet) because they give a battle-tested, non-bypassable SECOND enforcement boundary outside the Python host where caps cannot be evaded even by a compromised agent — but this reintroduces a cloud/custodial dependency that directly conflicts with Christian's stated local-first / zero-cloud preference, plus KYC and unverified vendor-TEE trust. The opposing pull is the self-hosted smart-account route (ERC-7715 session keys, Coinbase spend-permissions as a modular owner, Safe/guardian recovery), which preserves self-custody but shifts audit and bug risk onto the builder and carries newer/less-mature SDK support. Sources do NOT confirm a drop-in self-hosted equivalent with identical guarantees, so this is a genuine unresolved tradeoff rather than a clear winner. Secondary tensions, all reconcilable: (a) circuit breakers / kill-switches are a defense AND a denial-of-service lever an attacker who learns thresholds can trip to force you flat — so halt logic must define safe-unwind, not just freeze; (b) preemptive flatten-on-shutdown is itself a trade into a possibly disorderly market (slippage/can't-fill), so it is not 'free' safety; (c) human-in-the-loop gates add latency and are themselves attackable via 'Lies-in-the-Loop' summaries, mitigated only by rendering RAW tx fields not LLM rationale; (d) median+MAD multi-source consensus is robust only to a MINORITY of poisoned feeds and can starve a legit strategy if k is too tight. No source contradicts the central propose/dispose/execute thesis.

top_techniques.name: Propose/dispose/execute split with deterministic non-LLM disposer (defer-on-critical-action)
top_techniques.why: Independently named by ~6 of 10 streams as THE production safety pattern and validated as the most attack-robust control protocol even against an agent that knows the protocol. treasuryforge already embodies it; the action items are to keep the disposer 100% deterministic stdlib (never an LLM), keep intents typed dataclasses with no free-form smuggle field, and make solvency/drawdown/P&L state independently computed and read-only to the agent.

top_techniques.name: Robust multi-source price ingestion: median + MAD outlier rejection
top_techniques.why: Cheapest, highest-leverage defense against the dominant 2026 attack surface (poisoned/manipulated oracles, fat-finger prints, flash spikes). ~30 lines pure stdlib, no deps; the planned Kraken + Coinbase venues already provide two independent feeds. Caveat: robust only to a minority of corrupted sources.

top_techniques.name: Cumulative time-windowed spend budgets / velocity limits
top_techniques.why: The single highest-value GAP in current policy.py, which caps only per-tx notional and trade count — letting a wrong/injected agent bleed the treasury via many small in-cap trades. Reuses the existing _prune_window deque pattern; pure stdlib; later maps 1:1 onto ERC-7715 daily-allowance and session caps. Must count EXECUTED fills, not proposed intents.

top_techniques.name: Market-anomaly circuit breaker as a hard policy rule
top_techniques.why: Direct answer to the gray-box result that an adversary can wreck a trading agent purely by perturbing the observed market with no model access (victim profit cut 85-139%). Slots into the existing dispose() path alongside the drawdown breaker as an additive rule (return z-score, spread blowout, median-deviation flag). Must define safe-unwind to avoid being a DoS lever.

top_techniques.name: Tamper-evident HMAC-SHA256 hash-chained JSONL decision log
top_techniques.why: Pure-stdlib (hashlib/hmac/json) append-only ledger emitting one record per policy evaluation (intent, policy_version, system-state hash, allow/deny, reason, prev_hash). Turns the safety layer from a black box into an auditable trail, satisfies the 15c3-5/Reg-AT audit-trail expectation and the 'validated before real funds' goal. Key/log must live outside the agent's writable path; external anchoring needed for true non-repudiation.

top_techniques.name: Tiered approval (ALLOW/DENY/ESCALATE) with out-of-band cryptographically-signed gate + dedicated low-balance account
top_techniques.why: Classify intents on a risk gradient (notional, reversibility, novelty, destination); auto-execute low tier, ESCALATE high/anomalous/irreversible to an out-of-band signed owner confirm (Christian's existing phone->PC push channel) whose signing key lives OFF the agent host, defaulting to DENY on timeout. The low-balance agent account bounds worst case to what you fund, independent of code correctness. Render RAW tx fields, never LLM summary.

top_techniques.name: Custody-layer cap mirroring + on-chain batch-revoke kill switch (Coinbase spend-permissions / ERC-7715 session keys)
top_techniques.why: For the eventual OnchainExecutor, mirror policy caps into a non-bypassable signer/contract layer so a compromised Python host still cannot exceed the cap; kill-switch gains an on-chain revoke()/executeBatch(). Spend-permissions are Spearbit-audited and exclude arbitrary external calls. Caveat: on-chain revoke costs gas and is not instant under congestion, so the off-chain kill-switch remains the immediate stop.

top_techniques.name: Adversarial replay / red-team harness as a CI gate (AgentDojo Banking + Promptfoo + AI-Control game)
top_techniques.why: Operationalizes 'validate before real funds': feed poisoned ticks, spoofed/ghost-wall books, gray-box perturbations, and a deliberately-subversive attack policy (slow-drip under cap, rate-limit gaming, allowlist-adjacent symbols) into the PROPOSE step and ASSERT the policy engine + drawdown breaker bound cumulative loss. Success metric = 'attack contained', converted to permanent pytest regressions. Promptfoo runs locally and free; AgentDojo/AI-Control are research-grade and must be ported.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add cumulative time-windowed spend budgets (max_notional_per_window) to PolicyEngine
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (cross-cutting policy hardening; informs strategy decision indirectly)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The most-cited concrete GAP: today policy.py bounds single-trade notional and trade count but NOT total value moved per window, so an injected or buggy agent can drain via many in-cap trades. Pure stdlib, reuses the existing _prune_window deque, fully testable in SimExecutor before funds, and later maps 1:1 onto ERC-7715 daily allowances. Must count executed fills and persist them for restart-resilience.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Robust multi-source price ingestion (median + MAD outlier rejection) as a DataIngest layer before the strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Neutralizes the dominant 2026 attack surface (single-source oracle poisoning, fat-finger, flash spikes) for ~30 lines of stdlib. Directly favors a path that gives >=2 independent venues; the planned Kraken paper + Coinbase on-chain feeds supply them for free. Robust only to a minority of poisoned feeds — document that limit and tune k to avoid data starvation in real volatility.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Make all P&L / solvency / drawdown state independently computed by the executor/ledger and READ-ONLY to the agent
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Closes the specification-gaming / reward-hacking class: the agent must never self-report or be able to write the metric it is judged on. Reward-Hacking-Benchmark and AI-Control results show RL/LLM agents exploit writable accounting and then confidently self-justify (72% of exploits). Cheap to enforce in a local-first build and a strong argument for the mechanical/quant strategy whose 'rationale' is deterministic.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add a market-anomaly circuit breaker (return z-score / spread blowout / median-deviation) as a hard policy rule with defined safe-unwind
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (cross-cutting policy hardening)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The direct, model-independent answer to the gray-box attack that wrecks a trading agent purely by perturbing observed market data (no model access; profit cut 85-139%). Additive rule reusing the drawdown-breaker enforcement path. Must specify safe-unwind behavior, not just freeze, because breakers are themselves a DoS lever an attacker can trip once thresholds are known.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Tamper-evident HMAC-SHA256 hash-chained JSONL decision log emitted at the dispose() boundary, key/log stored outside the agent's writable path
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Pure-stdlib authoritative 'what happened, under which policy, in what order, why' ledger. Satisfies the 15c3-5/Reg-AT audit-trail expectation, makes 'validated before real funds' provable, and (with deterministic rule-fired rationale as source of truth) reinforces the mechanical-strategy explainability edge over LLM-discretionary. Add verify_chain() and a frozen canonical-v1 schema; needs external anchoring for true non-repudiation.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Build an adversarial replay harness (poisoned ticks, spoofed/ghost-wall books, gray-box perturbations, slow-drip attack policy) as a required CI gate before SimExecutor->PaperExecutor->OnchainExecutor promotion
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (validation discipline; informs all three)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Turns adversarial robustness into a release gate rather than an aspiration, fitting the existing build-and-verify methodology. Success metric = 'attack contained' (every over-cap/off-allowlist/post-kill-switch intent blocked). Promptfoo runs locally and free; borrow AgentDojo Banking + AI-Control red/blue framing. Caveat: only covers attacks you encode — green runs are necessary, not sufficient.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Tiered ALLOW/DENY/ESCALATE policy with out-of-band cryptographically-signed approval (reuse phone->PC push) + a dedicated low-balance agent account
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Bounds blast radius twice: the dedicated low-balance account caps worst case to what you fund regardless of any rule bug, and ESCALATE routes high/anomalous/irreversible/first-mainnet actions to a signed out-of-band confirm whose key lives OFF the agent host (defaults to DENY on timeout). Render RAW tx fields to defeat Lies-in-the-Loop. An unsigned/in-band approval channel is security theater.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Add an append-only git-backed code/policy-change audit trail and a single-principal 'no pooled funds / no advice' invariant in config + README
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (compliance + future-proofing)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The personal-funds exemption (trade only your own capital, no pooling, no advice) keeps treasuryforge out of broker-dealer/adviser/CASP registration in major jurisdictions, making the whole project legally tractable for a solo builder (verify locally; US SEC-vs-CFTC is security-vs-commodity). Codify as a hard policy rule rejecting any non-owner account/counterparty. The git + execution-log trail completes the 15c3-5/Reg-AT control-stack match as hygiene, not a registration claim. Not legal advice.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Atomic state writes (os.replace) + intent-logged-before-execute with client-order-ID idempotency + startup/interval reconciliation loop against venue ground-truth
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (ops resilience)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The load-bearing crash-recovery pattern for a single-process local-first agent with no orchestrator: on ungraceful kill, the next startup diffs its ledger against exchange/on-chain truth and self-heals instead of double-submitting. Idempotency keys are mandatory or the heal step itself double-executes. Pure stdlib via os.replace + the existing Executor interface as the seam.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: SIGTERM/SIGINT graceful-shutdown sequence: flip file-based kill-switch (checked every cycle) -> preemptive flatten/cancel_all -> fsync audit ledger; plus a wall-clock mandate auto-expiry policy rule
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: none (ops resilience)
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Gives a solo operator a 'walk away safely' guarantee: an unattended or wedged agent winds down to a flat/revoked state rather than holding risk indefinitely. The kill-switch as a file the policy checks every cycle works even if the loop is wedged. Caveat: flatten itself trades into a possibly disorderly market (slippage), so it is not free; verify auto-expiry semantics before real funds.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Deterministic stdlib input sanitization (strip invisible Unicode Tag/control chars, neutralize Markdown links) + hardcoded egress host allowlist on the executor HTTP layer
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (1) chain/exchange
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Zero-model, stdlib-only, few-dozen-line closes of entire exfiltration/injection vector classes (steganographic invisible-char injection, image-URL data exfil, redirect to attacker domains). Perfectly aligned with the local-first stdlib constraint. Closes only the specific vectors it targets, so it layers under the policy/quarantine controls rather than replacing them.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Deflated Sharpe Ratio (Lopez de Prado/Bailey) as a pre-promotion gate that records every parameter-sweep trial count in the audit log
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The trading-specific form of specification gaming: a solo builder tuning strategies on his own machine will overfit the backtest unless trials are deflated. Pure-Python, production-grade, directly enforces 'validate before real funds'. Only relevant on the mechanical/quant path; needs honest trial logging or it under-corrects, and a passing DSR is necessary not sufficient (crypto non-stationarity).

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Provenance/integrity tainting: tag every market/news/tool input with a trust label that propagates, and add a policy rule that caps or blocks notional when supporting evidence is low-trust
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Extends the existing deterministic policy layer to be the authoritative gate on injection/memory-poisoning (the attack class behind 2026's largest agent losses, e.g. the cited $45M incident). Only fully needed if the agent ingests untrusted text or keeps memory; for the mechanical path keep the LLM out of the action loop entirely (strongest version). Trust scores are heuristic and gameable, so this layers on top of hard caps, not instead.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Spoof/ghost-wall detection (order-to-trade ratio, depth-persistence) keying decisions off executed volume, wired as a policy veto/throttle
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (2) strategy
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: Only matters IF the strategy ever uses order-book depth/imbalance as a feature; for a simple mechanical strategy that sizes off executed price it is unnecessary. Useful as a policy throttle if depth is consumed, but heuristics false-positive on legitimate fast market-maker cancellation and miss coordinated cross-venue layering, so it is a lower-priority, conditional addition.

recommended_for_treasuryforge.technique: Defer custody-layer cap mirroring (ERC-7715 session keys / Coinbase spend-permissions with on-chain batch-revoke) to the Base Sepolia testnet phase; keep off-chain policy as line one
recommended_for_treasuryforge.decision_mapped: (3) custody
recommended_for_treasuryforge.why: The strongest blast-radius backstop for real funds (a compromised host cannot exceed a contract-enforced cap), and the self-hosted spend-permissions route preserves Christian's self-custody preference over managed-MPC vendors. But the vendor/SDK ecosystem (Human.tech WaaP, MetaMask Agent Wallet launched 2026-06-08) is new and largely vendor-described/unaudited from these sources — so testnet-validate scope semantics before mainnet, avoid unlimited ERC-20 approvals, and remember on-chain revoke needs gas and is not instant. Do NOT adopt managed-custody now given the zero-cloud preference.

verdicts.technique: Funding-carry as first IntentGenerator: stdlib urllib POST to Hyperliquid /info (type=fundingHistory) -> fee-aware net-carry compute -> paired (spot_buy, perp_short) intents under a |spot_qty - perp_qty| <= epsilon delta-band policy invariant, validated in SimExecutor before live funds.
verdicts.assessment: Mostly verified, with two corrections. CONFIRMED REAL (2026): Hyperliquid's /info fundingHistory endpoint is genuinely public, no-auth, POST with JSON body, reachable with pure stdlib urllib against api.hyperliquid.xyz/info. The widely-cited 422 'Failed to deserialize' error applies ONLY to third-party RPC proxies (Quicknode/Chainstack indexer path), NOT the official endpoint — so the stdlib implementation works as described. Funding carry is a live, active strategy in 2026; net APY has compressed to ~10-15% in favorable conditions, 0-5% neutral, and NEGATIVE in bear/negative-funding regimes. The recommendation's caveats are well-calibrated and survive scrutiny: it explicitly flags headline 10-30% as gross/pre-fee, mandates a fee-aware net-carry check before proposing, and requires SimExecutor validation on real historical funding first. The delta-neutral structure does map 1:1 onto propose->policy->execute, and the |spot_qty - perp_qty| <= epsilon invariant is a sound, easily-reasoned-about policy rail. A genuine architectural bonus the rationale undersold: Hyperliquid portfolio/cross margin lets the spot leg collateralize the perp short on a SINGLE venue, so the two legs' PnL offset — materially de-risking liquidation versus a cross-venue carry, and avoiding multi-exchange custody. TWO REFUTATIONS. (1) Source quality: one of the four cited sources, github.com/aoki-h-jp/funding-rate-arbitrage, does NOT actually support the stated implementation — it is CCXT-based (not stdlib), CEX-only (Binance/Bybit/OKX/Gate/CoinEx/Bitget), does NOT support Hyperliquid, is detection-only with no execution, and is unmaintained since Aug 2023. It should be dropped as supporting evidence; the QuickNode/Chainstack/official GitBook docs carry the real weight. (2) 'Ship as the FIRST IntentGenerator' overstates independence from the open Phase-2 decisions. READING funding is free/decoupled and a perfect first build. But EXECUTING the paired trade for real forces premature resolution of (1) chain/venue = Hyperliquid and (3) custody = an onchain Hyperliquid account — both still-open. Combined with marginal economics at solo size (the corpus itself flags sub-$10k accounts as fee-drag-dominated and funding-flip-exposed), going LIVE with carry first would be a money-handling risk, not a value-add.
verdicts.caveats: Adopt the SimExecutor/intent-generator half now; do NOT route real funds through it first. Concretely: (a) build the urllib fundingHistory poller + fee-aware net-carry calculator + delta-band invariant against the OFFICIAL api.hyperliquid.xyz/info (not Quicknode/Chainstack — they 422); (b) backtest net carry on real historical funding in SimExecutor and prove it clears round-trip fees (~0.21% example) plus a margin-of-safety buffer before any intent is allowed to fire; (c) add an explicit funding-flip guardrail — auto-flatten/exit policy when net carry turns negative, since negative-funding persistence is the documented #1 killer; (d) defer live execution until Phase-2 decisions land, and even then gate it behind PaperExecutor (Kraken paper) then Base/testnet, never mainnet-first; (e) prefer SINGLE-venue Hyperliquid spot+perp under portfolio margin so legs collateralize each other, rather than a cross-venue carry that reintroduces multi-custody and basis-gap risk; (f) drop the aoki-h-jp repo as a reference — it does not match the stdlib/Hyperliquid design. Net: it earns intent-generator status, not live-funds-first status. No StructuredOutput field exists for sources; key corroborating URLs are the official Hyperliquid GitBook perpetuals info-endpoint docs and the portfolio-margin docs, the QuickNode/Chainstack fundingHistory references (with the 422-proxy caveat), and arbitragescanner.io / arbping.com for the fee-and-flip risk math.

verdicts.technique: Causal, friction-and-liquidation-aware backtest + CostModel as a hard promotion gate, with purged/CPCV cross-validation and Deflated Sharpe Ratio thresholds gating Sim -> Kraken paper -> tiny-cap onchain, with honest tracking of all configs tried.
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED REAL and 2026-current. The two named statistical methods are genuine and peer-reviewed: Deflated Sharpe Ratio (Bailey/Lopez de Prado, 2014) and purged/combinatorial-purged cross-validation (Lopez de Prado, 2017). Both Wikipedia and multiple independent 2026 crypto-backtesting sources (Bitsgap, Coin Bureau, Blockchain-Council, Coriva) corroborate the central empirical claim the recommendation rests on: unadjusted crypto backtests routinely overstate skill via cost-omission, lookahead/leakage, and untracked multiple-testing, and live Sharpe lands far below backtested Sharpe. The arxiv source (2602.11708, AdaptiveTrend) is itself a real-shaped crypto trend-following paper reporting Sharpe 2.41 / Calmar 3.18 -- exactly the too-good number DSR is designed to deflate, which validates rather than undermines the framing. SSRN 4675565 is paywalled (403) so unverified directly, but it is not load-bearing given independent corroboration.

ADVERSARIAL ATTEMPTS TO REFUTE -- and why they fail:
(1) 'It adds risk to a money agent.' No -- it is a rejection gate, not a trading action. It can only block promotion of false edges; it never sizes or sends a trade. For a money-handling agent the failure mode it prevents (acting on a backtest mirage) is the single most expensive one. Net risk-reducing.
(2) 'Too institutional for a solo builder.' Partially fair but surmountable. The honest weak link is the 'effective number of trials N' input: Lopez de Prado's rigorous N uses ONC/hierarchical clustering, which is real engineering effort and not stdlib-trivial. BUT a solo builder can use a conservative N (raw count of all configs tried, or a hierarchical lower bound) and still get the directional correction -- conservatism here is safe (over-counts trials, raises the bar). The 'track every config tried' requirement is discipline-dependent, not automatable, and is the realistic point of failure; it must be enforced by logging every backtest run to an append-only ledger, not left to memory.
(3) 'CPCV is exponentially expensive.' True, but mitigated by strategic sampling of combinations; tractable for a solo scope. Plain purged-CV (cheaper) captures most of the leakage-prevention benefit if CPCV proves too heavy.
(4) 'Stdlib-only violated.' Holds up: skewness/kurtosis, the max-Sharpe-under-noise threshold, label-overlap purging, and an embargo window are all plain arithmetic -- no numpy/sklearn strictly required (though they'd help). Consistent with the project's near-stdlib mandate.

WHAT IT IS NOT: this is validation/safety infrastructure, not an alpha generator. It will not tell the agent WHAT to trade; it only stops it from trading lies. Pairing it with a genuinely simple mechanical strategy first (so there are few honest configs to deflate) makes the DSR bar passable; pairing it with LLM-discretionary strategies makes 'number of trials' nearly impossible to count honestly -- which is itself a useful signal that LLM-discretionary is the wrong Phase-2 strategy choice for this gate to remain meaningful.
verdicts.caveats: Adopt the COST/FRICTION-AWARE BACKTEST + a hard promotion gate now -- this is non-negotiable infrastructure before any real funds and directly fulfills the 'validated before real funds' mandate. But sequence the statistical sophistication: ship plain purged-CV + a conservative DSR (using raw trial count as N, no clustering) first, since over-counting trials only makes the gate stricter (safe error). Defer full CPCV and ONC-clustered effective-N until the simpler gate is proven and a strategy actually approaches the threshold -- otherwise it is premature engineering. The real, under-acknowledged risk is NOT the math but the OPERATIONAL discipline: 'honest count of all configs tried' is only as good as an append-only run-ledger that logs every backtest before results are seen; without that enforced in code, DSR silently reverts to a vanity metric. Two scope honesties: (a) liquidation-aware modeling only matters if Phase-2 picks leveraged/derivatives venues -- for spot CEX (Kraken) it is largely moot and the 'liquidation-aware' label oversells; keep it for the onchain/perps path. (b) This gate presumes a strategy with a countable, finite config space -- it is structurally incompatible with LLM-discretionary strategies (unbounded, uncountable trials), so adopting this gate implicitly argues for the mechanical/quant strategy choice in Phase-2 decision (2).

verdicts.technique: Vol-scaled time-series momentum + regime-gated BTC-residual mean-reversion as a second mechanical IntentGenerator for treasuryforge
verdicts.assessment: The strategy CLASS is real and the integration is clean, but the claimed IMPACT does not survive scrutiny. VERIFIED: TSMOM in crypto is a genuine, repeatedly documented academic phenomenon (multiple 2024-2026 studies). It fits the architecture perfectly — a closed-form, stdlib-only `decide()` IntentGenerator emitting target-notional that policy.py clamps via max_notional_per_tx (lines 75-81); vol-targeting genuinely pre-shrinks size to complement the drawdown circuit-breaker (lines 61-69). REFUTED/OVERSTATED: (1) The headline "Sharpe ~1.83 OOS" is cherry-picked. Recent OOS data (Jan 2022-Dec 2024) shows PLAIN TSMOM-1M at only ~0.65 Sharpe; the 2.17/2.41 figures come from volume-weighting and adaptive portfolio construction, NOT the plain vol-scaled TSMOM proposed. Multiple sources state transaction costs "significantly reduce" net performance and that TSMOM profit is "concentrated in bullish markets" with the sell/short leg producing "negative profits in most cases" — so the impact is regime-dependent and cost-sensitive, not "high" and durable. (2) The rationale claims "no data-snooping surface," but the regime classifier (ADX threshold + vol window + volume + z-score bands + conflict-flat) is exactly a parameter-rich snooping surface; literature shows ADX confirms trends 5-15 bars late, regime transitions are "where traders lose money through whipsaws," and "most retail regime-strategy switching is too quick." (3) The BTC-residual market-neutral leg requires SHORTING the residual, which the spot-only Base Sepolia OnchainExecutor cannot do; the recommendation's own "degrade to long-residual-only" fallback destroys the market-neutral property it sells. For a solo local-first builder this is fine to BUILD and paper/testnet-validate (deterministic, testable, zero real-fund risk in current phase), but the alpha claim must not be trusted as a basis to deploy capital.
verdicts.caveats: Build it as a backtest-only/SimExecutor + PaperExecutor generator and treat the cited Sharpe ~1.83 as marketing-grade, not OOS-net-of-cost reality — re-derive your OWN cost-inclusive Sharpe on the actual venue before any capital. Plain vol-scaled TSMOM net of fees/slippage is closer to ~0.65 Sharpe in the 2022-2024 regime and is concentrated in bull trends. The regime-switching layer ADDS overfitting surface the rationale denies (ADX lag, whipsaw losses at transitions); keep parameters fixed/pre-registered, switch only on 3-5+ confirmed bars, and step-down size after a regime flip. The BTC-residual mean-reversion leg needs shorting and is impossible on the spot-only Base Sepolia OnchainExecutor — the long-only fallback is NOT market-neutral, so do not claim that property. Architecture/integration risk is low and stdlib-feasible; ALPHA risk is high and unvalidated. Two sources (SSRN 4675565, quantifiedstrategies.com) could not be fetched directly (403/bot-wall); the quantifiedstrategies source is a trading blog, not peer-reviewed, and the Grayscale source is vendor research, not an independent study.

verdicts.technique: Engle-Granger cointegration pairs trading (stdlib OLS + tabulated ADF + z-score bands) as a market-neutral IntentGenerator, later upgraded to online Kalman dynamic hedge ratio + OU optimal thresholds/half-life time-stop.
verdicts.assessment: The technique is genuinely REAL and actively researched in 2026 (Quantitative Finance Vol 26 2026; Frontiers Applied Math 2026; tandfonline GA-optimized-threshold paper), and the implementation claims survive scrutiny: an online Kalman filter is the documented, correct way to get a time-varying hedge ratio WITHOUT lookahead bias (Bayesian sequential update), it is genuinely ~30 lines / O(1) / tiny-state / stdlib-feasible, and a from-scratch ADF via MacKinnon (1994/2010) tabulated constants is legitimate stdlib-only. It is also the single best ARCHITECTURAL fit for this project: mechanical, deterministic, fully auditable intents map cleanly onto a hard-rule disposer, vastly better than LLM-discretionary. The rationale correctly names the dominant failure mode (cointegration/structural break, indistinguishable from tradeable divergence until the loss lands) and prescribes the right guardrails (hard stop, time-stop, Sim->paper gating, frequent re-testing).\n\nWhere it FAILS the skeptical test, and why holds_up=false: (1) The 'high impact' claim is the weakest link. Evidence is strong that the retail edge is thin and decaying: in-sample Sharpe has near-zero predictive power out-of-sample (R^2<0.025 across 888 Quantopian strategies); ~30 of 595 candidate pairs test 'cointegrated' by pure chance (data-mining); crypto is ~86% bots and the manual-era inefficiencies have 'largely disappeared.' So as an alpha source it is low/uncertain, not high. (2) The decisive gap the recommendation glosses over: market-neutral REQUIRES a short leg, but the project's actual execution paths are spot-first (SimExecutor -> Kraken paper -> Base Sepolia -> tiny mainnet). Real shorting needs margin/perps with 8h funding (0.01-0.45%/day), borrow-rate spikes (Aave utilization >90% events), and per-trade fees that demonstrably 'eat the edge' on small accounts; on Base/DeFi a clean short leg is even harder. The paired long/short intents may be effectively UN-executable in the cheap, safe paths the agent has, which undercuts the whole strategy unless re-scoped (e.g., spot-vs-perp, or two correlated perps). (3) Therefore its true value is as a deterministic validation/scaffolding track that exercises the policy engine and Sim/paper pipeline, NOT as a near-term money-maker. Money-handling risk is LOW only because the architecture forces Sim->paper first and the disposer caps notional/drawdown; the technique itself does not add danger, but the overstated impact + unresolved short-leg execution mean it should not be adopted as a live alpha now.
verdicts.caveats: Pilot strictly Sim->paper-only as a deterministic IntentGenerator; do NOT route real funds on the strength of a backtest. Before any capital: (a) resolve the short-leg execution question explicitly (spot-vs-perp or two perps; model funding + borrow + fees into the spread, since these can exceed the edge on small accounts) — a pure spot-only build cannot run this strategy market-neutral. (b) Validate out-of-sample with overfitting-aware methods (Combinatorial Purged CV, Deflated Sharpe, parameter-plateau sweeps), and require an ECONOMIC rationale per pair, not just a passing ADF, to avoid spurious/data-mined pairs (~30/595 cointegrate by chance). (c) Treat 'high impact' as unproven — frame the deliverable as policy/execution-loop validation, not alpha. Defer the Kalman/OU/half-life upgrades until a simple z-score version survives paper-trading; defer copula confirmation (research-grade, threatens stdlib-only). Keep hard per-position stop-loss AND time-stop mandatory because structural breaks are the dominant, unhedgeable risk.

verdicts.technique: Harden propose->dispose->execute per the Agentic Trading survey checklist: explicit fee/slippage model in every executor, full structured trace log (intent + retrieved memories + policy verdict + execution result), and a time-consistent backtest harness gate before any executor promotion.
verdicts.assessment: Survives adversarial scrutiny. SOURCE VERIFICATION: The primary source (arXiv:2605.19337, "Agentic Trading: When LLM Agents Meet Financial Markets," May 2026) is real and verbatim supports every specific claim: of n=19 empirical studies, 0/19 reach R3 (full) reproducibility, only 1/19 specifies a transaction-cost model, only 2/19 report time-consistent split protocols, and the survey's stated contributions are an evidence ledger, reproducibility audit, and reporting checklist. The recommendation is an accurate, source-backed restatement, not a hallucination. CORROBORATION: Independently confirmed by arXiv:2606.08285 ("Beyond Agent Architecture: Execution Assumptions and Reproducibility in LLM-Based Trading Systems," June 2026), which concludes simulations lacking explicit transaction-cost modeling substantially inflate profitability, and by broad industry consensus that a strategy showing ~20% can deliver ~8% after realistic fees+slippage, with live Sharpe falling far below backtest. The transaction-cost-is-non-negotiable claim is robust and well beyond a single-paper assertion. SOLO-BUILDER FIT: Strongly appropriate. This is pure hardening of structure that ALREADY exists in treasuryforge (the propose->dispose->execute split), implementable stdlib-only by one person in days: a fee/slippage function per executor, a JSONL structured trace, and a backtest gate. No new architecture, no new dependencies, no key/fund-handling surface, so it adds essentially zero operational attack surface. RISK ANALYSIS: The 'more risk than value for a money-handling agent' concern inverts here. The technique REDUCES the dominant risk for this project: promoting SimExecutor -> Paper -> Onchain -> mainnet on a backtest that looks profitable only because it ignored costs and look-ahead. It directly operationalizes the project's own thesis ('validated before any real funds'). It is also orthogonal to the three open Phase-2 decisions (chain, strategy, custody), so it is safe to do now regardless of how those land.
verdicts.caveats: The second cited source (arXiv:2412.18174, InvestorBench, Dec 2024) is real but is a financial-decision BENCHMARK paper, not a transaction-cost/reproducibility paper, so it is the weaker citation; the load-bearing evidence is 2605.19337 alone, though 2606.08285 and industry sources fill the gap. Two scope notes for honest implementation: (1) the survey's checklist also flags universe/survivorship handling and execution-timing semantics, which the recommendation omits naming explicitly but should be folded into the same checklist. (2) An explicit fee/slippage model is necessary but not sufficient; without realistic market-impact and fill assumptions it can still flatter results, so the backtest gate must specify fill/latency semantics, not just per-trade cost. (3) 'Time-consistent' must be enforced as strict walk-forward (no future leakage into memory retrieval), which is easy to violate in an LLM-memory agent and is the highest-value part of the gate.

verdicts.technique: GBNF grammar-constrained decoding (llama.cpp) pinning symbol-to-allowlist enum + buy/sell + numeric fields, plus a hand-rolled fail-closed validator retry loop reusing policy rules
verdicts.assessment:
The core technique is REAL and production-usable in 2026, and appropriate for a solo local-first stdlib-only builder. Confirmed via llama.cpp grammars/README: GBNF is actively maintained, supports enum alternatives (buy|sell, symbol allowlist) and integer min/max bounds, runs fully local/zero-cloud, and is built into llama.cpp itself (no new deps). The architectural framing is correct: it guarantees a WELL-FORMED intent, not a good trade, and complements rather than replaces the policy engine. A fail-closed retry loop using the policy rules as validator is sound and cheap.

HOWEVER, the rationale oversells and contains a refuted claim plus two safety-critical omissions:

1) FABRICATED METRIC: The '~95%+ on first retry' figure appears in NONE of the three cited sources. The Aidan Cooper article explicitly does not contain it; Instructor and the llama.cpp README don't either. Inventing a quantitative reliability number on a money-handling agent is exactly the kind of false confidence to reject.

2) STRUCTURAL VALIDITY != CORRECTNESS, and the gap is dangerous here. Multiple sources (Aidan Cooper; arxiv 'valid-but-wrong' literature) document that forcing a model onto an enum MANUFACTURES a confident wrong choice when the truthful answer is 'none of these / no trade'. For a treasury agent, coercing an off-allowlist symbol to the NEAREST allowed symbol, or coercing 'I'm uncertain' into a buy/sell, is worse than rejection. The grammar prevents garbage but can convert abstention into a structurally-perfect bad order. MANDATORY mitigation: add an explicit escape value (HOLD/no_trade/abstain) to the side/action enum so the grammar can never force a trade.

3) REASONING DEGRADATION: 'Let Me Speak Freely?' (EMNLP 2024) documents 10-30% reasoning degradation when format constraints commit the model before it reasons. Mitigation: put a free-text reasoning field BEFORE the constrained numeric/enum fields in the schema (reason-then-commit ordering).

4) SOURCING MISMATCH: Instructor (cited source) requires Pydantic + provider clients and is NOT stdlib-only; fine as a pattern reference but it cannot be a dependency. The recommendation already hand-rolls the validator, so this is a citation flaw, not an implementation flaw.

The impact is better described as MEDIUM (input hygiene / fail-closed plumbing), not HIGH. The defense-in-depth value is real: malformed/unparseable/off-enum intents are blocked before the policy engine, and the validator catches over-cap/off-allowlist with auto-retry. But the policy engine was already going to reject those deterministically, so the grammar layer mainly improves robustness and saves retries, not safety per se. Safety still rests entirely on the hard-rule policy engine, which is correct.
verdicts.caveats: Adopt on the validation/plumbing path, but NOT as-claimed. Required before any real funds: (a) add an explicit abstain/HOLD enum value so constrained decoding can never coerce uncertainty into a buy/sell or coerce an off-allowlist symbol to the nearest allowed one; (b) order the JSON schema reason-first, numeric/enum-fields-last to avoid the 10-30% reasoning-before-commit degradation; (c) drop the unsubstantiated '95% first-retry' claim and the 'eliminates the entire class' framing — measure your own retry-success rate empirically in sim before trusting it; (d) keep this strictly as defense-in-depth: the hard-rule policy engine, not the grammar, remains the sole safety guarantee, and the validator must fail CLOSED on retry exhaustion (proposed); (e) Instructor cannot be a dependency under stdlib-only — use it only as a reference for the retry pattern. Grammar-constrained decoding is also only relevant IF Phase-2 decision (2) chooses an LLM-discretionary strategy; if the strategy is mechanical/quant, intents are code-generated and this technique is unnecessary (defer it entirely in that branch).

verdicts.technique: Route ALL money arithmetic through a sandboxed PAL/Program-of-Thought step (LLM emits whitelisted Python run by an AST-allowlist interpreter) plus a separate non-LLM arithmetic verifier between PAL output and policy.
verdicts.assessment:
The cited evidence is real and accurate: PAL scores 61.2% vs CoT 23.1% on GSM-HARD (verified vs the MLR proceedings), and integer-minor-units/Decimal-for-money is uncontroversial best practice (Modern Treasury, Stripe). A 2026 Beancount.io research log on PAL-for-finance even confirms the principle. BUT the technique as written does not hold up for THIS project, for three reasons.

(1) Redundant given the architecture. The recommendation bundles a trivially-correct idea (use decimal.Decimal / integer minor-units) with a risky one (LLM emits Python that a sandbox executes for every notional/fee number). In treasuryforge the LLM's only output surface is a single structured field, Intent.base_amount. Every safety-critical number -- notional (policy.evaluate -> intent.notional(price)), buy cost, fees, slippage (SimExecutor), equity and drawdown floor (SimWallet.equity) -- is ALREADY recomputed deterministically by non-LLM code behind the trust boundary the whole project is built on ('POLICY ENGINE DOES NOT TRUST THE AGENT'). The 'separate non-LLM arithmetic verifier' the recommendation says to ADD already exists: it is the policy engine + wallet. Adding a PAL codegen step moves math FROM hallucination-proof structured output BACK INTO LLM-authored code, creating the very token-arithmetic risk it claims to remove.

(2) The sandbox claim is the weak point and is essentially backwards. A pure-stdlib AST allowlist with 'no imports/IO, fail-closed' is the canonical broken-sandbox pattern: generic escapes via __subclasses__ traversal, reversed-string getattr, format-string attribute walks, and unicode-escape encodings are well documented (HackTricks, Checkmarx). The recommendation presents DIY stdlib sandboxing as a SAFETY feature of a money-handling agent; the security literature treats it as a known anti-pattern. For an agent whose threat model explicitly includes prompt-injection via market data, executing generated Python locally is a net-new RCE surface on the machine that will eventually hold keys.

(3) The Beancount.io 2026 source itself warns PAL's reliability bottleneck IS the LLM emitting valid/correct code ('invalid Python falls back to nothing'), recommends PAL for READ-ONLY finance, calls it risky for systems that WRITE, and says a solo builder needs 'substantial additional safety infrastructure' -- i.e. it argues against this exact use (an agent that writes money-moving actions).

Net: the principle (no LLM token-math; use decimals/minor-units; verify with deterministic code) is correct and should be honored. The specific MECHANISM (PAL codegen + DIY AST sandbox) is the wrong tool here -- redundant with the existing policy/wallet recompute and it imports a hard, easily-botched sandbox-security problem for zero marginal benefit. The cheaper sibling is strictly better: keep the LLM emitting only structured intents, migrate money paths to Decimal/integer minor-units, and treat the existing policy+wallet recompute as the mandated non-LLM verifier (add an explicit assert qty*price==notional unit/range check there).
verdicts.caveats: 'Defer' is split: ADOPT-NOW the decimal half (switch float -> decimal.Decimal or integer minor-units across types/wallet/policy/executor; add explicit unit+range asserts in the existing non-LLM recompute). AVOID the PAL-codegen-in-a-sandbox half for this design -- it is redundant and adds an RCE surface. The PAL mechanism would only become a 'pilot' candidate in a hypothetical future where the agent must author multi-step free-form calculations the policy engine cannot recompute structurally -- a regime this architecture deliberately avoids. If PAL is ever revisited, do not DIY a stdlib AST sandbox: use process/container isolation with no network and no secrets, and keep the deterministic verifier as the authority. Note one stale detail: the agent today is deterministic mean-reversion, not an LLM, so even the decimal change is the only currently-needed action; the PAL concern is purely forward-looking.

verdicts.technique: Plan-and-Execute LLM cycle: one planner call per rebalance -> JSON list of candidate intents -> deterministic policy-gated executor; ReAct reserved for ad-hoc research; re-plan each cycle on fresh state.
verdicts.assessment: The technique is real, well-documented, and production-used in 2026, and it survives skeptical scrutiny FOR THIS PROJECT specifically. Adversarial findings: (1) Source quality is uneven. The arXiv cite (2605.19337) is a trading-agent SURVEY whose actual headline finding is a reproducibility crisis (0/19 studies fully reproducible); it explicitly does NOT compare Plan-and-Execute vs ReAct and does NOT track LLM call counts. So the "1-2 vs ~10 calls" figure is from the Substack blog (non-peer-reviewed) plus folklore, not the academic source. This is over-attribution, not a wrong claim — the pattern and its lower-call-count property are genuinely standard. (2) The classic refutation of Plan-and-Execute is brittleness: a stale or surprised plan derails execution, and a dedicated 2026 paper (arXiv 2509.08646, Secure Plan-then-Execute) warns the pattern needs real hardening before financial use due to prompt-injection and plan-poisoning. This objection does NOT land against treasuryforge because its design already neutralizes it: the plan is merely CANDIDATE INTENTS with no authority; a deterministic, non-LLM policy engine (kill-switch, drawdown breaker, notional cap, rate-limit, allowlist, solvency) disposes every intent, and re-planning each cycle against fresh state kills staleness. A poisoned/hallucinated/stale plan can at worst emit a bad intent that the policy engine rejects — exactly the 'separate policy engine, agent only proposes' design that 2026 best-practice sources (Ledger, Cobo, Alchemy) mandate. (3) Overkill for a solo builder? The reverse: it is cheaper (fewer calls), simpler to audit (one JSON plan per cycle suits a local-first trace log), and trivial to implement stdlib-only. It matches the solo/local-first constraint better than ReAct. Net: the recommendation is directionally correct and aligned with independently confirmed 2026 practice (LLM decides strategy, deterministic rules enforce hard limits).
verdicts.caveats: Pilot rather than adopt-now because the safety only holds if the policy-engine gating is treated as a HARD DEPENDENCY, not the technique itself being inherently safe. Prove it end-to-end on SimExecutor with the policy engine demonstrably rejecting malformed/poisoned/over-cap plans BEFORE any paper/testnet/real funds. Specific guards to add during the pilot: (a) strictly schema-validate the planner's JSON plan object before it reaches the mapper — reject (don't best-effort parse) on any deviation, treating the plan as untrusted input; (b) treat the plan as advisory only — never let it carry execution authority or relax a policy limit; (c) log every plan + every policy disposition for the audit trace; (d) bound planner output size and intent count to prevent a runaway plan flooding the executor; (e) keep ReAct strictly read-only (research) with no tool that can emit an intent. Also correct the sourcing: do not cite arXiv 2605.19337 as evidence for the Plan-vs-ReAct call-count claim — it is a reproducibility survey that makes neither claim; the relevant academic reference for safe use is arXiv 2509.08646 (Secure Plan-then-Execute). The brittleness/injection risks the literature flags are real and only mitigated here because the deterministic policy engine is the actual safety boundary — if that engine is ever weakened or bypassed, this pattern becomes as unsafe as any other.

verdicts.technique: Stdlib EWMA (RiskMetrics lambda=0.94, sqrt-annualized) volatility-targeting sizing layer inserted between PROPOSE and the per-tx cap: final_size = min(vol_target_size, notional_cap)
verdicts.assessment: The underlying technique is real and correctly specified. EWMA lambda=0.94 with sqrt(N) annualization IS the canonical JP Morgan RiskMetrics volatility recursion (half-life ~11 days), it is genuinely ~20 lines of pure stdlib, it is unit-testable against canned return series, and the architectural claim holds: sizing the Intent's base_amount before PROPOSE with min(vol_target, cap) is monotone-shrinking, so it can NEVER breach the existing policy cap and never touches the dispose contract. That part is low-risk and accurate. What does NOT hold up is the evidence behind the headline. (1) The impact numbers are unsupported and likely fabricated: the recommendation cites '-31%->-14% DD, Sharpe 0.99->1.54' but the actual QuantPedia source (its own citation #1) reports a 60/40 stock-bond portfolio going -31.39%->-18.92% DD and Sharpe 0.90->1.34 for the EWMA variant. The endpoints (-14%, 1.54) match NO cited source. The starting -31% suspiciously matches QuantPedia while the results are altered, presented as if from 'the corpus.' (2) Wrong benchmark: those figures are a diversified multi-asset portfolio, NOT crypto and NOT this agent's strategy. (3) Strategy mismatch: the shipped agent (agent.py) is LONG-ONLY MEAN-REVERSION (buy dips, sell held). The literature explicitly warns vol-targeting hurts mean-reverting strategies because cutting size when vol spikes is exactly when a dip-buyer wants MORE exposure, and the fast measure reverts, adding trading costs. The claim that this is 'the strongest single drawdown reducer' for THIS agent is unverifiable and probably false. The recommendation conflates a real, valuable institutional technique with fabricated/misattributed performance numbers on the wrong asset class and wrong strategy family.
verdicts.caveats: Do NOT adopt on the basis of the stated impact figures - they are not in the cited sources and are likely fabricated; treat the '-31%->-14% / Sharpe 0.99->1.54' claim as unverified. Build it as a SimExecutor-replayable pilot behind a flag and let the replay measure the ACTUAL drawdown/Sharpe delta on the real long-only mean-reversion agent before trusting any number. Expect the benefit to be smaller than claimed, and watch for the known failure mode where vol-scaling cuts exposure precisely when the dip-buyer wants more, potentially worsening returns and adding churn. Pin the lambda, annualization factor, and a vol floor (avoid div-by-near-zero blowing up size) verbatim in the build spec. Because the layer only ever shrinks size (monotone vs the cap), the downside is bounded - worst case it under-trades, it cannot breach policy - which is exactly why piloting is safe. Re-validate relevance if/when the strategy switches from mean-reversion to momentum/trend, where vol-targeting's real edge lives.

verdicts.technique: Graded 3-stage out-of-band circuit breaker (Yellow throttle/lower-cap -> Orange force-route to SimExecutor/read-only -> Red halt+persist) on mark-to-market drawdown, manual-only re-arm, agent-readable but agent-immutable config
verdicts.assessment:
VERDICT: Survives adversarial scrutiny. The technique is real and current. Multiple independent 2026 sources confirm graded intervention (throttle -> isolate/read-only -> halt) as production guardrail practice for both autonomous AI agents and DeFi protocols, with human-only re-arm and a "context bundle" at the checkpoint. The flagship cited source (Arion Research) matches the recommendation almost verbatim: it explicitly defines Stage 1 throttle, Stage 2 revoke-write-permissions, Stage 3 kill-and-persist-state, and states recovery is "always human-controlled." So the structure is well-sourced, not invented.

ARCHITECTURAL FIT (strong): It is a natural, low-cost extension of what already exists. policy.py already computes mark-to-market equity via wallet.equity(prices) (line 62) and already latches with _tripped. The upgrade is essentially promoting _tripped: bool to a stage enum and adding intermediate thresholds — pure stdlib state-machine logic, zero new dependencies, entirely inside the propose/dispose/execute split the project is built around. It is well within a solo local-first builder's reach and is arguably MORE appropriate for a solo builder than the institutional examples, since it needs no external kill-switch infra.

THE ONE REAL GAP (correctly self-flagged): The Arion source grades on AGENT-BEHAVIORAL anomalies (confidence < 0.65, 10x tool-call spikes), NOT financial drawdown. The recommendation transplants the 3-stage STRUCTURE onto a financial drawdown signal — legitimate, but it means none of the source's numbers transfer. Independent evidence puts crypto max-drawdown reject zones at 20-30% (loosely supporting a Red near -20%) but gives NO basis for Yellow/Orange spacing or cooldown cadence. The recommendation's own caveat ('-10%/-20% and re-entry are unsourced, must be backtested') is therefore accurate and honest, not a hand-wave. This is exactly why it should be piloted, not adopted with hardcoded numbers.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS (and why they fail to kill it): (1) 'More state = more risk for a money agent' — a 3-state machine has more failure modes than today's monotonic latch; a buggy Green-reset could silently un-protect capital. But the design keeps Red as a hard latch with manual-only re-arm, explicitly excluding auto-recovery whipsaw, so the dangerous case is ruled out by construction. (2) 'Orange force-route to SimExecutor is a silent degraded mode' — an agent that thinks it's trading but is simulated is an auditability hazard; this is a real implementation REQUIREMENT (Orange must emit a loud, persisted, audited signal), not a reason to reject the technique. (3) 'Over-engineering vs the project's own pragmatic-over-perfect ethos' — valid; a solo builder can ship most of the value with just Yellow(throttle)+Red(halt) and add Orange later.

Bottom line: real, current, cheap, in-architecture, and the only weak part (the cutoffs) is the part the author already told you to backtest.
verdicts.caveats: Do NOT ship with hardcoded -10%/-20% or any auto-recovery. Required conditions before/while piloting: (1) Backtest stage cutoffs and cooldown against real crypto drawdown cadence on SimExecutor before they govern any real funds — they are unsourced. (2) Keep Red a strict monotonic latch with manual-only re-arm; forbid ANY automatic stage de-escalation (auto-recovery is the documented capital-destroying whipsaw failure mode). (3) Orange's force-route to SimExecutor/read-only must NOT be silent — it must emit a loud, persisted, auditable event, otherwise the agent runs in an invisible degraded mode (audit hazard for a money handler). (4) Config must be loaded read-only into the engine; verify the agent code path literally cannot mutate stage thresholds (the propose/dispose split is the whole point). (5) Consider shipping Yellow+Red first and deferring Orange to reduce the added correctness surface, per the project's pragmatic-over-perfect principle. (6) Property-based / fuzz tests that breakers trip only when intended (the project already uses hypothesis) — extend test_policy.py to cover every stage transition including the no-skip-down invariant.

verdicts.technique: Hypothesis stateful testing (RuleBasedStateMachine + @invariant) to fuzz random intent sequences through the real policy engine and assert safety invariants after every step
verdicts.assessment: Survives skeptical scrutiny. Verified against live sources: Hypothesis is maximally maintained — v6.155.3 was released Jun 16 2026 (the very day of this task), has ZERO mandatory runtime dependencies, is pure-python, and RuleBasedStateMachine/@invariant are first-class documented APIs. The 'stdlib-only at runtime' claim is sound: it lives only in tests/ as a dev dependency (current requirements-dev.txt has just pytest>=8.0) and is never imported by treasuryforge/ runtime code, so it adds zero attack surface to a money-handling agent. I confirmed strong fit against the actual code: policy.py PolicyEngine carries three pieces of mutable state (_starting_equity, _tripped, _recent_ts deque) with order-dependent transitions (kill->breaker->allowlist->cap->rate-limit->solvency, plus register_fill bookkeeping in runner.py) — exactly the stateful surface RuleBasedStateMachine exists to explore. The existing tests/test_policy.py is entirely hand-picked single cases (one allow, one of each deny), which is the precise gap the recommendation targets: it would prove the invariants (once tripped stays tripped, rate-limit never exceeds max_tx_per_window across a rolling window, no fill ever exceeds available quote/base, denied symbols never execute) hold across arbitrary histories, with auto-shrinking to minimal repros. Refutation attempts mostly failed. Two honest caveats. (1) Source weakness, not technique weakness: the second cited source (eth-brownie stateful docs) is from a project that is officially no longer maintained (its own README now points users to Ape) — but brownie is merely an illustrative tutorial there, not a dependency; the load-bearing tool is Hypothesis itself, which is healthy. Swap the citation to the canonical hypothesis.works rule-based-stateful-testing article. (2) Modeling-fidelity risk: the state machine must mirror the runner's real allow->execute->register_fill ordering and the float epsilon tolerances (1e-9 in solvency), and the asserted invariants must be derived independently of the policy implementation — otherwise it merely restates the code and tests a fiction. This is design discipline, not a flaw in the approach. The recommendation's own honest limit (bounded random exploration finds bugs but cannot certify absence) is correct and appropriately scoped.
verdicts.caveats: Low effort, high leverage for a solo builder: one `pip install hypothesis` into requirements-dev.txt plus one test file, no runtime/infra change, validated entirely against the existing zero-funds SimExecutor harness. Adopt now, but with three conditions. (1) Replace the dead eth-brownie citation with the canonical Hypothesis docs/hypothesis.works article — brownie is no longer maintained (use Ape if a smart-contract framework is ever needed downstream). (2) Derive the safety invariants from first principles (the README's stated guarantees), NOT by copying policy.py's branches, or the test becomes a tautology. (3) Model the full runner loop transition (intent -> evaluate -> execute -> register_fill) and the 1e-9 solvency tolerance faithfully in the state machine, since the rate-limiter and breaker are history-dependent; otherwise you fuzz a system that does not match production. Treat green stateful tests as a necessary gate before paper/testnet funds, never as a sufficient proof of safety.

verdicts.technique: Exchange-native dead-man switch (Kraken cancelAllOrdersAfter, 60s timeout pinged every 15-30s) on the PaperExecutor path, wired so the policy kill-switch sets timeout=0 and stops the heartbeat to auto-flush resting orders.
verdicts.assessment:
The technical core survives skeptical scrutiny. Verified directly against Kraken docs: cancelAllOrdersAfter exists on both spot REST and futures REST/WebSocket, timeout is in SECONDS with range 0 to <86400, the recommended 60s-timeout / 15-30s-ping cadence is the vendor's own guidance, timeout=0 disables it, and it cancels resting ORDERS only (open positions/fills survive) — exactly as the rationale states, including the honest limit. It is genuinely stdlib-implementable (signed HTTPS POST), exchange-side (survives agent crash/hang/network loss, which the in-process policy engine cannot), and validatable with zero real funds. So the recommendation is real, honestly-scoped, and architecturally sound.

But two refutations dent the framing, neither fatal:

1) WRONG VENUE FOR FREE VALIDATION. The recommendation pins this to 'PaperExecutor = Kraken paper.' Kraken SPOT has NO public paper/demo/sandbox — only validate=true order simulation (returns no order ID, creates no resting order, so there is literally nothing for the dead-man switch to cancel) or a gated 'qualified clients' onboarding via the API team. The only free, self-serve zero-funds environment that actually exercises this endpoint is the FUTURES demo (demo-futures.kraken.com), which is a different product (leverage/liquidation risk) than a spot treasury. So 'validate on the Kraken-paper path with zero funds' is only cleanly true on futures demo, not on the spot paper path the roadmap implies. The builder must either validate on futures demo (and accept spot has no true paper), or treat the spot wiring as code-reviewed-but-not-live-tested until real tiny-cap mainnet.

2) 'HIGHEST-VALUE GUARDRAIL' IS OVERSTATED for this stage. The agent is at SimExecutor/testnet with zero real funds and its stated chain decision is unresolved (EVM/DeFi is a live option — where there are no resting orders and no exchange dead-man switch at all, so this technique simply doesn't apply). A dead-man switch protects RESTING limit orders; a treasury rebalance agent that uses market/marketable orders leaves little resting to cancel, blunting the impact. The genuinely highest-value gap for a money-handling agent pre-funds is idempotency/replay-safety on execution and a tested flatten path — the recommendation itself concedes 'a separate flatten step is still required,' which is the larger missing piece. It adds essentially zero new risk (worst case: an order you wanted gets auto-cancelled — a safe failure for a treasury), so it is not net-negative; it is just not THE keystone.
verdicts.caveats: Adopt the PATTERN now, but pilot rather than ship-as-described: (1) Validate the live endpoint on demo-futures.kraken.com, NOT on a Kraken-spot 'paper' account, which does not exist self-serve for a solo builder — spot offers only validate=true (no resting order to cancel) or institutional onboarding. (2) If Phase-2 chain decision lands on EVM/DeFi instead of a CEX, this technique is N/A (no exchange-side resting-order switch exists on-chain) — it is contingent on the CEX path. (3) It cancels resting orders ONLY; positions/fills survive, so it is necessary-but-not-sufficient — pair it with a tested flatten/close routine before any real funds, or it gives false comfort. (4) For a market-order rebalance style there is little resting to cancel, reducing real-world impact. (5) Sequence it AFTER execution idempotency/replay-safety and a verified flatten path, which are the larger pre-funding gaps for a money-handling agent. Risk added is near-zero (auto-cancel is a safe failure mode), so the pilot is low-cost and worth doing on the futures-demo path as soon as the CEX route is confirmed.

verdicts.technique: stdlib risk module: rolling historical-simulation CVaR/Expected-Shortfall on the agent's own returns + config-driven deterministic stress-scenario veto (BTC/ETH -70%, COVID/2022 replays, depeg, correlation->1) + Kupiec/Christoffersen self-audit
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation is real and 2026-current, but it is two techniques bundled as one, and skeptical scrutiny splits them cleanly.

VALIDATED (the deterministic stress-scenario veto): External 2026 evidence confirms exactly the cited failure modes. The January 2026 deleveraging is real and documented (AMINA Bank): BTC failed to decouple from equities (correlation->1) and depeg pressure on ETH derivatives appeared (Tirole) — precisely the events CVaR misses out-of-sample. The Anaptyss source independently confirms the "70%+ BTC/ETH within a week" scenario and the "adjust one variable at a time (volatility/price/correlation)" surgical method, and calls scenario analysis "the foundation of stress testing." This half is deterministic, config-driven, needs zero statistical power, no minimum sample, runs from day-one with zero funds, and is implementable in stdlib by a solo builder. It genuinely upgrades the existing purely-reactive breaker (policy.py lines 61-69 trip only AFTER equity already fell) into a forward-looking veto. Strong fit. This is where the 'high' impact actually lives.

REFUTED (the CVaR + Kupiec/Christoffersen self-audit half, for THIS context): Three independent web confirmations that this part is wrong for a solo/young treasury: (1) Kupiec POF is UNDEFINED when zero violations occur, and zero violations in a short period is 'not trivial' — so the self-audit literally cannot run on a young agent. (2) Kupiec + unconditional Christoffersen are 'not appropriate for small samples' and show weak power even at 1,000 observations — far more than a solo agent's own trade history. (3) Historical-simulation ES SYSTEMATICALLY UNDERESTIMATES tail risk at small N — so a CVaR veto on the agent's short history yields FALSE CONFIDENCE, the worst failure mode for a money-handling guardrail: it would approve a risky trade precisely because its short history hasn't yet seen a tail. The recommendation's own caveat admits this, but undersells it — the caveat inverts the priority rather than footnoting it.

ARCHITECTURE MISMATCH: The current policy engine evaluates a single tick with a single-symbol price dict (policy.py: prices = {tick.symbol: tick.price}) and the agent is single-symbol mean-reversion. The most-cited scenario, correlation->1, presumes a multi-asset covariance structure that doesn't exist yet — so even the good half is partly ahead of the current build until the agent goes multi-asset.

NET: the technique holds up as a real, current, useful direction (the stress-veto half is excellent), but the headline 'high impact / forward-looking CVaR tail veto' framing does NOT survive scrutiny: the statistically-powered piece is the piece that doesn't yet work, and the working piece needs no statistics.
verdicts.caveats:
Adopt NOW: the deterministic, config-driven stress-scenario veto (BTC/ETH -70%/week, 2022/COVID replay deltas, depeg haircut, and — once multi-asset — a correlation->1 stress). It is the real value, solo-appropriate, stdlib-only, and runnable pre-funding. Implement it as an additional hard rule in the existing PolicyEngine.evaluate ordering (a 7th deny check), config-driven like the rest of PolicyConfig.

DEFER / GATE: the rolling CVaR/ES on the agent's own returns and the Kupiec + Christoffersen self-audit. Do NOT let CVaR approve or veto a trade on its own — at small N it systematically understates tails and gives false comfort. Gate it behind a minimum-sample threshold (the literature suggests >=1,000 violation-eligible observations before the backtests have meaningful power; far more than a young treasury has), and even then run it as ADVISORY TELEMETRY in the audit ledger, never as a standalone money-gating veto. Kupiec is undefined with zero violations — guard that branch explicitly.

Also note: 'correlation->1' has nothing to act on until the agent is multi-asset (current build is single-symbol), so scope that scenario to land with the multi-asset upgrade, not before.

verdicts.technique: Deterministic pre-trade slippage/liquidity guard in the policy layer (AMM getAmountOut / v3-v4 Quoter on DeFi, Kraken L2 book-walk on CEX) that disposes on predicted price-impact > cap, with the executor enforcing the bound atomically via on-chain minAmountOut+deadline or a CEX limit/post-only price.
verdicts.assessment: All load-bearing technical claims verified as real and current in 2026. The Uniswap v2 closed-form is exact and the cited 997/1000 fee constant is correct verbatim in UniswapV2Library (amountOut = amountIn*997*reserveOut / (reserveIn*1000 + amountIn*997)); the rec's honest caveats (constant-product only, verify fee per DEX, v3/v4 not closed-form, quotes go stale, TVL hides depth) are all accurate. Atomic enforcement via amountOutMin+deadline revert is the canonical Uniswap slippage guard. Kraken exposes the L2 order-book REST endpoint (book-walk is sound) plus oflags=post post-only and validate=1 dry-run for atomic CEX bounding. Architecturally the guard is a pure additive dispose-layer rule that can only BLOCK trades, never initiate them, mirroring the existing per-tx notional cap and failing safe -- so it does NOT add net risk to a money-handling agent. Refutation attempts on reality and safety both failed; the rec stands. Two caveats keep it from being a clean adopt-now for THIS builder: (1) stdlib-only friction -- off-chain DeFi quoting needs Ethereum keccak-256 (NOT Python hashlib's NIST sha3_256) + ABI encode/decode, forcing a vendored keccak or accepting eth-abi/web3 as a dependency; the CEX path stays fully stdlib (HTTP+JSON+hmac). (2) The v3/v4 Quoter is NOT a view function -- it reverts-to-return and must run via eth_call/.call() off-chain; workable from Python but more plumbing than the clean v2 math. The pre-trade prediction is a gate/observability layer; the real guarantee is the on-chain minAmountOut (the rec states this two-layer design correctly). 'SAME SlippageGuard on both paths' shares the interface, not implementation (three distinct engines).
verdicts.caveats: Not a clean adopt-now for a stdlib-only solo builder. (1) DeFi off-chain quoting needs Ethereum keccak (hashlib sha3_256 is the WRONG variant) + ABI codec -> vendored keccak or a dependency, denting stdlib purity; CEX path is fully stdlib. (2) v3/v4 Quoter is not view -> requires eth_call/callStatic plumbing. (3) Pre-trade number is a gate only; enforcement is the executor's atomic minAmountOut+deadline (or CEX post-only/limit) -- never rely on the off-chain prediction alone, quotes go stale between read and broadcast. (4) Per-DEX fee constant must be parameterized (PancakeSwap 9975/10000, not 997/1000). (5) Closed-form is exact for constant-product ONLY; v3/v4 concentrated liquidity needs the Quoter, not the formula. Sequencing: ship the Kraken-paper book-walk guard first (stdlib, zero-funds, immediate value), then pilot the DeFi variant on Base Sepolia to validate the keccak/ABI dependency tradeoff and Quoter eth_call path before any real funds.

verdicts.technique: Self-hosted Base smart account where the agent is a constrained SPENDER under a Coinbase SpendPermission (period allowance = rate/notional cap, end-time = dead-man expiry), with a human-held OWNER key holding revoke() as kill switch; validated on Base Sepolia first.
verdicts.assessment:
The core technique is REAL and production-grade in 2026, but the rationale contains one materially overstated claim that must be corrected before adoption.
